title: Wavefront prefetch: KV-cache + kernel path interplay (TRT-LLM vs vLLM) - OnPrem/VPC
preview: Deep technical session to validate Quasar Systems' on-prem inference strategy. Focus: interaction between KV cache prefetch strategies (wavefront/pipeline), kernel selection heuristics, and CUDA Graph replay behavior across TRT-LLM and vLLM runtimes. Primary goals: identify stability knobs for CUDA Graph replay, quantify latency variability with prefetch wavefront, and outline pilot instrumentation for on-prem NVLink cluster.
text: Deep technical session to validate Quasar Systems' on-prem inference strategy. title: RosaTech - Gateway & Onramp Integration Planning
preview: Initial integration planning focused on onramp/gateway architecture, SDK/adapter choices (LangChain/LlamaIndex), quota enforcement, and observability hooks. Customer leaning towards Hosted for quick POC but has strict security gating that may push towards Dedicated or Private VPC. SE to produce benchmarks and integration checklist.

title: POC load & token-profile scoping - NectarFlow
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2028-06-05
Start: 16:00 PT
Duration: ~68 minutes
Attendees: Ava (Redwood AE), Miguel (Redwood SE), Liam (Redwood SE), Priya (NectarFlow Head ML), Jon (Platform), Elena (SRE), Marco (Product)

Summary: quick POC scoping focused on burst profile, concurrency, input/output token length distribution, and an initial benchmark plan. Customer emphasized tail latency and cost sensitivity; interested in dedicated pool if predictable.

Topics: success criteria, traffic capture format, concurrency model, QPS distributions, token-length histograms, benchmark mechanics (replay vs synthetic), timeline for POC

[00:00] Ava (Redwood AE): All right hi everyone thanks for joining, I'll kick it off. Quick intros — I'm Ava, AE for the account, Miguel and Liam are our SEs who will run the benchmark.
[00:18] Priya (NectarFlow): Hi, Priya here. We run NectarFlow's assistant workflows, a mix of short system prompts and long-context summarizations. Our main pain is unpredictable burst from search-ops and tail latency spikes.
[00:35] Miguel (Redwood SE): Cool. To set expectations, today we want to capture your QPS shape, concurrency, token in/out distributions and agree success criteria —  we'll propose a replay-based benchmark after we get traffic.
[00:48] Jon (Platform Eng): Just to add, our infra is GCP us-west4, most inference traffic currently goes to a managed endpoint that we control. We're open to staging on Redwood hosted or a Dedicated pool.
[01:05] Elena (SRE): SOC2/SSO questions up front — will need to see audit log behavior and key management options.
[01:12] Ava: Yep we'll share the security pack. For now focus on workload: can you describe your typical request types and token lengths?
[01:28] Priya: Rough breakdown — 60% short form Q/A (avg 40 input tokens, 80 output tokens), 30% multi-step assistant flows (avg 120 in, 400 out), 10% long document summarization where inputs can be 2k-6k tokens and outputs 200-800 tokens — those are the pain points for latency.
[01:52] Miguel: That's great detail. Do you have any QPS numbers or concurrency targets?
[01:58] Priya: Normal baseline is 12 qps with spikes to 220 qps for short bursts lasting 30-90s, plus longer sustained peaks of ~40-60 qps for 10-30 minutes tied to batch jobs.
[02:15] Liam (Redwood SE): So two-dimensional: very sharp microbursts and longer tails. For POC we typically ask for a 14-day capture and we'll extract a burst heatmap and token-length histogram.
[02:29] Marco (Product): SLA we care most about is p95 and p99 for interactive flows, p99 ideally < 400ms for short Q/A and < 2s for multi-step assistant. Summaries can be higher but want predictable window.
[02:44] Miguel: Noted. For the benchmark we'll simulate realistic concurrency and same token distributions so the measured p95/p99 map to your production shape.

---
[03:10] (Discussion: data capture format)
[03:12] Jon: We can produce a CSV with timestamp, request_id, prompt_id, input_tokens, output_tokens_estimate, route, user_tier. Is that enough?
[03:25] Miguel: Yes, plus optional model_hint column; for replay we prefer to include sampling temperature and any function calls info. Make sure to hash user IDs.
[03:38] Priya: We'll scrub PII. How do you handle KV cache / prefix caching? We have lots of repeated prompts.
[03:50] Liam: Redwood runtime supports prefix caching and will reuse KV cache across similar sessions in Dedicated. For hosted we can enable caching policies but keys are ephemeral. We'll capture repeat ratios and factor that into the replay.

[04:12] (On dedicated vs hosted trade-offs)
[04:14] Priya: We're cost-sensitive; how different is cost if we go Dedicated?
[04:19] Ava: Dedicated gives predictable throughput and isolation but you pay for reserved capacity. We'll provide a model-level cost projection (tokens/hour and per-month reservation) vs on-demand hosted which is per-token. For heavy sustained base load Dedicated often is lower TCO.
[04:38] Marco: Also we need to understand warm-start/kv refill costs for cold-start sessions.
[04:42] Miguel: We'll include warmup procedures in the benchmark — run a 15-minute warmup to populate KV for common session prefixes.

[05:05] (Benchmark design discussion)
[05:07] Miguel: Proposed approach: 1) ingest 14 day csv, 2) produce heatmap of QPS & burst windows, 3) pick representative 3-hour windows: baseline, microburst, long-sustained peak, 4) run replay in our sandbox with same token mix, measure p50/p95/p99, GPU utilization, and token throughput.
[05:31] Priya: How accurate is replay vs live? We have some dynamic context and calls to external tools.
[05:36] Liam: Replay is effective for steady-state CPU/GPU and latency profiling but for tool calls we will synthesize or stub responses. We'll note limitations in the report. We'll also run a synthetic worst-case test (max tokens) and a TTL burst to measure tail behavior.

[06:00] (Timeline)
[06:02] Ava: Can NectarFlow get the scrubbed CSV by next week? That gives us time to run benchmarks and present results the week of 6/18.
[06:12] Priya: Yes we can aim for 6/12.
[06:15] Ava: Great — target deliverable timeline: traffic by 6/12, harness + first replay by 6/15, benchmark results and recommendation by 6/19, follow-up deep-dive on infra placement week of 6/22.

[06:34] (Constraints & success criteria)
[06:36] Priya: Success criteria: 1) POC must show p99 latency within agreed SLOs for the three representative windows, 2) A cost projection within 15% of projections, and 3) no regression in output quality for our core prompts (we'll provide eval set).
[06:54] Miguel: For quality checks we'll compare model outputs across baseline model you run today vs candidate variants. We'll use redwood's eval harness and provide an automatic diff report.

[07:12] (Tail latency and mitigation)
[07:14] Elena (SRE): What mitigation options exist for tail spikes? Autoscale, fallback models?
[07:20] Liam: Smart routing: we can define fallbacks to lower-cost/quantized variants for non-critical routes. Autoscaling limits and warm pools can absorb microbursts; for massive sustained spikes it's about capacity planning. We can demonstrate automatic fallback in the POC.

[07:40] (Instrumentation & observability)
[07:42] Miguel: We'll expose per-request token breakdown, per-route p95/p99, GPU memory utilization and KV cache hit rates. We'll hand off a dashboard link in the report.
[07:55] Jon: Need to ensure logs meet retention needs for audits — we asked about audit log exports to our SIEM.
[08:02] Ava: We'll include available integrations and retention tiers in the security pack.

---
[08:25] (Detailed Q/A — edge cases and clarifications)
[08:28] Priya: A few edge scenarios: 1) partially streaming outputs where we cut off early, 2) forced re-rolls (re-asks by the client), and 3) very long chain-of-thought debug traces.
[08:44] Miguel: We'll instrument streaming measurement (time-to-first-token and per-token latency) and measure cost of re-rolls by counting additional tokens. For chain-of-thought, we'll include an isolated test with long contexts to show GPU memory pressure.

[09:10] (On prompt distribution)
[09:13] Priya: We'll include three prompt buckets and mark which are high-value (must preserve top quality).
[09:21] Liam: For high-value prompts we'll recommend no quantized fallback during initial rollouts. For lower-tier routes we can enable int8 or 4-bit profiles. We'll model cost delta.

[09:45] (Benchmark run specifics)
[09:48] Miguel: Tech details: the replay harness uses VCR-style playback with timestamp scaling to reproduce bursts. We will run with realistic concurrency using a traffic driver (locust-based) and measure token/s, GPU util, and tail latencies. We'll run on an isolated Redwood sandbox; if you prefer we can also run the same harness on your dedicated infra.
[10:12] Jon: For the dedicated test, we need network egress numbers and exact instance SKUs.
[10:18] Ava: We'll supply SKU mapping and expected egress math in the cost sheet.

[10:30] (Security short note)
[10:31] Elena: KMS/HSM? Key control for private deploys?
[10:34] Miguel: Private / Dedicated options support customer-managed KMS and audit forwarding. We'll add details to the security pack.

[10:50] (Recap & next steps)
[10:52] Ava: Recap — Redwood will: share benchmark template, security pack, pricing comparison and sample replay harness. NectarFlow will provide scrubbed 14 day traffic and eval prompts by 6/12. We aim to run initial replay and deliver results by 6/19.
[11:12] Priya: That works. Any blockers— no blockers from our side other than scrubbing time. We'll coordinate with Jon and Elena.
[11:24] Marco: Also can you include an example of how caching changes latency for repeated prompts?
[11:29] Liam: Yes we'll include a cache-hit vs miss section in the report with microbenchmarks.

[11:45] Ava: Perfect. I'll send calendar invites for the replay window and the follow-up deep dive. Thanks everyone.
[11:53] (Meeting ends)

Action items (verbatim):
- Ava Chen: Send preliminary pricing + dedicated vs hosted cost model - due 2028-06-09
- Miguel Torres: Upload benchmark harness + replay script to shared folder - due 2028-06-08
- Priya Singh: Export and scrub 2 weeks traffic + sample prompts - due 2028-06-12

Shared artifacts referenced:
- Benchmark template & README (Redwood): https://redwood.company/benchmarks/nectarflow (placeholder)
- Security pack (SOC2, KMS options, audit logs) - to be shared via secure link
- Replay harness (locust driver + replay scripts) - will be uploaded to shared folder

Notes on transcript quality: automatic transcript; occasional word swap (e.g., "kv" transcribed as "K V"), speaker overlaps.

End of file.

title: Horizon QBR: Capacity Prioritization & Roadmap Sync
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2028-01-11
Start: 15:00 PST
Duration: ~57 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE); Marco Diaz (Redwood SE); Priya Raman (Horizon Head of ML); Tom Ellis (Horizon CTO); Liam Park (Horizon SRE)

Summary:
Quick QBR / health check to align on capacity needs for H1 rollout, clarify cost tradeoffs between hosted vs dedicated, confirm security posture for Private/VPC option, and prioritize roadmap items (autoscale, KV caching improvements, and rollout gating).

Topics:
- Current usage & spikes
- Cost vs latency tradeoffs (dedicated pools vs hosted)
- Security and compliance checklist (SSO, KMS, audit logs)
- Roadmap priorities and pilot scope
- Next steps + benchmarks

Action items (recap in call): see metadata action_items list above.

Transcript Body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick rollcall?
[00:06] Priya Raman: Hi, Priya here. I have Tom and Liam with me.
[00:09] Tom Ellis: Hey, Tom on the line.
[00:11] Liam Park: Liam, SRE - here.
[00:13] Marco Diaz: Marco from Redwood, SE, thanks for making time.
[00:16] Ava Chen: Cool. Goal for today — align on capacity priorities and figure next steps for the H1 rollout. We'll be pragmatic: usage, cost, and security.

[00:34] Priya Raman: Short context — we've ramped production in December, see a steady baseline ~250M tokens/month but bigger spikes during retraining runs and batch scoring, peaks up to 8k rps for short windows.
[00:48] Tom Ellis: Also the team is sensitive to tail latency, customers complain when p95 exceeds 700ms for chat flows. So cost matters but uptime and p95 are more important for us.

[01:12] Marco Diaz: Got it. Do you have distribution of sequence lengths? That drives batching and kernel choices.
[01:18] Priya Raman: Yes, we'll attach a histogram — most calls are 32–128 tokens but scoring jobs push up to 1024 tokens; a small percent >2048 during debugging.

[01:42] Ava Chen: Thanks. Two big levers we recommend: 1) dedicated pools for predictable latency and isolation, 2) prefix / KV cache for your conversational flows to cut per-token cost. Note: our Redwood Dedictated offering can reserve pools and also allow burst into hosted for peaks.

[02:10] Liam Park: How does cache invalidation work? We have dynamic user state so TTLs might be short.
[02:18] Marco Diaz: K V cache supports TTL and per-session eviction; but important note — large session state can increase memory pressure so we typically suggest a 48–72 hour TTL or event-based eviction. For dynamic state, you can keep minimal prompt context in the cache and rehydrate others from your storage.

[02:52] Tom Ellis: Security — we need SSO, SAML, RBAC, and KMS integration. Also audit logs for compliance. Does Redwood Private support customer-managed KMS/hardware HSM?
[03:04] Ava Chen: Yes — Private supports KMS integrations and HSM in our on-prem bundle; we'll send the SOC2 + ISO pack. Note that for VPC mode we can give a private control plane with customer-managed keys.

[03:30] Priya Raman: What's the estimated unit cost delta if we move to dedicated GPUs vs staying hosted? Rough numbers to justify a reserved commit.
[03:37] Marco Diaz: Short answer: depends on utilization. At 60–70% sustained utilization dedicated usually reduces per-token cost by 20–40% depending on model family and quantization. We can run a tailored pricing model. We'll send scenarios: low, medium, high utilization.

[04:04] Ava Chen: Marco, maybe show the benchmark numbers we ran last week for Horizon 12B v2?
[04:08] Marco Diaz: Sure, quick highlights — on A100-style hardware, p50 for completions at 512 context was 140ms, p95 around 420ms for single-stream; with batching (4–8) you get better throughput but p95 jumps, so it's a balancing act. Note: quantized runs we tested had ~1.7x throughput improvement but some minor output noise in one eval prompt.

[04:45] Priya Raman: That matches our internal findings. We care about p95 under 500ms for chat. Scoring batch jobs can tolerate higher p95.

[05:02] Liam Park: Can Redwood autoscale within a dedicated pool? We need headroom for daily peaks without manual intervention.
[05:08] Marco Diaz: Yes, dedicated autoscaling policies are configurable. You can set target utilization, cooldown windows. We also have burst capacity which temporarily borrows hosted capacity; that comes with some failover rules — e.g., if hosted capacity unavailable we fallback to lower-tier model variant.

[05:34] Tom Ellis: Concern — fallback to lower-tier model might change output quality. How do we detect/regressions fast?
[05:42] Ava Chen: Good point. We recommend guardrails: prompt-level quality checks and an eval suite attached to your rollout policy. Redwood Console can surface quality deltas and send alerts on model drift.

[06:00] Priya Raman: For the QBR, our priorities are: 1) guarantee p95 < 500ms for conversational product, 2) reduce monthly inference costs by ~25% within 6 months, 3) secure KMS and audit trail for compliance.

[06:20] Marco Diaz: We can map those to a plan: A) Run targeted benchmark on your representative prompts (I will run Horizon 12B v2 512C context) — gives latency curves; B) Pilot dedicated pool with 2 reserved nodes for 4 weeks to measure real-world utilization; C) Implement KV caching on top of low-hanging conversational flows.

[06:49] Priya Raman: Sounds good. What about the procurement timeline? We have finance and legal reviews; if the pilot looks good we want to commit to a Q2 reserved capacity.

[06:58] Ava Chen: Typical process is pilot -> procurement conversation -> reserved contract. We'll provide pricing scenarios and security docs to speed legal. If procurement is parallel, we can line up enterprise terms early.

[07:15] Priya Raman: Can you summarize how dedicated + burst pricing works again?
[07:19] Marco Diaz: Sure — baseline is commit (monthly/annual), which gives you reserved units at a fixed per-token or per-hour effective rate. Burst allows short-term use of hosted capacity beyond your reserved units at a higher per-unit price. You only pay for what you use; we include an overage / pre-approved cap to avoid surprises.

[07:40] Tom Ellis: Are there penalties for scaling down after commit? We want flexibility but not locked into huge spend.

[07:47] Ava Chen: Contracts are negotiable; there are standard ramp-down clauses. For pilots we usually suggest a short-term commitment or month-to-month for the first quarter to avoid over-commitment.

[08:05] Liam Park: On the technical side: how do you handle model versioning and rollouts? We need canary and automatic rollback.
[08:13] Marco Diaz: Redwood Console supports canary rollouts and A/B split; you can pin a percent of traffic to a new model and watch metrics. If QoE drops or latency spikes, automatic fallback kicks in. We also retain request traces and cost per token by token type.

[08:40] Priya Raman: Any caveats with longer contexts (2k+)? Our scoring sometimes uses 2048 context length for summarization.

[08:46] Marco Diaz: Longer contexts increase memory and KV cache footprint; kernel selection changes — sometimes different transformer kernels run better. For 2k+ we sometimes recommend offloading non-critical state or using shorter near-history windows plus external retrieval.

[09:10] Ava Chen: Okay, let's capture concrete deliverables: Marco will run the benchmark with your prompt set; Ava will send pricing scenarios and SOC2 pack; Priya will send token histograms and sample prompts.

[09:22] Priya Raman: Quick operational question — latency observability: does Redwood surface per-route p95 by token length?
[09:28] Marco Diaz: Yes, per-route, per-model, and you can slice by input length. We also have token-level cost breakdown and sampling.

[09:41] Tom Ellis: There was a question internally about vendor lock-in — if we go Private on-prem is switching away harder?

[09:49] Ava Chen: Private is customer-controlled infra; we provide tooling and APIs consistent with hosted offering. You own keys and data, so migration out is a standard export path for models, configs, and logs. We can provide a migration runbook.

[10:05] Liam Park: Any suggestions for cost control during heavy batch scoring windows?

[10:11] Marco Diaz: Batch scheduling with off-peak windows, queueing, and using lower-precision quantized variants for scoring helps. Also caching repeated prefixes and deduping requests.

[10:29] Priya Raman: For our pilot scope, can we keep the pilot to 2 reserved nodes with autoscale up to 3 and burst into hosted during spikes?

[10:35] Ava Chen: Yes, that's a common conservative pilot. We'll model expected utilization and show cost delta.

[10:44] Tom Ellis: Last, how long until we get the detailed benchmark and the pricing scenarios?

[10:49] Ava Chen: We'll aim to deliver pricing by 2028-01-18 and benchmark results by 2028-01-18 as well. Technical deep-dive meeting planned week of 2028-01-23 — SE-led.

[11:05] Priya Raman: Great. Anything else?
[11:08] Liam Park: One more SRE note — we want alerting for KV cache pressure and a playbook for eviction.
[11:14] Marco Diaz: We'll include a recommended alerting set and the eviction playbook in the benchmark notes.

[11:25] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. Recap in the thread: pricing scenarios, SOC2 + KMS docs, benchmark results, sample prompt upload, and schedule the deep dive.

[11:37] Tom Ellis: Thanks all.
[11:39] Priya Raman: Thanks.
[11:40] Marco Diaz: Thanks, talk soon.

[11:45] Meeting ends.

text:
Meeting Header:
Date: 2028-01-11
Start: 15:00 PST
Duration: ~57 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE); Marco Diaz (Redwood SE); Priya Raman (Horizon Head of ML); Tom Ellis (Horizon CTO); Liam Park (Horizon SRE)

Summary:
Quick QBR / health check to align on capacity needs for H1 rollout, clarify cost tradeoffs between hosted vs dedicated, confirm security posture for Private/VPC option, and prioritize roadmap items (autoscale, KV caching improvements, and rollout gating).

Topics:
- Current usage & spikes
- Cost vs latency tradeoffs (dedicated pools vs hosted)
- Security and compliance checklist (SSO, KMS, audit logs)
- Roadmap priorities and pilot scope
- Next steps + benchmarks

Action items (recap in call): see metadata action_items list above.

Transcript Body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick rollcall?
[00:06] Priya Raman: Hi, Priya here. I have Tom and Liam with me.
[00:09] Tom Ellis: Hey, Tom on the line.
[00:11] Liam Park: Liam, SRE - here.
[00:13] Marco Diaz: Marco from Redwood, SE, thanks for making time.
[00:16] Ava Chen: Cool. Goal for today — align on capacity priorities and figure next steps for the H1 rollout. We'll be pragmatic: usage, cost, and security.

[00:34] Priya Raman: Short context — we've ramped production in December, see a steady baseline ~250M tokens/month but bigger spikes during retraining runs and batch scoring, peaks up to 8k rps for short windows.
[00:48] Tom Ellis: Also the team is sensitive to tail latency, customers complain when p95 exceeds 700ms for chat flows. So cost matters but uptime and p95 are more important for us.

[01:12] Marco Diaz: Got it. Do you have distribution of sequence lengths? That drives batching and kernel choices.
[01:18] Priya Raman: Yes, we'll attach a histogram — most calls are 32–128 tokens but scoring jobs push up to 1024 tokens; a small percent >2048 during debugging.

[01:42] Ava Chen: Thanks. Two big levers we recommend: 1) dedicated pools for predictable latency and isolation, 2) prefix / KV cache for your conversational flows to cut per-token cost. Note: our Redwood Dedictated offering can reserve pools and also allow burst into hosted for peaks.

[02:10] Liam Park: How does cache invalidation work? We have dynamic user state so TTLs might be short.
[02:18] Marco Diaz: K V cache supports TTL and per-session eviction; but important note — large session state can increase memory pressure so we typically suggest a 48–72 hour TTL or event-based eviction. For dynamic state, you can keep minimal prompt context in the cache and rehydrate others from your storage.

[02:52] Tom Ellis: Security — we need SSO, SAML, RBAC, and KMS integration. Also audit logs for compliance. Does Redwood Private support customer-managed KMS/hardware HSM?
[03:04] Ava Chen: Yes — Private supports KMS integrations and HSM in our on-prem bundle; we'll send the SOC2 + ISO pack. Note that for VPC mode we can give a private control plane with customer-managed keys.

[03:30] Priya Raman: What's the estimated unit cost delta if we move to dedicated GPUs vs staying hosted? Rough numbers to justify a reserved commit.
[03:37] Marco Diaz: Short answer: depends on utilization. At 60–70% sustained utilization dedicated usually reduces per-token cost by 20–40% depending on model family and quantization. We can run a tailored pricing model. We'll send scenarios: low, medium, high utilization.

[04:04] Ava Chen: Marco, maybe show the benchmark numbers we ran last week for Horizon 12B v2?
[04:08] Marco Diaz: Sure, quick highlights — on A100-style hardware, p50 for completions at 512 context was 140ms, p95 around 420ms for single-stream; with batching (4–8) you get better throughput but p95 jumps, so it's a balancing act. Note: quantized runs we tested had ~1.7x throughput improvement but some minor output noise in one eval prompt.

[04:45] Priya Raman: That matches our internal findings. We care about p95 under 500ms for chat. Scoring batch jobs can tolerate higher p95.

[05:02] Liam Park: Can Redwood autoscale within a dedicated pool? We need headroom for daily peaks without manual intervention.
[05:08] Marco Diaz: Yes, dedicated autoscaling policies are configurable. You can set target utilization, cooldown windows. We also have burst capacity which temporarily borrows hosted capacity; that comes with some failover rules — e.g., if hosted capacity unavailable we fallback to lower-tier model variant.

[05:34] Tom Ellis: Concern — fallback to lower-tier model might change output quality. How do we detect/regressions fast?
[05:42] Ava Chen: Good point. We recommend guardrails: prompt-level quality checks and an eval suite attached to your rollout policy. Redwood Console can surface quality deltas and send alerts on model drift.

[06:00] Priya Raman: For the QBR, our priorities are: 1) guarantee p95 < 500ms for conversational product, 2) reduce monthly inference costs by ~25% within 6 months, 3) secure KMS and audit trail for compliance.

[06:20] Marco Diaz: We can map those to a plan: A) Run targeted benchmark on your representative prompts (I will run Horizon 12B v2 512C context) — gives latency curves; B) Pilot dedicated pool with 2 reserved nodes for 4 weeks to measure real-world utilization; C) Implement KV caching on top of low-hanging conversational flows.

[06:49] Priya Raman: Sounds good. What about the procurement timeline? We have finance and legal reviews; if the pilot looks good we want to commit to a Q2 reserved capacity.

[06:58] Ava Chen: Typical process is pilot -> procurement conversation -> reserved contract. We'll provide pricing scenarios and security docs to speed legal. If procurement is parallel, we can line up enterprise terms early.

[07:15] Priya Raman: Can you summarize how dedicated + burst pricing works again?
[07:19] Marco Diaz: Sure — baseline is commit (monthly/annual), which gives you reserved units at a fixed per-token or per-hour effective rate. Burst allows short-term use of hosted capacity beyond your reserved units at a higher per-unit price. You only pay for what you use; we include an overage / pre-approved cap to avoid surprises.

[07:40] Tom Ellis: Are there penalties for scaling down after commit? We want flexibility but not locked into huge spend.

[07:47] Ava Chen: Contracts are negotiable; there are standard ramp-down clauses. For pilots we usually suggest a short-term commitment or month-to-month for the first quarter to avoid over-commitment.

[08:05] Liam Park: On the technical side: how do you handle model versioning and rollouts? We need canary and automatic rollback.
[08:13] Marco Diaz: Redwood Console supports canary rollouts and A/B split; you can pin a percent of traffic to a new model and watch metrics. If QoE drops or latency spikes, automatic fallback kicks in. We also retain request traces and cost per token by token type.

[08:40] Priya Raman: Any caveats with longer contexts (2k+)? Our scoring sometimes uses 2048 context length for summarization.

[08:46] Marco Diaz: Longer contexts increase memory and KV cache footprint; kernel selection changes — sometimes different transformer kernels run better. For 2k+ we sometimes recommend offloading non-critical state or using shorter near-history windows plus external retrieval.

[09:10] Ava Chen: Okay, let's capture concrete deliverables: Marco will run the benchmark with your prompt set; Ava will send pricing scenarios and SOC2 pack; Priya will send token histograms and sample prompts.

[09:22] Priya Raman: Quick operational question — latency observability: does Redwood surface per-route p95 by token length?
[09:28] Marco Diaz: Yes, per-route, per-model, and you can slice by input length. We also have token-level cost breakdown and sampling.

[09:41] Tom Ellis: There was a question internally about vendor lock-in — if we go Private on-prem is switching away harder?

[09:49] Ava Chen: Private is customer-controlled infra; we provide tooling and APIs consistent with hosted offering. You own keys and data, so migration out is a standard export path for models, configs, and logs. We can provide a migration runbook.

[10:05] Liam Park: Any suggestions for cost control during heavy batch scoring windows?

[10:11] Marco Diaz: Batch scheduling with off-peak windows, queueing, and using lower-precision quantized variants for scoring helps. Also caching repeated prefixes and deduping requests.

[10:29] Priya Raman: For our pilot scope, can we keep the pilot to 2 reserved nodes with autoscale up to 3 and burst into hosted during spikes?

[10:35] Ava Chen: Yes, that's a common conservative pilot. We'll model expected utilization and show cost delta.

[10:44] Tom Ellis: Last, how long until we get the detailed benchmark and the pricing scenarios?

[10:49] Ava Chen: We'll aim to deliver pricing by 2028-01-18 and benchmark results by 2028-01-18 as well. Technical deep-dive meeting planned week of 2028-01-23 — SE-led.

[11:05] Priya Raman: Great. Anything else?
[11:08] Liam Park: One more SRE note — we want alerting for KV cache pressure and a playbook for eviction.
[11:14] Marco Diaz: We'll include a recommended alerting set and the eviction playbook in the benchmark notes.

[11:25] Ava Chen: Thanks everyone. Recap in the thread: pricing scenarios, SOC2 + KMS docs, benchmark results, sample prompt upload, and schedule the deep dive.

[11:37] Tom Ellis: Thanks all.
[11:39] Priya Raman: Thanks.
[11:40] Marco Diaz: Thanks, talk soon.

[11:45] Meeting ends.

Notes on transcript quality: auto-generated, contains mis-hearings and occasional speaker attribution errors. References shared during call: pricing-sheet-v1 (internal link), SOC2-matrix.pdf (sent via email), benchmark-scripts (repo link).

title: Cygnus Health — Modeling & Tool-Calling Scouting
preview: Initial scoping with Cygnus Health on model selection and deployment constraints. Key drivers: PHI compliance (HIPAA-like), long-context summaries from patient histories (2-6k tokens), Spanish/Portuguese support, and tool-calling into EHR and lab-result APIs. Discussed hosted vs dedicated vs private (VPC/on-prem) tradeoffs, fallback routing, caching, and expected QPS. Agreed next steps: security docs, benchmark plan, and POC scheduling.

title: Key rotation playbook sync and BYOK handoff - Evermoor Finance
preview:
Meeting header: 2027-09-30 15:00 UTC | Duration ~58m | Recording: yes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Ramos (Redwood SE), Martin Geller (Evermoor Head of Security), Priya Nair (Cloud Architect), Lena Ortiz (Legal), Samir Patel (Infra)

Summary: Quick sync to align on Evermoor's required key rotation playbook, BYOK handoff flow for EU-regulated vaults, and legal/escrow constraints. Focus on operational steps, audit trail needs, and an HSM connector proof plan.

[00:00:00] Ava: hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick roll call?
[00:00:07] Martin: hi martin here, head of security at evermoor. we have priya lena and samir on from infra and legal.
[00:00:12] Ava: awesome, im ava chen from redwood, and diego our se will run through the ops flow.
[00:00:18] Diego: thanks, yeah i'll go through the sequence diagrams and then we can drill into questions.
[00:00:27] Priya: hi, quick note - we're on a 30 minute maintenance window for rotation windows, hoping not to blow that.
[00:00:33] Ava: noted. objective here is to confirm the runbook so rotation doesn't impact tokens or running inferences, and to confirm how BYOK/HSM handoff will look with your EU vaults.
[00:00:44] Diego: ok jumping in - i'll start with our default flow: key creation in customer KMS, optional import into Redwood via secure key-transport (wrap/unwrap) using an HSM-backed envelope key, then we maintain a key reference only, we never store raw key material.
[00:00:58] Martin: quick clar — when you say import, do you mean you copy keys to redwood's HSM or just a reference? compliance really wants to know who holds the unwrapped key.
[00:01:09] Diego: great question. we support two modes: 1) redwood-managed keys where we host the wrapping keys in our HSM and we hold the key material encrypted at rest; 2) BYOK with customer-managed HSM: we accept wrapped key material and never keep an unwrapped copy — the unwrap happens inside your HSM at handoff time if you choose on-prem HSM, or inside your cloud KMS if you want to keep custody.
[00:01:36] Priya: so for EU-regulated workloads we want option 2 only — we need key sovereignty. remote unwrap must be in our VPC or on-prem HSM.
[00:01:44] Diego: understood, we have a private deployment pattern that installs a connector in your VPC that facilitates a wrap/unwrap handshake with your HSM, and we can run the control plane separately if required.
[00:01:55] Priya: that connector — is it agent-based? does it require outbound 443? firewalls etc?
[00:02:02] Diego: it's a small connector service, typically outbound TLS to our control plane is used for orchestration, but the crypto operations (wrap/unwrap) can be pinned to your HSM endpoints. we can do an on-prem-only flow with no egress if you opt for air-gapped private deploy.
[00:02:20] Samir: that last bit matters. our security team wants no egress for key material, ever.
[00:02:25] Ava: we can support that via private deploy and a customer-managed control plane. we'll capture that as a requirement.
[00:02:31] [00:05:10] Diego: moving on to rotation cadence: we recommend rotating KEKs (key-encrypting keys) quarterly for high-sensitivity, but Evermoor's payments workload might want monthly rotation. rotation can be scheduled and is zero-downtime when using prefix cache invalidation + streaming KV cache update.
[00:05:25] Martin: we care about token invalidation window. what happens to in-flight sessions?
[00:05:32] Diego: we do a staged rotation: create new KEK, wrap new DEKs under new KEK, rewrap data lazily on access, maintain dual-key acceptance window for 7 days by default. this can be shortened to your SLA.
[00:05:50] Priya: rewrap lazily is fine but we need audit provenance that shows which DEK used at time of encryption and who initiated rewrap.
[00:05:58] Diego: all operations emit audit events to audit-log stream: key-create, key-rotate, key-wrap, unwrap request origin, and operator identity via SSO/RBAC binding. logs are tamper-evident and can be forwarded to your SIEM.
[00:06:16] Martin: tamper-evident — do you use signed log chains or write-once storage?
[00:06:22] Diego: we support write-once append-only storage for audit logs plus HMAC-signed log chunks. you can also ingest logs into your own S3/GCP bucket via push with server-side encryption.
[00:06:33] [00:08:05] Priya: on RBAC, we need separation between ops who can rotate and devs who can trigger model inferences. can you map to our existing SAML groups?
[00:08:13] Ava: yes, SSO/SAML integration is standard. we map SAML groups to Redwood roles and can limit key-op permissions to a small group. we also support time-limited elevation and approval flows.
[00:08:27] Lena: legal question — escrow: if keys are customer-managed, is there any situation where Redwood can be compelled to provide access? we need clarity for contract.
[00:08:37] Ava: contractually we don't have unwrapped key access for BYOK-customer-managed flows. for redwood-managed keys we have operational access but it's constrained by SOC2 controls and access logs; we can provide audit evidence. escrow exceptions would be covered in the legal playbook we share.
[00:08:52] Lena: okay we'll want a redline for language that says 'no unwrapping without explicit customer request under subpoena with mutually agreed escrow process'.
[00:09:00] [00:11:40] Diego: demo time — here's a simplified sequence: 1) customer generates DEK and KEK in their KMS; 2) export DEK wrapped under KEK; 3) transfer wrapped blob to Redwood via TLS; 4) Redwood stores the wrapped blob and references KEK metadata. when an unwrap is required for migration or certain operations, unwrap happens in customer HSM with a signed request.
[00:11:58] Priya: small nit — we cannot export some keys from HSM due to policy, so we need an integration where the HSM signs a transfer attestation rather than exporting the key.
[00:12:09] Diego: we support attestation-based flows: your HSM can sign a key-wrapping certificate or an attestation token that proves possession of the key without export. Redwood can accept that attestation and proceed with envelope encryption patterns.
[00:12:22] Martin: can you show how the attestation ties to the audit trail? i want an immutable proof chain.
[00:12:28] Diego: attestation tokens are logged and stored with the operation metadata. we include the HSM attestation signature in the audit entry and also log the certificate chain. these entries are integrity-protected.
[00:12:39] [00:15:00] Priya: data residency question — our EU vaults cannot have key material pass through non-EU infrastructure. will the connector ensure that the key blobs never traverse non-EU?
[00:15:08] Ava: yes, for private deploy the key transfer is local to your region. our control plane can be regionalized, or you can run the control plane in your VPC. we will document all network paths.
[00:15:18] Samir: we need IP/port requirements for firewall allow-lists for the connector.
[00:15:22] Diego: we'll provide a minimal allow-list: outbound 443 to control-plane endpoint (if using hosted control plane) or none if you self-host. for HSM connectivity we will need your HSM IPs if it's an on-prem HSM.
[00:15:30] [00:19:45] Martin: audit retention — regulators ask for 7 years for payments logs. can Redwood store logs that long, and are they queryable?
[00:19:53] Ava: yes we have long-term log retention options as an add-on. logs can be exported to your storage for archiving and are searchable via our console for the retention window.
[00:20:03] Priya: what about encryption in transit — TLS is fine, but do you support mTLS between the connector and Redwood endpoints?
[00:20:10] Diego: yes mTLS is supported for the connector, certificate management can be automated through ACME or you can inject certs via PKI.
[00:20:18] [00:23:00] Diego: practical runbook steps we propose: 1) pre-checks (inventory keys, list aliases, current rotation cadence, maintenance windows); 2) deploy connector in staging; 3) simulate key rotation in staging with a sample dataset; 4) run live pilot in a single region; 5) roll out production with canary rewrap.
[00:23:18] Priya: for the simulation, can you provide a script to run the rewrap and emit the exact audit events? we want parity with prod logs.
[00:23:26] Diego: yes, we'll provide a simulation harness that emits audit events and a CSV for the compliance team.
[00:23:32] [00:27:10] Martin: SLA — if a rotation goes bad, what's the rollback? we cannot lose availability for auth traffic.
[00:27:18] Diego: rollback plan: we keep previous KEK active and accept both old and new keys for a configured window. in catastrophic case, you can failover to redwood-managed KEK if previously provisioned as a contingency, but that requires contract and legal sign-off for key custody.
[00:27:35] Lena: that contingency is exactly what we'll need contract language for — fallback to provider-managed only with explicit customer authorization.
[00:27:42] [00:31:50] Priya: operationally, who can execute a manual rotation? our ops lead must approve. can we require two-person approval?
[00:31:58] Ava: yes we support approval workflows and 2-person ops approvals for key-critical actions. those approvals are logged and require SSO auth.
[00:32:07] Samir: curious about cryptoperf — any impact on latency for inferences after rotation?
[00:32:12] Diego: negligible for DEK rewrap on access; for cold re-encryption jobs there is CPU/GPU work but it's backgrounded. token-level latency is unaffected because inference uses cached DEKs; we just atomically swap references.
[00:32:28] [00:36:00] Diego: demo snippet — here's the audit event sample: {event: 'key-rotate', actor: 'priya@evermoor', attestation: 'signed-by-hsm-abc123', timestamp: '2027-09-30T14:59:30Z'} — note: transcript auto-caption sometimes munges tokens, actual log will be JSON with signature fields.
[00:36:18] Martin: we need retention, immutability, and export to our SIEM. can you commit to SIEM endpoint schemas?
[00:36:25] Ava: we'll provide a schema and a mapping spreadsheet for your security team.
[00:36:30] [00:40:00] Priya: network: for HSM we use a hardware HSM that only accepts connections from our jump host. will connector be able to talk to that host?
[00:40:08] Diego: yes, connector can be located in same subnet as HSM or route via the jump host. we have example terraform modules for the placement.
[00:40:20] Priya: good. for attestation, we use vendor-specific PKCS#11 extensions, will you support PKCS#11 flows?
[00:40:28] Diego: we support PKCS#11 and KMIP interfaces; our connector uses the vendor driver to call native ops. some vendor nuances exist but we've integrated with major HSMs.
[00:40:40] [00:45:15] Lena: legal again — what logs do you preserve about legal requests? subpoena logs?
[00:45:23] Ava: we log every legal or compliance access request and maintain a changelog with the request artifact. that can be exported as part of incident response packs.
[00:45:32] Martin: any pen-test or cryptographic review we can provide them?
[00:45:37] Ava: yes, we can share recent pentest summaries and our cryptographic design doc under NDA.
[00:45:44] [00:50:00] Diego: edge-case: key compromise. if a KEK is suspected compromised, sequence is: revoke KEK, create new KEK, rewrap DEKs under new KEK, force reissue of any active credentials that depended on compromised keys, and run forensics via the tamper-evident logs.
[00:50:18] Priya: how fast can rewrap job complete for our DB size?
[00:50:24] Diego: depends on data size; our batching engine can do parallel rewrap at X GB/min per pool — we'll run sizing with your dataset during the pilot.
[00:50:34] [00:54:00] Martin: ok this is super helpful. next steps recap?
[00:54:06] Ava: i'll send SOC2/ISO artifacts, diego will send runbook and simulation harness, priya will share key inventory and rotation cadence, lena will draft escrow redline.
[00:54:16] Lena: i'll also ask legal for any regulatory notes on cross-border warrants.
[00:54:22] Samir: i'll coordinate firewall allow-list and share HSM endpoint details with diego.
[00:54:30] Ava: great, we'll target the HSM deep-dive the week of 2027-10-10. thanks everyone.
[00:54:36] Diego: thanks all.
[00:57:30] [END]

[00:12:22] Martin: can you show how the attestation ties to the audit trail? i want an immutable proof chain.
[00:12:28] Diego: attestation tokens are logged and stored with the operation metadata. we include the HSM attestation signature in the audit entry and also log the certificate chain. these entries are integrity-protected.
[00:12:39] [00:15:00] Priya: data residency question — our EU vaults cannot have key material pass through non-EU infrastructure. will the connector ensure that the key blobs never traverse non-EU?
[00:15:08] Ava: yes, for private deploy the key transfer is local to your region. our control plane can be regionalized, or you can run the control plane in your VPC. we will document all network paths.
[00:15:18] Samir: we need IP/port requirements for firewall allow-lists for the connector.
[00:15:22] Diego: we'll provide a minimal allow-list: outbound 443 to control-plane endpoint (if using hosted control plane) or none if you self-host. for HSM connectivity we will need your HSM IPs if it's an on-prem HSM.
[00:15:30] [00:19:45] Martin: audit retention — regulators ask for 7 years for payments logs. can Redwood store logs that long, and are they queryable?
[00:19:53] Ava: yes we have long-term log retention options as an add-on. logs can be exported to your storage for archiving and are searchable via our console for the retention window.
[00:20:03] Priya: what about encryption in transit — TLS is fine, but do you support mTLS between the connector and Redwood endpoints?
[00:20:10] Diego: yes mTLS is supported for the connector, certificate management can be automated through ACME or you can inject certs via PKI.
[00:20:18] [00:23:00] Diego: practical runbook steps we propose: 1) pre-checks (inventory keys, list aliases, current rotation cadence, maintenance windows); 2) deploy connector in staging; 3) simulate key rotation in staging with a sample dataset; 4) run live pilot in a single region; 5) roll out production with canary rewrap.
[00:23:18] Priya: for the simulation, can you provide a script to run the rewrap and emit the exact audit events? we want parity with prod logs.
[00:23:26] Diego: yes, we'll provide a simulation harness that emits audit events and a CSV for the compliance team.
[00:23:32] [00:27:10] Martin: SLA — if a rotation goes bad, what's the rollback? we cannot lose availability for auth traffic.
[00:27:18] Diego: rollback plan: we keep previous KEK active and accept both old and new keys for a configured window. in catastrophic case, you can failover to redwood-managed KEK if previously provisioned as a contingency, but that requires contract and legal sign-off for key custody.
[00:27:35] Lena: that contingency is exactly what we'll need contract language for — fallback to provider-managed only with explicit customer authorization.
[00:27:42] [00:31:50] Priya: operationally, who can execute a manual rotation? our ops lead must approve. can we require two-person approval?
[00:31:58] Ava: yes we support approval workflows and 2-person ops approvals for key-critical actions. those approvals are logged and require SSO auth.
[00:32:07] Samir: curious about cryptoperf — any impact on latency for inferences after rotation?
[00:32:12] Diego: negligible for DEK rewrap on access; for cold re-encryption jobs there is CPU/GPU work but it's backgrounded. token-level latency is unaffected because inference uses cached DEKs; we just atomically swap references.
[00:32:28] [00:36:00] Diego: demo snippet — here's the audit event sample: {event: 'key-rotate', actor: 'priya@evermoor', attestation: 'signed-by-hsm-abc123', timestamp: '2027-09-30T14:59:30Z'} — note: transcript auto-caption sometimes munges tokens, actual log will be JSON with signature fields.
[00:36:18] Martin: we need retention, immutability, and export to our SIEM. can you commit to SIEM endpoint schemas?
[00:36:25] Ava: we'll provide a schema and a mapping spreadsheet for your security team.
[00:36:30] [00:40:00] Priya: network: for HSM we use a hardware HSM that only accepts connections from our jump host. will connector be able to talk to that host?
[00:40:08] Diego: yes, connector can be located in same subnet as HSM or route via the jump host. we have example terraform modules for the placement.
[00:40:20] Priya: good. for attestation, we use vendor-specific PKCS#11 extensions, will you support PKCS#11 flows?
[00:40:28] Diego: we support PKCS#11 and KMIP interfaces; our connector uses the vendor driver to call native ops. some vendor nuances exist but we've integrated with major HSMs.
[00:40:40] [00:45:15] Lena: legal again — what logs do you preserve about legal requests? subpoena logs?
[00:45:23] Ava: we log every legal or compliance access request and maintain a changelog with the request artifact. that can be exported as part of incident response packs.
[00:45:32] Martin: any pen-test or cryptographic review we can provide them?
[00:45:37] Ava: yes, we can share recent pentest summaries and our cryptographic design doc under NDA.
[00:45:44] [00:50:00] Diego: edge-case: key compromise. if a KEK is suspected compromised, sequence is: revoke KEK, create new KEK, rewrap DEKs under new KEK, force reissue of any active credentials that depended on compromised keys, and run forensics via the tamper-evident logs.
[00:50:18] Priya: how fast can rewrap job complete for our DB size?
[00:50:24] Diego: depends on data size; our batching engine can do parallel rewrap at X GB/min per pool — we'll run sizing with your dataset during the pilot.
[00:50:34] [00:54:00] Martin: ok this is super helpful. next steps recap?
[00:54:06] Ava: i'll send SOC2/ISO artifacts, diego will send runbook and simulation harness, priya will share key inventory and rotation cadence, lena will draft escrow redline.
[00:54:16] Lena: i'll also ask legal for any regulatory notes on cross-border warrants.
[00:54:22] Samir: i'll coordinate firewall allow-list and share HSM endpoint details with diego.
[00:54:30] Ava: great, we'll target the HSM deep-dive the week of 2027-10-10. thanks everyone.
[00:54:36] Diego: thanks all.
[00:57:30] [END]

title: POC deployment mode checklist — Polaris Research
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2027-09-09 15:30 UTC
Duration: 54 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Morales (Redwood SE), Riley Park (Redwood CSM), Maya Singh (Polaris Head of ML), Tom Reynolds (Infra Lead), Ellen Park (Security), Samir Patel (Product)

Auto-summary (auto-gen, may be rough): Quick POC scoping to pick a deployment mode for Polaris' document-extraction inference pipeline. Discussed success criteria (p95 latency, throughput, quality), expected workload burst patterns (nightly analytics + peak inference during business hours), benchmark plan (2-week sprint), cost/latency tradeoffs, and security requirements (SOC 2, VPC, KMS). Next: run hosted vs dedicated microbenchmarks, share security docs, draft pilot success criteria.

00:00 - Ava Chen: hey everyone, thanks for taking the time. quick roll call — say hi and your role so we can capture notes.
00:08 - Maya Singh: hi, maya here, head of ml at polaris. we own the ingestion and model stack.
00:12 - Tom Reynolds: tom, infra lead, i'll be owning the pilot infra and network.
00:15 - Ellen Park: ellen, security — i'll be validating compliance and data flows.
00:18 - Samir Patel: samir, product, coordinating success criteria.
00:21 - Diego Morales: diego from redwood, i'll run benchmarks and techn stack questions.
00:25 - Ava Chen: cool. agenda: align on success metrics, sizing, benchmarks, and decide hosted/dedicated/private for the 6-week POC. no hard sales pitch, just options.

02:10 - Samir Patel: high level — we have an existing transformer model that does entity extraction on financial docs. expected qps average 12 req/s with bursts 80 req/s during end-of-day batch, bursty tokens per request avg 1.2k tokens but some long docs up to ~10k tokens.
02:34 - Maya Singh: correct, token distribution is heavy tail. latency goal is p95 < 250ms for short docs (<2k tokens) and < 1.2s for long jobs, but we'd prefer tighter for interactive features.
02:50 - Tom Reynolds: side constraint: data must not leave our VPC for regulated clients; we need to understand private option or dedicated in VPC.
03:05 - Ellen Park: also SOC 2 + ISO questions, and key management — we use a customer-managed KMS. can redwood support BYOK (bring your own key) for private/dedicated?

04:00 - Diego Morales: quick notes: redwood supports three modes — hosted api (fast to start), dedicated (reserved GPU pools, single-tenant) and private (VPC / on-prem with customer network). BYOK is supported for private and dedicated with our enterprise package, hosted has envelope encryption but not full BYOK unless in private mode. (note: auto-transcript sometimes says B-Y-OK as bye-ok)
04:28 - Maya Singh: good — we need that detail. cost is a big factor. our CFO will ask host vs dedicated delta.

06:00 - Ava Chen: success criteria thought-starter: 1) functional parity (extraction accuracy within 1% of baseline), 2) latency p95 targets by doc-size, 3) throughput for peak windows (80 req/s sustained for 10 minutes), 4) cost target: total TCO for pilot under $X (we discussed range in follow-up), 5) security & data residency constraints met. does that capture it?
06:25 - Samir Patel: yes, but we want also rollback plan and canary routing in case of regressions.
06:40 - Diego Morales: we can support canary rollouts in dedicated and private modes; hosted supports version pinning and traffic split but not customer-isolated network routing.

08:00 - Tom Reynolds: risk question — what if we need GPUs with specific kernels or quantization? we run mixed models some int8, some fp16.
08:18 - Diego Morales: runtime supports quantization-friendly exec path; we can test both fp16 and int8. for specific kernels we can validate on our dedicated hardware during the benchmark window. sometimes the transcript mangles kernels as 'kernel suns' or so.

10:00 - Maya Singh: benchmark plan? we'd like a two-week sprint where you run hosted and dedicated runs against our traces. output should include p50/p95/p99 latency, token throughput, GPU utilization, and cost per 1k tokens approximation.
10:20 - Diego Morales: agreed. proposal: week 1 synthetic + replay of 48h traces on hosted to get baseline, week 2 dedicated/reserved run to measure improvement and isolation. we'll deliver a spreadsheet with the metrics and a recommendation.
10:40 - Ava Chen: timeline: goal is to make deployment decision by end of Sept. pilot starts early Oct. suggested POC length 6 weeks (build, iterate, production integration).

12:00 - Ellen Park: security specifics we'll need: network diagram, data flow, retention policy, audit logs, how long are KV caches persisted, ability to turn off prefix caching for sensitive docs, and evidence of SOC 2/ISO.
12:28 - Diego Morales: noted: audit logs and per-route tracing available in Redwood Console; KV cache persists in memory by default and can be disabled or encrypted at rest in private mode. I'll include these details in security packet.

14:00 - Samir Patel: on cost — what's the rough differential between hosted and dedicated for our profile?
14:10 - Ava Chen: rough napkin: hosted gives fastest time-to-value but per-token unit cost is higher; dedicated reduces unit cost at the expense of commit; private adds operational overhead but meets strict VPC/KMS needs. we need to run the traces to produce real numbers.

15:30 - Tom Reynolds: fallback behavior: if dedicated capacity exhausted, will requests fail or route to hosted?
15:40 - Diego Morales: routing policy is configurable — we can set automatic fallback to hosted for non-sensitive traffic, or fail closed for sensitive clients. during POC we recommend testing both.

17:00 - Maya Singh: one thing — long jobs up to 10k tokens will likely be batched as async jobs. would Redwood support batch async processing in dedicated/private?
17:12 - Diego Morales: yes, dedicated/private support async batch queues and autoscaling policies; hosted supports queuing but has stricter per-request limits. we'll document recommended autoscaling curves.

19:00 - Ava Chen: let's capture explicit POC success criteria now:
19:10 - Samir Patel: 1) Extraction F1 within 1% of current baseline across sample set (accuracy)
19:20 - Maya Singh: 2) For short docs (<2k tokens) p95 latency < 250ms on average workload
19:30 - Maya Singh: 3) Sustained peak throughput 80 req/s for 10 minutes with less than 5% error rate
19:45 - Tom Reynolds: 4) Security: network flows stay within our VPC or private deployment option enabled, BYOK works, and audit logs delivered to our SIEM
20:00 - Ellen Park: 5) Cost: pilot TCO within agreed envelope — we'll define exact $$ via spreadsheet

21:10 - Diego Morales: good. i'll own the benchmark plan and produce a result doc. recommended runs: hosted baseline, dedicated single-tenant run, and private if you want to test BYOK and network constraints (private requires more setup time).

23:30 - Samir Patel: can you estimate setup time for private vs dedicated?
23:40 - Diego Morales: dedicated: about 3-7 business days to provision reserved capacity once contract signed; private (VPC or on-prem) usually 2-4 weeks depending on networking approvals. hosted is immediate.
23:58 - Ava Chen: note that private often needs our security engineer to be on the call to exchange firewall policies and certs.

26:00 - Tom Reynolds: any compliance gaps we should pre-flag?
26:12 - Ellen Park: encryption at rest, KMS integration, SSO via SAML, RBAC controls, and retention deletion hooks. also, where are logs stored and for how long?
26:30 - Diego Morales: Redwood Console has retention tiers; logs can be forwarded to your SIEM. for private installs we can provide longer retention locally. I'll include retention matrix in the security packet.

28:00 - Maya Singh: let's talk about measurement of model quality in the POC — we need to ensure no regression post-quantization.
28:12 - Diego Morales: we can include automated evals as part of the benchmark runs — run the quantized model and compute extraction metrics on holdout sets. if drift > threshold we fail the quantized rollout.

30:00 - Ava Chen: ok, cost modeling specifics: we'll run a per-1k-token cost estimate and a committed capacity amortized cost for dedicated. i'll attach a worked example.
30:20 - Samir Patel: can you include amortization of engineering time to maintain private? we want to see all-in TCO.
30:28 - Ava Chen: yes — we'll provide a worksheet with infra, ops, and license costs amortized over pilot.

32:00 - Tom Reynolds: networking detail — do you require inbound peering, or can you use a secure relay? we need to minimize our attack surface.
32:12 - Diego Morales: two options: VPC peering or egress-only relay via TLS tunnel. private deployments can be fully inside your VPC if you choose on-prem or VPC-private. we'll include both patterns.

34:00 - Maya Singh: benchmark metrics deliverable should include CSV of raw latency samples so our infra team can validate.
34:12 - Diego Morales: yep — raw logs, aggregated metrics, GPU traces, and annotated runbook.

36:00 - Samir Patel: decision criteria recap: accuracy, latency, throughput, security posture, and cost. are we aligned to choose hosting mode based on which knobs we value most?
36:18 - Ava Chen: exactly — hosted if time-to-value + low engineering ops is priority; dedicated if predictable throughput + lower unit cost; private if strict data residency/BYOK required.

38:00 - Ellen Park: one more security nuance — we need proof that KV cache can be scoped such that sensitive prefixes never cache. can that be enforced?
38:12 - Diego Morales: yes, we can disable prefix/KV caching per-route or per-customer. some transcripts call it K-V cash which is amusing, but i promise it's configurable.

40:00 - Tom Reynolds: what's the rollback plan if a model update regresses production?
40:10 - Diego Morales: traffic split and automatic rollback; we keep previous model in warm cache to resume quickly. for critical customers we recommend canary + manual approval.

42:00 - Maya Singh: for long-run jobs that take minutes, how do you surface progress and logs?
42:10 - Diego Morales: we stream incremental tokens in interactive mode and for async batch we provide job status endpoints and logs in console. logs can be forwarded to your observability stack.

44:00 - Ava Chen: ok let's set concrete dates: Diego will run the hosted baseline starting 2027-09-12 for 3 days, then dedicated runs 2027-09-19 to 2027-09-22. decision checkpoint 2027-09-29. does that work?
44:18 - Maya Singh: yes, we'll make traces available by 2027-09-10.
44:25 - Ellen Park: i'll review SOC 2 docs by 2027-09-14 and flag gaps.

46:00 - Diego Morales: i'll send a runbook and list of credentials / network items needed. note: private run will need firewall rules earlier.

48:00 - Samir Patel: any competitors we should be wary of technically? we looked at Anthropic's hosted and some teams evaluating Vertex for custom infra.
48:12 - Ava Chen: we also get compared to open-source self-hosted stacks; our differentiation is managed runtime + rollout tooling. sometimes the auto-transcript flips Vertex to 'vertexe' — ignore that.

50:00 - Tom Reynolds: sign-offs — who needs to approve pilot?
50:08 - Samir Patel: product + infra + security signoff. CFO will sign for cost.

52:00 - Ava Chen: recap (quick): 1) Diego runs hosted baseline 9/12, dedicated runs 9/19–9/22; 2) Maya provides traces by 9/10; 3) Ava sends pricing + security packet by 9/10; 4) decision checkpoint 9/29. I've added the action items. Anything to add?
52:30 - Ellen Park: please include exact retention windows and a sample network diagram.
52:40 - Ava Chen: will do. thanks everyone, we'll follow up via email and i'll create a calendar for the benchmark kickoff.
52:50 - Maya Singh: thanks all.
52:55 - Diego Morales: thanks, bye.

Transcription notes: occasional mis-hearings (BYOK referenced as 'bye ok', KV cache sometimes 'K V cash'), filler words present, some speaker labels collapsed in auto captions.

References mentioned: hosting cost comparison (link to be sent), Redwood security packet (link), benchmark runbook (to be sent by Diego).

title: POC workload delta, concurrency & token dynamics scoping - Veritide
preview: Discovery/POC scoping focused on characterizing Veritide's QPS bursts, concurrency distribution, and input/output token mix. Discussed goals for tail-latency SLOs, benchmark methodology (replay vs synthetic), required artifacts, and timeline for a 3-week pilot. Key concerns: data residency, on-prem option for regulated workloads, and ability to reproduce production burst patterns for kernel-level profiling.
text: Discovery/POC scoping focused on characterizing Veritide's QPS bursts, concurrency distribution, and input/output token mix. Discussed goals for tail-latency SLOs, benchmark methodology (replay vs synthetic), required artifacts, and timeline for a 3-week pilot. Key concerns: data residency, on-prem option for regulated workloads, and ability to reproduce production burst patterns for kernel-level profiling.

title: Runtime batching + prefetch playbook demo with Luxora Labs
preview: Redwood Optimize console demo focused on runtime batching and prefix/KV prefetch strategies. SE ran three scenarios (low, medium, high concurrency) and showed estimated token savings, latency impact, and cost delta. Customer interested in a 2-week pilot to validate savings on Luxora sessionized chat product; security and KMS integration are gating items.

title: Optimize walkthrough - quant strategy showcase (Horizon Health)
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-07-08
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood SE), Priya Rao (Horizon Health), Marcus Lee (Horizon Infra), Samantha Ortiz (Product)

Summary:
Quick Optimize console walkthrough focused on quantization profiles (INT8 vs FP8), runbook for profile selection, and how to balance throughput/latency with clinical fidelity for Horizon Health's triage & summarization workloads. Customer wants an action plan and a short pilot to validate latency & QA metrics.

Topics covered:
- baseline profiling and what data we need
- INT8 sensitivity vs FP8 for token-level hallucination risk
- fallback rules in Optimize (when to switch to FP8 or model variant)
- console demo: profile editor, heatmaps, per-prompt quality delta
- security/hosting notes for PII in health prompts

Notes / Shared links:
- Sandbox link: https://app.redwood.ai/sandbox/horizon-health (Redwood will grant access)
- Optimize runbook: https://redwood.ai/docs/optimize/qprofiles (shared during call)
- Benchmark template (CSV) referenced: "Horizon-benchmark-template.csv" (Ava to attach)

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava: Hey everyone thanks for joining, we're excited to walk through the Optimize console and qprofiles today, we built this specifically to help teams like Horizon trim cost without losing clinical fidelity.
[00:18] Priya: Thanks Ava, quick context — we're running a triage/summarization stack, about 200k requests/day right now, peak latency target 600ms tail, and accuracy is basically patient safety first, cost second.
[00:35] Diego: Got it. Before we jump in, a couple clarifying Qs — are those requests mostly short prompts or longer context windows? That impacts quant choices.
[00:45] Marcus: Mostly multi-turn, average 512 tokens, some 1k for long case summaries. We also have strict data residency and SSO requirements.
[01:03] Ava: Perfect, we'll flag the VPC/dedicated notes later. Today we'll focus on INT8 vs FP8 tradeoffs; Diego will demo how the console highlights sensitivity per prompt and how to author fallback rules.
[01:22] Speaker 1 (background): mhm
[01:30] Diego: Pulling up the Optimize console — okay, here's the qprofile editor. On the left you see the profile selector: default, conservative, aggressive. Each row is a kernel/precision pair.
[01:45] Priya: Quick question — we've heard FP8 is newer and sometimes better at preserving subtlety, is that what you see?
[01:54] Diego: Yes, FP8 tends to keep distribution of logits closer to FP16, so for tasks sensitive to small logit differences — like negation or numeric accuracy — FP8 often beats INT8. But INT8 can be significantly faster and cheaper on certain GPUs.
[02:12] Ava: Also note that our runtime supports automatic prefix caching and KV cache reuse, which shifts the cost math — if you have a lot of repeated context, INT8 speedup compounds.
[02:27] Marcus: Are there models you recommend for INT8-first strategies?
[02:33] Diego: Short answer: depends on architecture. For some open LLMs we onboarded, INT8 with a conservative per-layer layout works well. We'll run a profile sweep on your actual model and prompts to recommend a default.
[02:45] Priya: What's the sensitivity surface look like? Can we get a heatmap by prompt?
[02:52] Diego: Yes — in the console 'Quality delta' heatmap shows per-prompt change vs baseline FP16. You can filter by length, intent, and error type.
[03:05] Samantha: Nice, can it show hallucination vs formatting errors?
[03:10] Diego: We tag common error classes via our eval harness, so yes — you'll see categories. It's not perfect automatic classification, sometimes it mistakes OOV names, there's noise.
[03:25] Ava: We do recommend a small human eval on samples flagged high-risk.
[03:32] [04:00] Diego: I'm switching to the demo sandbox run we did with a similar EHR workload — here's an INT8 profile applied, latency down ~40% on p95, cost down 30%, but the quality delta for clinical question answering is +2.7% wrong answers on our labeled set.
[04:20] Priya: That delta is concerning. How do you mitigate that?
[04:26] Diego: Two patterns: 1) hybrid profiles — INT8 for most layers but FP8 for attention or specific MLP layers; 2) fallback rules — if the confidence metric or QA score drops under threshold, route to FP8 or a higher-capacity model.
[04:45] Marcus: Does that routing add latency overhead?
[04:49] Diego: Minimal if you do prefix/kv-aware routing — initial attempt may be slightly slower for that single request, but overall SLOs stay intact because only a small percentage escalate.
[05:02] [05:30] Ava: Let me show the rule editor — you can create a rule: "if prompt contains 'acute' OR intent==diagnosis AND quality_score<0.92 -> route FP8/model-x". You can test it in-console with canned prompts.
[05:55] Priya: Can we run a canary on a subset of traffic? We prefer to pilot on 5% first.
[06:03] Diego: Absolutely, rollout is built-in. You can weight by route, by user segment, or by percentage. Logs show token-level diffs for failed cases.
[06:18] [06:50] Marcus: About the security stuff — for clinical logs, we need KMS and audit logs, does Optimize change anything?
[06:58] Ava: No differences. For Private/Dedicated customers we integrate with your KMS/HSM, and all audit logs are forwarded to your SIEM. We'll share the SOC2 + ISO docs after the call.
[07:12] Samantha: If we do Dedicated, do we still get Optimize console features?
[07:18] Diego: Yes — console + Optimize are available in Dedicated and Private; difference is control plane location and model artifact placement.
[07:30] [08:00] Diego: Back to profiles — here's an example of a hybrid profile we use in clinical pilots: INT8 on early transformer blocks, FP8 on last 3 layers + attention. That preserves final token distribution and reduces hallucination risk.
[08:20] Priya: How do you pick which layers to keep at higher precision?
[08:25] Diego: We run a layer-sensitivity sweep — incremental quantize of each layer and measure QA drop. The console visualizes per-layer sensitivity so you can craft an optimal profile without guessing.
[08:40] [09:05] Speaker 2 (background): yeah
[09:10] Diego: Also, if you have stricter latency P95 targets, we can bias profiles to speed — e.g., INT8 across the board and add a strict fallback for any low-confidence outputs.
[09:24] Samantha: Is there an ROI calculator?
[09:28] Ava: Yes, Optimize has a cost simulator. Enter your qprofile, traffic volume, token counts and it projects unit cost and monthly savings. We'll run this for Horizon's numbers in the POC.
[09:44] Priya: Walk me through what you need from us to start the POC.
[09:50] Ava: Three things: 1) 1-2k anonymized prompts representative of triage and summarization tasks, 2) baseline labels or success criteria (what constitutes a correct summary), and 3) decision on infrastructure (Hosted vs Dedicated pilot).
[10:04] Priya: We can share prompts, may take legal sign-off on data extraction. Marcus will help coordinate.
[10:12] Marcus: Yep, we'll produce an anonymized dump. Timeline: ~by July 12.
[10:18] Ava: Great. Diego — show the per-prompt diff for one sample?
[10:22] Diego: Sure — here's a sample patient-summarization prompt in the console test harness. Baseline FP16 answer and INT8 answer side-by-side. You can see difference: INT8 missed a negation in medication history. That's classic small-logit sensitivity.
[10:38] Priya: So we should prioritize negation-heavy prompts for human review.
[10:42] Diego: Exactly. And the console can tag those automatically if they contain certain patterns. It's probabilistic though — sometimes it flags benign variations.
[10:56] [11:30] Ava: We'll attach a short guide on how to assemble the sample set with de-identification suggestions.
[11:37] Samantha: For the POC run, can we test both INT8-first and FP8-first strategies and compare?
[11:44] Diego: Yep. The sandbox supports parallel runs and the Optimize report will give you per-strategy deltas, cost sims, and per-error-class breakdown.
[12:00] Priya: Curious on hardware sensitivity — if we move to our on-prem fleet later, will these qprofiles port?
[12:08] Diego: They should, but kernel availability varies. Our compilation layer selects optimized kernels — sometimes INT8 speedups are bigger on A100 vs newer accelerators, and FP8 runtime support is still catching up on some older infra. We'll note compatibility in the profile export.
[12:25] Marcus: So we'll want a compatibility matrix.
[12:28] Ava: We'll include that — we'll map profiles to known hardware families.
[12:33] [13:00] Diego: Another trick — confidence calibration. Even with INT8, we can calibrate the model's confidence estimator and use that as a cheap signal to trigger fallback instead of re-running expensive full-checks.
[13:17] Priya: What's the false positive rate on that approach?
[13:22] Diego: In our pilots it's ~3-6% extra fallbacks, but it prevents most critical misses. We'll show numbers from prior health pilots in the POC report.
[13:35] [14:00] Ava: Quick demo — creating a canary rule and pushing to 5% traffic. (typing) There, you can see traffic distribution and live logs.
[14:18] Samantha: The UI looks good, can we change JSON config for infra-as-code?
[14:24] Diego: Yes, every profile and rule has an API and CLI export, so you can check these into your infra repo.
[14:34] Marcus: How do we audit changes? Need CSOps audit trail for compliance.
[14:39] Ava: All changes are logged and tied to SSO identity; we also support webhooks to your audit pipeline.
[14:46] [15:10] Priya: Any risks we should be aware of?
[15:15] Diego: Main risk is underestimating distribution shift — if production prompts drift significantly from your sample set, profiles can break. So continuous monitoring with small human-in-the-loop checks at rollout is recommended.
[15:29] Samantha: How often do customers re-run sensitivity sweeps?
[15:33] Diego: Typically weekly during the POC, then monthly in steady-state or whenever you push a new prompt set or model version.
[15:41] [16:00] Ava: OK let's talk timelines: if you share prompts by July 12, Diego can run sweeps and have initial INT8/FP8 candidate profiles and a first report by July 15-16.
[16:12] Priya: That works. We'll also need pricing for monthly Dedicated vs Hosted Optimize add-on.
[16:20] Ava: I'll send pricing tiers today. Note Dedicated has a minimum commit but gives lower per-token costs for heavy throughput.
[16:31] Marcus: Are there any licensing constraints on model artifacts? If we use open models and quantize them, can we keep those artifacts on-prem?
[16:40] Diego: Yes, for Private/Dedicated you can host model artifacts in your VPC/on-prem. Our onboarding process includes an artifact transfer guide and license checklist.
[16:52] [17:30] Priya: How do you report regression alerts? We want automated alerts if a metric drops beyond a threshold.
[17:37] Diego: Optimize includes quality monitors and regression alerts tied to prompt sets; you define thresholds and alert targets (Slack, PagerDuty, email).
[17:49] Ava: We'll add a sample alerting policy for clinical severity levels.
[17:55] [19:00] Diego: Demo wrap-up — here's the export of the baseline run (CSV). It contains per-prompt FP16 vs INT8 diffs, QA flags, latency p50/p95. We'll attach this to the follow-up.
[19:18] Priya: Great. Any competitor differences we should consider?
[19:22] Ava: Compared to managed APIs like OpenAI, Redwood gives you more control over profiling and fine-grained fallback policies and better observability into token-level diffs. Versus Anthropic, feature parity depends on model family, but our focus is serving economics and safety controls.
[19:41] Marcus: Noted.
[19:46] [21:00] Samantha: Last question — do you support model surgery or layer remapping for stubborn cases?
[21:06] Diego: We don't alter model weights in the platform, but we support per-layer quantization overrides and some compensated kernels. For deeper surgery we'd work with you via Professional Services.
[21:21] Priya: OK, that's fine.
[21:26] Ava: Recap of next steps: Redwood to grant sandbox access, Priya/Marcus to share anonymized prompts by July 12, Diego to run sweeps and deliver an INT8/FP8 candidate report by July 15, Ava to send pricing + SOC2 docs by EOD tomorrow.
[21:48] Priya: Sounds good.
[21:51] Marcus: We'll confirm calendar for the follow-up.
[21:55] Ava: Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the attachments.
[22:02] Diego: Thanks, bye.

(End of recording)
