title: Sparrow Analytics migration & parity planning with Redwood
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2027-03-22 15:00 PT
Duration: ~47 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Diaz (Redwood SE), Sonia Patel (Redwood SolutionsEng - Optimize), Liam O'Connor (CTO, Sparrow), Priya Sharma (ML Eng), Carlos Mendes (Infra), Tasha Nguyen (PM)

Summary:
Quick migration walkthrough for Sparrow's production workloads (currently on OpenAI + Together); focus on parity mapping for prompts, cost SLOs, and console-driven rollout with Optimize recommendations. Discussed hosted vs dedicated vs private (VPC) tradeoffs and initial pilot plan.

Topics covered:
- Redwood API parity: request/response shape, streaming, function-calling mapping
- Console demo: route configs, rollout policies, canary, A/B, metrics
- Optimize: batching/quantization recommendations for Sparrow workloads
- Security/compliance: SOC 2, SSO, KMS, audit logs, data residency
- Migration plan: test corpus, parity run, fallback routing, canary timeline

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call?
[00:06] Liam O'Connor: Hey Ava, Liam here, Priya, Carlos and Tasha on my side.
[00:09] Priya Sharma: Hi, Priya.
[00:11] Marco Diaz: Marco from Redwood - hi all.
[00:13] Sonia Patel: Sonia here - I'll cover Optimize recs.
[00:17] Ava Chen: Cool, so goal today is a migration and parity planning session. We'll show the Console, talk through the API differences from OpenAI/Anthropic/Together, and sketch a pilot.
[00:28] Tasha Nguyen: Sounds good. We're most worried about subtle parity — like tokenization, function call deterministic outputs for our summarization pipeline. Also latency spikes.
[00:36] Priya Sharma: And cost. Our current spend is concentrated on OpenAI streaming endpoints and a Together-fine tuned model for some routing.
[00:44] Marco Diaz: Great, we'll map those pieces. First quick note: Redwood supports the streaming semantics and function/tool calling — the surface is similar but there are a few naming differences and some behavior nuances, especially around partial output flushing and stop-token handling. We'll demo.
[01:05] Liam O'Connor: Cool — any gotchas with K-V cache or prefix caching? We rely on a reuse cache to cut tokens in half for chat style sessions.
[01:13] Marco Diaz: We have KV cache, yes — it's automatic for session-based inferences, and Console exposes cache hit metrics per route. In some early transcriptions it shows up as kay-vee cache, but it's the same concept. For your workload, Optimize usually suggests prefix caching combined with small-time-to-live to avoid staleness.
[01:35] Priya Sharma: OK. How do you measure parity? We need determinism for our regression suite.
[01:40] Ava Chen: We usually do a parity run: you provide a test set, we run it against your current stack and Redwood's recommended model/config. We'll capture token-level diffs, latency percentiles, and quality deltas.
[01:51] Carlos Mendes: If we go private (VPC), will the audit logs still be accessible? We need to tie into Splunk.
[01:58] Sonia Patel: Yes — Private installations stream audit logs to your SIEM. Hosted gives you long retention in Console and an export for compliance. We'll send the SOC2 pack shortly.
[02:12] Ava Chen: Marco, let's pop into the Console to show route configs and the canary flow.
[02:18] [screen share starts - Console UI noise]
[02:22] Marco Diaz: Here is the route list. You can create a route and pin a model version, set rollout percent, and attach Optimize profiles. For Sparrow, I'd recommend a canary of 5-10% initially with traffic mirroring to get a baseline.
[02:40] Tasha Nguyen: Mirroring would be huge — we want to compare outputs without impacting users. How deep is the mirroring? Do you support full kv-state mirroring?
[02:50] Marco Diaz: We mirror request payloads and can capture KV cache state for sessioned calls; there are caveats on PII — we redact per your config.
[02:58] Priya Sharma: Streaming behavior — in OpenAI we get token chunks quickly and we rely on that. Any latency delta?
[03:05] Marco Diaz: On our hosted API median is low single-digit 100s ms for typical chat sequences; we showed your SLO target of 250ms P95 earlier in your onboarding form, and our dedicated pools can meet that consistently. There can be variance during cold starts if cache warm-up is needed. For streaming we flush partial tokens based on configured thresholds.
[03:29] Liam O'Connor: Can you replicate Together replicable outputs? Our fine-tuned Together models have deterministic seeds.
[03:36] Marco Diaz: Determinism is tricky across model families; we can pin RNG seeds and run exact temperature/beam settings, but model architecture differences may still change output. For that case we recommend a parity tolerance matrix — exact match vs semantic equivalence. We'll help set evaluation metrics.
[03:58] Priya Sharma: About costs — what levers do we have? Quantization, batching?
[04:03] Sonia Patel: Optimize will generate recommendations: switching to 4-bit quantized variants for non-critical outputs, setting batching windows for throughput jobs, and using prefix caching for interactive routes. We estimate 30-60% token-cost reduction for some workloads, but we need your profile.
[04:20] Carlos Mendes: If we want a hybrid — hosted for web, private for PHI processing — how's that configured?
[04:26] Ava Chen: You can split by policy: routes in Console route by region, compliance labels, or cost. Private deployments can host sensitive routes in your VPC and hosted handles public traffic; Smart routing does fallback if private capacity is exhausted.
[04:43] Tasha Nguyen: Nice. What's the onboarding timeline for a pilot? Two weeks?
[04:47] Marco Diaz: Typical pilot (parity run + basic canary) is 1-2 weeks after we get your test corpus and allowlist. A full production rollout usually takes longer for dedicated capacity or private installs due to network setup.
[04:59] Liam O'Connor: OK — show us an example of function calling mapping; we use tools heavily to call our DB/ops endpoint.
[05:06] Marco Diaz: Sure - here's an example response mapping. (reads) The schema-based function-call is compatible; the difference is Redwood uses explicit function metadata in the route config and supports auto-fallback if the function times out. Sometimes the transcriber mislabels this as function/calling versus tool-call but it's the same idea.
[05:30] Priya Sharma: Any changes to authentication? We're on SSO with SCIM provisioning.
[05:34] Ava Chen: We support SAML/SCIM, OIDC, RBAC; Console integrates with your IdP and you can map groups to roles. For private deployments we can sync via SCIM but you manage the IdP side.
[05:47] Carlos Mendes: KMS integration? We need customer-managed keys.
[05:50] Marco Diaz: Yes, KMS/HSM supported. For hosted we offer envelope encryption with CMKs in your cloud account or a managed option. For private, keys stay in your vault. We'll include architecture notes in the security pack.
[06:05] Tasha Nguyen: Great. Can we run a small parity bench now? Like 50 prompts?
[06:10] Marco Diaz: We can run an ad-hoc demo now against a sample set, but it won't be isolated from production metrics. Better to do a controlled run. If you have a small sample uploaded now we can run a quick compare.
[06:22] Priya Sharma: I can paste 20 quick prompts in chat, but they include internal names — might be redaction concerns.
[06:30] Ava Chen: If you paste them here we'll redact in transit; still prefer upload to shared drive.
[06:35] [brief pause — chat messages exchanged]
[06:50] Liam O'Connor: We'll upload a sanitized set after this meeting.
[06:54] Sonia Patel: On Optimize side: we'll run simulations on your traffic profile and recommend batching window sizes and quantization profiles. For example, for long-form summarization we often use dynamic batching at 40-80ms window and 4-bit quantized model variants for cost savings.
[07:13] Priya Sharma: Our summarizer has a tight latency SLO for interactive users — can't trade too much latency for cost.
[07:18] Sonia Patel: Understood — Optimize will produce multiple profiles: latency-first, cost-first, balanced. You can map them to routes in Console and do A/B tests.
[07:31] Marco Diaz: One more note on fallbacks: you can configure fallback to an open, cheaper model when a premium model is under pressure. Also region fallback if a region hits capacity. We can instrument per-route cost buckets so you see unit economics.
[07:48] Liam O'Connor: That addresses our concern of outage graceful degradation.
[07:51] Tasha Nguyen: Great. Let's recap next steps.
[07:54] Ava Chen: Recap: 1) Sparrow to upload sanitized prompt set + traffic pattern by 2027-03-26. 2) Redwood will share SOC2/security pack by 2027-03-25. 3) Marco will provision parity cluster and propose 2-hour parity window for week of 2027-03-29. 4) We'll schedule a canary plan and share Console link.
[08:16] Liam O'Connor: That works. Any blockers on our end? Carlos?
[08:19] Carlos Mendes: We'll confirm VPC peering and IP allowlist by Friday.
[08:22] Priya Sharma: We'll prepare the prompt set.
[08:24] Tasha Nguyen: I'll coordinate calendar invites.
[08:26] Marco Diaz: I'll also attach a short checklist for the parity run (tokenization settings, temperature, stop tokens, function-calls schema).
[08:33] Ava Chen: Perfect. Thanks everyone. We'll follow up in email with links and action items.
[08:38] [meeting ends] 

Notes about transcription: occasional mislabels — e.g., speaker indices show as Speaker 2 in the chat export; some product names truncated. Shared artifacts referenced: https://redwood.example.com/decks/sparrow-onramp (deck), https://drive.example.com/sparrow/parity-prompts (placeholder), and parity-benchsheet.xlsx (spreadsheet).

End of transcript.

title: Cedar Edge - Growth & Regional Provisioning Review
preview: Quarterly renewal/expansion checkpoint. Customer confirmed growth trajectory (2.5x tokens next 12 months), wants to add EU (Frankfurt) and APAC (Singapore) regions, prefers a reserved capacity + burst mix. Procurement timeline for contract extension targeting April PO. Key blockers: SOC2 evidence, KMS integration details, and latency SLOs for EU traffic. Agreed next steps and technical validation plan.

title: Launch communication drill and late-stage risk triage
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-08
Start: 10:00 UTC | Duration: ~78 minutes
Attendees: Ava, Mateo, Rajat, Maya, Priya, Ben, Sophie, Connor, Ellen, Diego

Summary:
Quick launch communication drill focused on who speaks to customers during degradations, last-mile runbook edits, and triage of three late-stage risks: kernel fallback latency spike, KV warming regression, and outage communications cadence. Ran through a simulated degraded scenario and validated handoffs between SEs, release, and ops.

Topics covered:
- Communication tree and customer bulleting cadence
- Live-run simulation results for canary 2
- Risk #1: kernel fallback causing 2x tail latency at 95th pct
- Risk #2: cold-start KV cache warming causing token-cost spike
- Risk #3: SSO + SAML flakiness on private deployments during region failover
- Rollback vs degrade decisions and automated fallback triggers

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava Chen: okay, let's get started. thanks everyone for joining the drill. the goal is to exercise the comms path and late stage triage so we all know who says what if we see a tail-latency incident during launch. priya, want to kick with the comms tree?
[00:18] Priya Nair: yeah, sure. so top-level, we have a Tier 0 pager for P0s. on the customer side we notify account owners within 5 minutes, public status page after 15, targeted emails for impacted customers after 30 minutes, and then a follow-up within 24 hours. page rotation is the same as normal oncall schedule but we add an extra release lead for the first 72 hours.
[00:38] Ben Howard: small note — add a single designated AE point to avoid multiple outreach. we had conflicting messages last cycle, customers were confused.
[00:46] Ava Chen: agreed, that was ugly. let's say Connor is the AE liaison for this launch — he'll clear external wording with me and Priya before it goes out.
[00:56] Connor Patel: i'll do that, happy to.
[00:58] Mateo Ruiz: ok, simulated failure we ran earlier — canary 2 saw a fallback to the quantized kernel at about 11:43 PST, and we observed a trailing tail from 200 ms to ~450 ms p95 but p99 spiked to around 1.2s on longer contexts. i have logs, but quick primer: kernel selection logic overreacted to transient memory pressure.
[01:24] Rajat Singh: memory pressure as in OOM throttles or just scheduler backpressure?
[01:28] Mateo Ruiz: scheduler backpressure plus a small gc on the runtime that freed addresses slower, and the fallback path uses a different attention kernel that's single-threaded for short batches.
[01:40] Maya Torres: that rings a bell — we merged the kernel fallback last sprint to address a rare oom. the test harness didn't exercise long context tails.
[01:48] Ellen Zhao: from security — i want to call out that during the fallback we also saw an extra auth check failing for SAML assertions on private nodes, likely a timing edge. not necessarily exploitable but makes customer-facing messaging tricky.
[02:02] Diego Marquez: that's the private cluster peering case we worried about; region failover added a slight clock skew and the SAML validation window is short.
[02:12] Ava Chen: okay, so three buckets: performance tail from kernel fallback, KV-cache cold-start cost increase, and SAML timing issues. let's walk the simulated script. priya you will role-play comms?
[02:22] Priya Nair: yes. simulated scenario: canary latency degradation at T0, engineering determines model fallback is happening, decide whether to degrade to smaller model or roll back the kernel change. we need to exercise decision trees.
[02:34] Ben Howard: i triggered the sim at 11:43 in the test cluster. Mateo, you have logs?
[02:40] Mateo Ruiz: sending link now — scoreboard.log and kernel-monitor.csv (link: internal:/canary2/sim-2026-12-08). note the spike aligns with concurrent large-context requests.
[02:54] [crosstalk, someone coughs] 
[03:00] Sophie Li: quick question: what are our SLO thresholds that trip an automated rollback vs manual intervention? do we have a firm number?
[03:07] Rajat Singh: current rollout config uses a p95 latency threshold of 300ms and a p99 threshold of 900ms for automated rollback on canary. but those were tuned for smaller contexts. for long contexts the p95 baseline is already higher.
[03:22] Mateo Ruiz: right — and the automatic policy didn't account for sequence-length distribution shift. the canary had an unusual batch of long prompts coming through which skewed metrics.
[03:30] Ava Chen: so we've got a false positive risk. priya, what's the decision tree if automatic rollback fires erroneously?
[03:38] Priya Nair: fallback: if auto rollback triggers, oncall gets paged and release lead decides within 10 minutes to confirm rollback. we also have a temp-degrade option in the routing policy to shift some percent of traffic to a smaller model instead of rolling back. that's less disruptive to releases but may change cost and output.
[03:52] Ben Howard: we practiced the temp-degrade yesterday; it took about 6 minutes to shift policy and 18 minutes for key caches to warm, so customers experienced a short period of elevated cost.
[04:04] Ava Chen: note for comms: we need to call out likely impact — short spike in latency, potential higher token costs for some customers, and a follow-up with more detailed post-mortem. keep the message short and avoid technical blame.
[04:18] Ellen Zhao: and include that SSO flakiness was observed but not confirmed as exploit. re security, we'll write an appendix explaining mitigations and the timeline for SAML patching.
[04:30] Diego Marquez: for private deployments, customers may need to restart the auth adapter if the clock skew hits them. include the restart instructions in the runbook.
[04:40] Priya Nair: action: priya will add that instruction to runbook; ben will write the one-liner for the customer bulletin.
[04:48] [keyboard tap noises] 
[05:00] Mateo Ruiz: returning to the KV cache warming issue — we saw a token-cost spike when cold nodes served requests; cost per token went up because requests fell back to longer compute path. the autoscaler later spun up, but the initial 12 minutes matter.
[05:18] Rajat Singh: can we warm caches faster? maybe prefetch the top prefixes for high-traffic tenants during rollout?
[05:24] Mateo Ruiz: yes, we can push a quick warm-up hook in the rollout plan to prepopulate KV for the top 50 tenants. script exists but needs integration with the release pipeline.
[05:35] Maya Torres: we should also throttle the canary traffic by certificate in the LB and make sure the initial burst doesn't trigger autoscaler oscillations.
[05:44] Priya Nair: ok prioritization: 1) add canary pre-warm hook, 2) adjust automatic SLO thresholds to be sequence-length-aware, 3) update comms templates to avoid confusing technical detail.
[05:56] Ava Chen: timeline on those?
[06:00] Mateo Ruiz: pre-warm hook — i can do a PR today and request merge tomorrow; integration might take until friday.
[06:06] Rajat Singh: SLO tuning — i'll follow up with product for sequence-length signals and we can deploy a metric adjustment by 2026-12-11.
[06:14] Ellen Zhao: i'll produce the security appendix tonight.
[06:18] Priya Nair: comms templates — i'll draft versions for P0 and P1 with one-line, three-line and full postmortem slots by EOD tomorrow.
[06:26] [20 minute simulation jump — team runs through scenario offline] 
[26:40] Ava Chen: back on the call. the simulation highlighted a confusing handoff between SE and release lead. specifically, the AE got customer questions before the release lead had a decision. we should formalize the pre-approval phrase.
[26:56] Diego Marquez: propose a canonical sentence: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." short, non-technical.
[27:12] Ava Chen: looks good. Mateo, if engineering confirms rollback, what's the expected customer-visible delta and how quickly can you revert?
[27:20] Mateo Ruiz: a rollback to previous kernel variant typically takes 4-7 minutes to complete on the control plane and another 8-12 for caches to stabilize under normal load. for long contexts, p99 may still be elevated until caches are fully warmed.
[27:34] Rajat Singh: important callout — a rollback also means we lose the small memory-safety gain from the new fallback; that risk is low for now but note it in the decision log.
[27:44] Ben Howard: who owns the decision log entry?
[27:46] Priya Nair: release lead — priya will own the decision log and commit it to the launch ops channel.
[27:52] Ava Chen: good. now let's role-play the AE message to customers. priya, can you read the short and the extended?
[28:00] Priya Nair: short: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." extended: "We observed increased latency for longer-context requests tied to a kernel fallback path exercised on canary nodes during rollout. We have mitigations in flight and may roll back the recent kernel change if the regression persists. Impact is localized to longer contexts and a subset of tenants; we'll provide timelines as they become available and a postmortem within 24 hours."
[28:34] Ava Chen: that's good. Diego — add a line for customers on dedicated/private setups about restarting the auth adapter if they see SAML errors.
[28:44] Diego Marquez: done. priya will include in the template.
[28:48] [brief silence] 
[29:00] Sophie Li: i want to flag downstream product expectations — some customers embed long prompts as single messages; this launch has a higher proportion of those users. should we ramp slower for those accounts?
[29:12] Ava Chen: yes, we can opt those accounts out of fast ramp and move them through a manual staging cadence. we need to identify top 30 accounts and mark them in the rollout dashboard.
[29:24] Mateo Ruiz: also reminder that the fallback kernel is single-threaded for small batches; for customers with bursty single requests it will hurt more. we can set per-account policy to avoid fallback for high-value tenants.
[29:36] Rajat Singh: let's not make the per-account policy too heavy — prefer a small set of opt-outs.
[29:42] Priya Nair: action: priya + sophie to create the opt-out list and strategy; due 2026-12-09.
[29:50] [keyboard clicking] 
[30:00] Ben Howard: incident comms cadence rehearsal — who reads from the playbook?
[30:04] Priya Nair: i'll read.
[30:06] Ben Howard: ok, then we simulate a follow up 30 minutes later where engineering confirms rollback. priya, craft the 30-minute update.
[30:14] Priya Nair: 30-minute update: "Engineering identified kernel fallback as the likely cause and has initiated a rollback on canary. We expect reduced latency within 15 minutes; further updates at 60 minutes. No evidence of customer data impact. Security is investigating a parallel SAML timing anomaly for private deployments."
[30:32] Ava Chen: perfect. make sure the language says "no evidence" not "no impact" to avoid definitive absolutes.
[30:38] Ellen Zhao: noted.
[30:40] [brief overlap, someone says "mm-hmm"]
[30:44] Mateo Ruiz: technical note — post-rollback we should run a focused long-context load test to validate caches warmed and p99 is back under 900ms. can we agree on a test window?
[30:54] Rajat Singh: reserve 45 minutes after rollback to run the validation. ben, please coordinate the window.
[30:58] Ben Howard: will do.
[31:00] [simulation end]

[31:20] Priya Nair: none.

[31:36] Mateo Ruiz: yes.

[31:44] Rajat Singh: agreed.

[31:52] Ben Howard: sounds good.

[32:14] Priya Nair: great. Ava, last word?
[32:18] Ava Chen: thanks everyone. two quick asks: one, keep comms concise and finalize templates by tomorrow; two, run the synthetic canary at 0600 PST and share the metric snapshot in slack. we want to catch regressions before the customer wave.
[32:30] Mateo Ruiz: i'll schedule the synthetic.
[32:32] Ava Chen: ok wrap up. next sync Monday if anything else emerges.

text:

Attendees: Ava, Mateo, Rajat, Maya, Priya, Ben, Sophie, Connor, Ellen, Diego

Summary:
Quick launch communication drill focused on who speaks to customers during degradations, last-mile runbook edits, and triage of three late-stage risks: kernel fallback latency spike, KV warming regression, and outage communications cadence. Ran through a simulated degraded scenario and validated handoffs between SEs, release, and ops.

Topics covered:
- Communication tree and customer bulleting cadence
- Live-run simulation results for canary 2
- Risk #1: kernel fallback causing 2x tail latency at 95th pct
- Risk #2: cold-start KV cache warming causing token-cost spike
- Risk #3: SSO + SAML flakiness on private deployments during region failover
- Rollback vs degrade decisions and automated fallback triggers

[00:00] Ava Chen: okay, let's get started. thanks everyone for joining the drill. the goal is to exercise the comms path and late stage triage so we all know who says what if we see a tail-latency incident during launch. priya, want to kick with the comms tree?
[00:18] Priya Nair: yeah, sure. so top-level, we have a Tier 0 pager for P0s. on the customer side we notify account owners within 5 minutes, public status page after 15, targeted emails for impacted customers after 30 minutes, and then a follow-up within 24 hours. page rotation is the same as normal oncall schedule but we add an extra release lead for the first 72 hours.
[00:38] Ben Howard: small note — add a single designated AE point to avoid multiple outreach. we had conflicting messages last cycle, customers were confused.
[00:46] Ava Chen: agreed, that was ugly. let's say Connor is the AE liaison for this launch — he'll clear external wording with me and Priya before it goes out.
[00:56] Connor Patel: i'll do that, happy to.
[00:58] Mateo Ruiz: ok, simulated failure we ran earlier — canary 2 saw a fallback to the quantized kernel at about 11:43 PST, and we observed a trailing tail from 200 ms to ~450 ms p95 but p99 spiked to around 1.2s on longer contexts. i have logs, but quick primer: kernel selection logic overreacted to transient memory pressure.
[01:24] Rajat Singh: memory pressure as in OOM throttles or just scheduler backpressure?
[01:28] Mateo Ruiz: scheduler backpressure plus a small gc on the runtime that freed addresses slower, and the fallback path uses a different attention kernel that's single-threaded for short batches.
[01:40] Maya Torres: that rings a bell — we merged the kernel fallback last sprint to address a rare oom. the test harness didn't exercise long context tails.
[01:48] Ellen Zhao: from security — i want to call out that during the fallback we also saw an extra auth check failing for SAML assertions on private nodes, likely a timing edge. not necessarily exploitable but makes customer-facing messaging tricky.
[02:02] Diego Marquez: that's the private cluster peering case we worried about; region failover added a slight clock skew and the SAML validation window is short.
[02:12] Ava Chen: okay, so three buckets: performance tail from kernel fallback, KV-cache cold-start cost increase, and SAML timing issues. let's walk the simulated script. priya you will role-play comms?
[02:22] Priya Nair: yes. simulated scenario: canary latency degradation at T0, engineering determines model fallback is happening, decide whether to degrade to smaller model or roll back the kernel change. we need to exercise decision trees.
[02:34] Ben Howard: i triggered the sim at 11:43 in the test cluster. Mateo, you have logs?
[02:40] Mateo Ruiz: sending link now — scoreboard.log and kernel-monitor.csv (link: internal:/canary2/sim-2026-12-08). note the spike aligns with concurrent large-context requests.
[02:54] [crosstalk, someone coughs] 
[03:00] Sophie Li: quick question: what are our SLO thresholds that trip an automated rollback vs manual intervention? do we have a firm number?
[03:07] Rajat Singh: current rollout config uses a p95 latency threshold of 300ms and a p99 threshold of 900ms for automated rollback on canary. but those were tuned for smaller contexts. for long contexts the p95 baseline is already higher.
[03:22] Mateo Ruiz: right — and the automatic policy didn't account for sequence-length distribution shift. the canary had an unusual batch of long prompts coming through which skewed metrics.
[03:30] Ava Chen: so we've got a false positive risk. priya, what's the decision tree if automatic rollback fires erroneously?
[03:38] Priya Nair: fallback: if auto rollback triggers, oncall gets paged and release lead decides within 10 minutes to confirm rollback. we also have a temp-degrade option in the routing policy to shift some percent of traffic to a smaller model instead of rolling back. that's less disruptive to releases but may change cost and output.
[03:52] Ben Howard: we practiced the temp-degrade yesterday; it took about 6 minutes to shift policy and 18 minutes for key caches to warm, so customers experienced a short period of elevated cost.
[04:04] Ava Chen: note for comms: we need to call out likely impact — short spike in latency, potential higher token costs for some customers, and a follow-up with more detailed post-mortem. keep the message short and avoid technical blame.
[04:18] Ellen Zhao: and include that SSO flakiness was observed but not confirmed as exploit. re security, we'll write an appendix explaining mitigations and the timeline for SAML patching.
[04:30] Diego Marquez: for private deployments, customers may need to restart the auth adapter if the clock skew hits them. include the restart instructions in the runbook.
[04:40] Priya Nair: action: priya will add that instruction to runbook; ben will write the one-liner for the customer bulletin.
[04:48] [keyboard tap noises] 
[05:00] Mateo Ruiz: returning to the KV cache warming issue — we saw a token-cost spike when cold nodes served requests; cost per token went up because requests fell back to longer compute path. the autoscaler later spun up, but the initial 12 minutes matter.
[05:18] Rajat Singh: can we warm caches faster? maybe prefetch the top prefixes for high-traffic tenants during rollout?
[05:24] Mateo Ruiz: yes, we can push a quick warm-up hook in the rollout plan to prepopulate KV for the top 50 tenants. script exists but needs integration with the release pipeline.
[05:35] Maya Torres: we should also throttle the canary traffic by certificate in the LB and make sure the initial burst doesn't trigger autoscaler oscillations.
[05:44] Priya Nair: ok prioritization: 1) add canary pre-warm hook, 2) adjust automatic SLO thresholds to be sequence-length-aware, 3) update comms templates to avoid confusing technical detail.
[05:56] Ava Chen: timeline on those?
[06:00] Mateo Ruiz: pre-warm hook — i can do a PR today and request merge tomorrow; integration might take until friday.
[06:06] Rajat Singh: SLO tuning — i'll follow up with product for sequence-length signals and we can deploy a metric adjustment by 2026-12-11.
[06:14] Ellen Zhao: i'll produce the security appendix tonight.
[06:18] Priya Nair: comms templates — i'll draft versions for P0 and P1 with one-line, three-line and full postmortem slots by EOD tomorrow.
[06:26] [20 minute simulation jump — team runs through scenario offline] 
[26:40] Ava Chen: back on the call. the simulation highlighted a confusing handoff between SE and release lead. specifically, the AE got customer questions before the release lead had a decision. we should formalize the pre-approval phrase.
[26:56] Diego Marquez: propose a canonical sentence: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." short, non-technical.
[27:12] Ava Chen: looks good. Mateo, if engineering confirms rollback, what's the expected customer-visible delta and how quickly can you revert?
[27:20] Mateo Ruiz: a rollback to previous kernel variant typically takes 4-7 minutes to complete on the control plane and another 8-12 for caches to stabilize under normal load. for long contexts, p99 may still be elevated until caches are fully warmed.
[27:34] Rajat Singh: important callout — a rollback also means we lose the small memory-safety gain from the new fallback; that risk is low for now but note it in the decision log.

[27:46] Priya Nair: release lead — priya will own the decision log and commit it to the launch ops channel.
[27:52] Ava Chen: good. now let's role-play the AE message to customers. priya, can you read the short and the extended?
[28:00] Priya Nair: short: "We are investigating a limited degradation affecting generation latency for long-context requests; engineering is actively assessing and we will provide an update within 30 minutes." extended: "We observed increased latency for longer-context requests tied to a kernel fallback path exercised on canary nodes during rollout. We have mitigations in flight and may roll back the recent kernel change if the regression persists. Impact is localized to longer contexts and a subset of tenants; we'll provide timelines as they become available and a postmortem within 24 hours."
[28:34] Ava Chen: that's good. Diego — add a line for customers on dedicated/private setups about restarting the auth adapter if they see SAML errors.
[28:44] Diego Marquez: done. priya will include in the template.
[28:48] [brief silence] 
[29:00] Sophie Li: i want to flag downstream product expectations — some customers embed long prompts as single messages; this launch has a higher proportion of those users. should we ramp slower for those accounts?
[29:12] Ava Chen: yes, we can opt those accounts out of fast ramp and move them through a manual staging cadence. we need to identify top 30 accounts and mark them in the rollout dashboard.
[29:24] Mateo Ruiz: also reminder that the fallback kernel is single-threaded for small batches; for customers with bursty single requests it will hurt more. we can set per-account policy to avoid fallback for high-value tenants.
[29:36] Rajat Singh: let's not make the per-account policy too heavy — prefer a small set of opt-outs.
[29:42] Priya Nair: action: priya + sophie to create the opt-out list and strategy; due 2026-12-09.
[29:50] [keyboard clicking] 
[30:00] Ben Howard: incident comms cadence rehearsal — who reads from the playbook?
[30:04] Priya Nair: i'll read.
[30:06] Ben Howard: ok, then we simulate a follow up 30 minutes later where engineering confirms rollback. priya, craft the 30-minute update.
[30:14] Priya Nair: 30-minute update: "Engineering identified kernel fallback as the likely cause and has initiated a rollback on canary. We expect reduced latency within 15 minutes; further updates at 60 minutes. No evidence of customer data impact. Security is investigating a parallel SAML timing anomaly for private deployments."
[30:32] Ava Chen: perfect. make sure the language says "no evidence" not "no impact" to avoid definitive absolutes.
[30:38] Ellen Zhao: noted.
[30:40] [brief overlap, someone says "mm-hmm"]
[30:44] Mateo Ruiz: technical note — post-rollback we should run a focused long-context load test to validate caches warmed and p99 is back under 900ms. can we agree on a test window?
[30:54] Rajat Singh: reserve 45 minutes after rollback to run the validation. ben, please coordinate the window.
[30:58] Ben Howard: will do.
[31:00] [simulation end] 
[31:02] Ava Chen: debrief highlights: we need clearer per-account escalation, pre-warm hooks, and SLO adjustments for sequence-length. also the communication templates look good but add the SAML appendix. any objections?
[31:16] Sophie Li: none.
[31:18] Diego Marquez: none.

[31:36] Mateo Ruiz: yes.
[31:38] Priya Nair: SLO tuning — rajat with product — aim 2026-12-11 for deployment.

title: 2026-11-08-thalassa-analytics-onboard-playbook-kickoff
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-11-08
Start: 15:30 PST
Duration: 62 minutes (recording)
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood CSM), Nora Li (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (Thalassa - Head of MLOps), Rajiv Menon (Thalassa - Platform Eng), Cara Boyle (Thalassa - Data Security), Tomás Ruiz (Thalassa - DevOps)

Summary:
Quick kickoff to align on onboarding runbook, network prerequisites for Private/Dedicated staging, initial pilot scope (embedding & chat endpoints), security checklist (KMS/retention/SOC2), and next week smoke test target. Some minor audio dropout around 00:32-00:45; auto-transcription had hiccups with product names (Redwood Dedicated sometimes transcribed as Redwood 'Deducted' etc.).

Topics discussed:
- Project goals and success criteria
- Staging networking and allowlist details for Private deployment (VPC peering)
- Pilot scope: 100k tokens/mo estimate, embedding indexing, and latency targets
- Security/compliance: KMS, audit logs, SSO, retention policies
- Rollout plan and runbook cadence (2-week sprints, smoke test on 2026-11-15)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. We'll do a quick round of intros and then walk through the onboarding playbook. I'm Ava, I'm the AE on the account. Diego and Nora are with me from the success and solutions teams.
[00:34] Lena: Hey all — Lena here. Thank you. We are excited, trying to get this lined up for a pilot by mid-November. We have some compliance gates we need to satisfy first.
[01:02] Diego: Great, that's exactly why we scheduled this. We'll try to be pragmatic. Quick ask: can someone confirm who owns the VPC peering and who will be the network contact?
[01:20] Rajiv: That'd be Tomás and me. We'll own the peering. We'll also need the IP ranges for staging and any egress destinations.
[01:45] Nora: Awesome. For the Private deployment we have two common patterns: VPC peering to Redwood Private control plane, or a customer-hosted connector. We recommend peering for faster setup. Caveat — certain HSM/KMS flows require additional networking rules.
[02:15] Cara: Quick question — do you have a sample KMS workflow? We might need to involve our infra security team to sign off.
[02:32] Ava: Yes, we have docs. I'll drop the KMS flow link after the call, that's in the onboarding runbook. For HSM we do support customer-managed keys via KMS/HSM integrations but that needs a short prescriptive config.
[03:05] Lena: For success metrics, we want two things: 1) embedding recall above our current baseline on retrieval, and 2) stable sub-200ms 95p latency for chat inference in staging. Is that realistic?
[03:32] Nora: Sub-200ms 95th is ambitious for certain model sizes, but for optimized quantized embeddings and tuned batching it's achievable at lower seq lengths. We'll capture target model families in the runbook.
[04:00] Tomás: On batching — will automatic batching be on by default for the Pilot? We want to avoid unpredictable latency spikes.
[04:28] Diego: We can set batching policies per-route. For the pilot we usually recommend conservative batch windows and KV cache enabled. We'll include the config in the sprint template.
[05:02] Rajiv: Ok, and how about data retention? We need to make sure logs don't persist beyond our retention window.
[05:24] Ava: You can set token retention and audit log retention per-tenant. For enterprise Private, audit logs can be configured to forward to your SIEM; we'll include an example.
[05:50] Cara: And does Redwood provide SOC2/ISO attestation documents we can give to procurement?
[06:07] Ava: Yes, we have SOC2 Type II and an ISO summary. I will attach the security pack in the follow-up email.
[06:35] Lena: Great. Can we talk about the pilot scope? We agreed on embeddings + chat. What are the concrete deliverables?
[06:52] Diego: Deliverables: 1) Setup staging Private connection; 2) Ingest 50k seed documents to evaluate embedding recall; 3) Wire chat route with a small prompt set; 4) Run smoke tests and latency benchmarks; 5) Post-pilot go/no-go decision. We'll aim for a 2-week pilot.
[07:30] Ava: We're proposing smoke test week starting 2026-11-15, so you'll have time to prepare network bits next week.
[07:50] Rajiv: That works. We'll try to get the staging CIDR and firewall owners by Monday.
[08:10] Nora: Quick note — when you ingest documents, if you have PII we recommend field-level redaction before upload; we can also show a transformation pipeline.
[08:34] Lena: We do have some PII in older docs. We'll scope a redaction step for the pilot.
[09:05] Tomás: For the VPC peering, do you require static egress IPs? Our infra folks prefer allowlisting outbound IPs.
[09:19] Ava: For hosted API folks we publish IP ranges, and for Redwood Private we provide edge egress addresses per region. We'll include the allowlist in the network checklist.
[09:50] Rajiv: Any constraints on TLS or mutual TLS for the connector?
[10:06] Nora: All traffic is TLS. Mutual TLS is supported in the connector flow if you opt into it; it's not mandatory for peering. The runbook includes an mTLS snippet.
[10:40] Cara: Regarding KMS, if we need HSM-backed keys for certain datasets, will that add latency to encrypt/decrypt on inference paths?
[11:02] Ava: Minimal — the HSM call is typically off the hot path; tokens and ephemeral session keys avoid synchronous HSM calls per request. We'll clarify exact flows in the security doc.
[11:38] Lena: About cost — can we get an estimate for 100k tokens per month? We need to budget for pilot and then scale projections.
[11:55] Diego: I'll produce a token-level estimate. Quick napkin: embeddings cost depends on vector dimension and model, and chat depends on model selection. We'll assume mid-size open models for pilot. Ava will send a formal estimate.
[12:20] Ava: Yup — I'll include a table with best/worst case for Dedicated vs Hosted per-token numbers.
[12:41] Tomás: Is there any work on our side for model selection or do you recommend specific families?
[12:55] Nora: We'll recommend a short list based on your latency targets — smaller quantized models for chat if latency critical; for embeddings we'll recommend high-quality embedding models we host.
[13:30] Lena: Ok. Logistics — who's the primary technical contact for day-to-day?
[13:45] Rajiv: That'll be me; Cara will handle security gating; Tomás handles infra.
[14:05] Diego: Perfect. So next steps: we'll email the runbook, the network checklist, and the KMS/security attachment. We'll also set the pilot smoke test for 11/15.
[14:30] Ava: One more thing — for canary and rollback policies, do you have requirements for A/B traffic routing?
[14:47] Lena: Not for pilot, but we will need that later for prod.
[15:05] Nora: Noted. We'll include a short note about A/B rollout tooling in the runbook — traffic split, rollback criteria, and monitoring hooks.
[15:35] Rajiv: About observability — do you emit per-route latency histograms and token-level cost metrics?
[15:50] Ava: Yes, in Redwood Console you get per-route latency, token breakdown, and alerting. We'll grant you a staging demo tenant so you can poke around.
[16:15] Tomás: Sounds good. Any gotchas you want us to be aware of?
[16:28] Nora: Watch for sequence length growth in chat — prompts + history. For predictable latency, limit context window or enable summarization. We'll share a prompt hygiene checklist.
[16:55] Lena: Good, we'll codify prompt length limits in the pilot.
[17:13] Diego: I'm also going to propose a weekly 30-minute sync during the two-week pilot — quick blockers and a rolling backlog. Thoughts?
[17:28] Rajiv: Works for us.
[17:38] Cara: We'll need procurement paperwork to start once the pilot is green — can you confirm billing contact and trial pricing terms?
[17:51] Ava: Billing contact is on our quote; I'll attach the trial pricing terms. For the pilot we usually provide a nominal credit and then show a consumption estimate.
[18:12] Lena: Perfect. One small transcription note — I heard 'Redwood Dedicated' come through as 'Redwood Deducted' in your notes, might be the auto-transcriber.
[18:25] Ava: Yeah, that sometimes happens — thanks for flagging.
[18:40] Diego: Ok, let's capture immediate action items: Diego will share the runbook and sprint template; Ava will share SOC2 + KMS doc + cost estimate; Rajiv and Tomás will share CIDR and firewall owners; Cara will confirm encryption needs.
[19:05] Tomás: We'll try to have network data by Monday.
[19:20] Rajiv: Agreed.
[19:35] Nora: One follow-up technical note — if you want to hit <200ms 95p we'll need to pin model size and route to dedicated low-latency pool. That is part of Dedicated or Dedicated-like reserved capacity; we can show a comparison in the cost table.
[20:02] Lena: Ok let's see the cost table before we finalize.
[20:18] Ava: We'll send that today.
[20:35] Diego: Any other questions from the Thalassa side?
[20:45] Cara: One more on IAM — we use SAML SSO and want RBAC enforced in the Console. Is that supported?
[21:00] Ava: Yes, SSO + SCIM provisioning and RBAC are supported on enterprise plans. We'll include an example SAML metadata file in the security pack.
[21:30] Rajiv: Great.
[21:45] Lena: That's it from me. Thanks everyone.
[21:55] Ava: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the docs and set the pilot smoke-test booking for 2026-11-15. Expect email from Diego with the runbook link.
[22:12] Diego: Thanks, talk soon.

(Recording continues — remaining minutes used for quick screenshare that the auto-transcript fuzzy-captured; a few timestamps and speaker labels may be off.)

Notes and resources referred:
- Onboarding runbook (link to: https://redwood.example.com/onboard/runbook/thalassa)
- Security pack (attached: SOC2_TypeII_summary.pdf, KMS_integration_guide.pdf)
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-11-08
Start: 15:30 PST
Duration: 62 minutes (recording)
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood CSM), Nora Li (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (Thalassa - Head of MLOps), Rajiv Menon (Thalassa - Platform Eng), Cara Boyle (Thalassa - Data Security), Tomás Ruiz (Thalassa - DevOps)

Summary:
Quick kickoff to align on onboarding runbook, network prerequisites for Private/Dedicated staging, initial pilot scope (embedding & chat endpoints), security checklist (KMS/retention/SOC2), and next week smoke test target. Some minor audio dropout around 00:32-00:45; auto-transcription had hiccups with product names (Redwood Dedicated sometimes transcribed as Redwood 'Deducted' etc.).

Topics discussed:
- Project goals and success criteria
- Staging networking and allowlist details for Private deployment (VPC peering)
- Pilot scope: 100k tokens/mo estimate, embedding indexing, and latency targets
- Security/compliance: KMS, audit logs, SSO, retention policies
- Rollout plan and runbook cadence (2-week sprints, smoke test on 2026-11-15)

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava: Hi everyone, thanks for joining. We'll do a quick round of intros and then walk through the onboarding playbook. I'm Ava, I'm the AE on the account. Diego and Nora are with me from the success and solutions teams.
[00:34] Lena: Hey all — Lena here. Thank you. We are excited, trying to get this lined up for a pilot by mid-November. We have some compliance gates we need to satisfy first.
[01:02] Diego: Great, that's exactly why we scheduled this. We'll try to be pragmatic. Quick ask: can someone confirm who owns the VPC peering and who will be the network contact?
[01:20] Rajiv: That'd be Tomás and me. We'll own the peering. We'll also need the IP ranges for staging and any egress destinations.
[01:45] Nora: Awesome. For the Private deployment we have two common patterns: VPC peering to Redwood Private control plane, or a customer-hosted connector. We recommend peering for faster setup. Caveat — certain HSM/KMS flows require additional networking rules.
[02:15] Cara: Quick question — do you have a sample KMS workflow? We might need to involve our infra security team to sign off.
[02:32] Ava: Yes, we have docs. I'll drop the KMS flow link after the call, that's in the onboarding runbook. For HSM we do support customer-managed keys via KMS/HSM integrations but that needs a short prescriptive config.
[03:05] Lena: For success metrics, we want two things: 1) embedding recall above our current baseline on retrieval, and 2) stable sub-200ms 95p latency for chat inference in staging. Is that realistic?
[03:32] Nora: Sub-200ms 95th is ambitious for certain model sizes, but for optimized quantized embeddings and tuned batching it's achievable at lower seq lengths. We'll capture target model families in the runbook.
[04:00] Tomás: On batching — will automatic batching be on by default for the Pilot? We want to avoid unpredictable latency spikes.
[04:28] Diego: We can set batching policies per-route. For the pilot we usually recommend conservative batch windows and KV cache enabled. We'll include the config in the sprint template.
[05:02] Rajiv: Ok, and how about data retention? We need to make sure logs don't persist beyond our retention window.
[05:24] Ava: You can set token retention and audit log retention per-tenant. For enterprise Private, audit logs can be configured to forward to your SIEM; we'll include an example.
[05:50] Cara: And does Redwood provide SOC2/ISO attestation documents we can give to procurement?
[06:07] Ava: Yes, we have SOC2 Type II and an ISO summary. I will attach the security pack in the follow-up email.
[06:35] Lena: Great. Can we talk about the pilot scope? We agreed on embeddings + chat. What are the concrete deliverables?
[06:52] Diego: Deliverables: 1) Setup staging Private connection; 2) Ingest 50k seed documents to evaluate embedding recall; 3) Wire chat route with a small prompt set; 4) Run smoke tests and latency benchmarks; 5) Post-pilot go/no-go decision. We'll aim for a 2-week pilot.
[07:30] Ava: We're proposing smoke test week starting 2026-11-15, so you'll have time to prepare network bits next week.
[07:50] Rajiv: That works. We'll try to get the staging CIDR and firewall owners by Monday.
[08:10] Nora: Quick note — when you ingest documents, if you have PII we recommend field-level redaction before upload; we can also show a transformation pipeline.
[08:34] Lena: We do have some PII in older docs. We'll scope a redaction step for the pilot.
[09:05] Tomás: For the VPC peering, do you require static egress IPs? Our infra folks prefer allowlisting outbound IPs.
[09:19] Ava: For hosted API folks we publish IP ranges, and for Redwood Private we provide edge egress addresses per region. We'll include the allowlist in the network checklist.
[09:50] Rajiv: Any constraints on TLS or mutual TLS for the connector?
[10:06] Nora: All traffic is TLS. Mutual TLS is supported in the connector flow if you opt into it; it's not mandatory for peering. The runbook includes an mTLS snippet.
[10:40] Cara: Regarding KMS, if we need HSM-backed keys for certain datasets, will that add latency to encrypt/decrypt on inference paths?
[11:02] Ava: Minimal — the HSM call is typically off the hot path; tokens and ephemeral session keys avoid synchronous HSM calls per request. We'll clarify exact flows in the security doc.
[11:38] Lena: About cost — can we get an estimate for 100k tokens per month? We need to budget for pilot and then scale projections.
[11:55] Diego: I'll produce a token-level estimate. Quick napkin: embeddings cost depends on vector dimension and model, and chat depends on model selection. We'll assume mid-size open models for pilot. Ava will send a formal estimate.
[12:20] Ava: Yup — I'll include a table with best/worst case for Dedicated vs Hosted per-token numbers.
[12:41] Tomás: Is there any work on our side for model selection or do you recommend specific families?
[12:55] Nora: We'll recommend a short list based on your latency targets — smaller quantized models for chat if latency critical; for embeddings we'll recommend high-quality embedding models we host.
[13:30] Lena: Ok. Logistics — who's the primary technical contact for day-to-day?
[13:45] Rajiv: That'll be me; Cara will handle security gating; Tomás handles infra.
[14:05] Diego: Perfect. So next steps: we'll email the runbook, the network checklist, and the KMS/security attachment. We'll also set the pilot smoke test for 11/15.
[14:30] Ava: One more thing — for canary and rollback policies, do you have requirements for A/B traffic routing?
[14:47] Lena: Not for pilot, but we will need that later for prod.
[15:05] Nora: Noted. We'll include a short note about A/B rollout tooling in the runbook — traffic split, rollback criteria, and monitoring hooks.
[15:35] Rajiv: About observability — do you emit per-route latency histograms and token-level cost metrics?
[15:50] Ava: Yes, in Redwood Console you get per-route latency, token breakdown, and alerting. We'll grant you a staging demo tenant so you can poke around.
[16:15] Tomás: Sounds good. Any gotchas you want us to be aware of?
[16:28] Nora: Watch for sequence length growth in chat — prompts + history. For predictable latency, limit context window or enable summarization. We'll share a prompt hygiene checklist.
[16:55] Lena: Good, we'll codify prompt length limits in the pilot.
[17:13] Diego: I'm also going to propose a weekly 30-minute sync during the two-week pilot — quick blockers and a rolling backlog. Thoughts?
[17:28] Rajiv: Works for us.
[17:38] Cara: We'll need procurement paperwork to start once the pilot is green — can you confirm billing contact and trial pricing terms?
[17:51] Ava: Billing contact is on our quote; I'll attach the trial pricing terms. For the pilot we usually provide a nominal credit and then show a consumption estimate.
[18:12] Lena: Perfect. One small transcription note — I heard 'Redwood Dedicated' come through as 'Redwood Deducted' in your notes, might be the auto-transcriber.
[18:25] Ava: Yeah, that sometimes happens — thanks for flagging.
[18:40] Diego: Ok, let's capture immediate action items: Diego will share the runbook and sprint template; Ava will share SOC2 + KMS doc + cost estimate; Rajiv and Tomás will share CIDR and firewall owners; Cara will confirm encryption needs.
[19:05] Tomás: We'll try to have network data by Monday.
[19:20] Rajiv: Agreed.
[19:35] Nora: One follow-up technical note — if you want to hit <200ms 95p we'll need to pin model size and route to dedicated low-latency pool. That is part of Dedicated or Dedicated-like reserved capacity; we can show a comparison in the cost table.
[20:02] Lena: Ok let's see the cost table before we finalize.
[20:18] Ava: We'll send that today.
[20:35] Diego: Any other questions from the Thalassa side?
[20:45] Cara: One more on IAM — we use SAML SSO and want RBAC enforced in the Console. Is that supported?
[21:00] Ava: Yes, SSO + SCIM provisioning and RBAC are supported on enterprise plans. We'll include an example SAML metadata file in the security pack.
[21:30] Rajiv: Great.
[21:45] Lena: That's it from me. Thanks everyone.
[21:55] Ava: Thanks all — we'll follow up with the docs and set the pilot smoke-test booking for 2026-11-15. Expect email from Diego with the runbook link.
[22:12] Diego: Thanks, talk soon.

(Recording continues — remaining minutes used for quick screenshare that the auto-transcript fuzzy-captured; a few timestamps and speaker labels may be off.)

Notes and resources referred:
- Onboarding runbook (link to: https://redwood.example.com/onboard/runbook/thalassa)
- Security pack (attached: SOC2_TypeII_summary.pdf, KMS_integration_guide.pdf)

title: Fallback orchestration roster and SLA playbook sync
preview:
Meeting header: Recorded at: 2026-10-28 14:00 UTC; Duration: 62 minutes; Attendees: Ava Chen (AE), Samir Patel (SE), Lena Ortiz (PM) — Redwood; Marcus Hale (SRE), Priya Singh (Platform PM), Diego Morales (CTO) — NimbusPay.

Summary: Short tabletop to align fallback orchestration responsibilities, canary drill schedule, and an SLA-focused playbook for a high-traffic payments release. Discussed circuit-breaker behavior, cross-region fallbacks, RBAC for runbook execution, and who owns automated rollback vs manual intervention.

Topics: fallback orchestration, circuit-breaker thresholds, canary drill dates, SLA playbook distribution, audit logging/KMS considerations, who to page on infra vs product, competitor fallback patterns.

[00:00] Ava Chen (AE): "Thanks everybody for joining, short agenda — align who does what if we see a sustained token-latency spike or an increase in 5xxs during the ramp."
[00:18] Marcus Hale (SRE): "Yeah hi, we ran the sim yesterday and saw KV cache miss storms on p99 after a cold restart, that was the big hairball. Also some autoscaler jitter, so we want to codify the circuit breaker."
[00:36] Samir Patel (SE): "Right, so two levers we use: local circuit-breaker at the routing tier and a global orchestration circuit that can triage routes into degraded modes. We should make clear who flips the global one."
[00:52] Priya Singh (Platform PM): "When you say flips, do you mean automatic or operator? Our compliance folks want manual gating for high-risk flows, at least initially."
[01:10] Samir: "We support both. Auto triggers can be SLO-based and then go to a manual approval step if it would affect payment success rates. The playbook needs that branching."
[01:28] Diego Morales (CTO): "For NimbusPay, payments success is non-negotiable — any automatic fallback to a cheaper model that changes output is risky unless rollback is immediate. We want an explicit canary flow and an easy panic button.
[01:45]"
[01:46] Ava: "Ok, let's map owners. Suggested: Redwood SE owns the orchestration rules and automatic thresholds; NimbusPay SRE approves any cross-region fallback; NimbusPay product owns customer-facing comms. Thoughts?"
[02:04] Marcus: "That seems fine but we need times — like who pages at 5, 15, 30 minutes. We'll add paging tiers."
[02:18] Samir: "Agree. Also add a KV cache warmup step during rollout to avoid the miss-storm. We can script a pre-populate with warmup tokens per shard."
[02:34] Priya: "Warmup is good. Also, who controls KMS access for rollback artifacts? We need audit logs and RBAC so only ops can run the rollback."
[02:48] Samir: "We can require a SAML SSO approval and a signed commit for rollback. We'll annotate the playbook with the KMS key alias and the HSM rotation window. Might be overkill but it's explicit.
[03:06]"
[03:08] Diego: "One thing we saw with competitor toolsets — they often rely on silent model-variant fallback that changes outputs and the app layer doesn't notice until customers complain. We need better trace propagation so we can identify model-variant at request level."
[03:26] Samir: "We already emit model_id + quant_profile in X-Trace, but we can make that required for canary traces. Add to playbook: mandatory trace tags for any canary traffic."
[03:40] Marcus: "About thresholds: start canary at 0.5% traffic for 30 minutes, then 5% for 2 hours, check billing delta and semantic-quality using our eval set. If p50 latency increases more than 30% or payment-failure delta > 0.1%, stop. Same idea?"
[03:58] Ava: "Yes, capture those numbers, and include both perf and business metrics. We'll add a stop rule for any downstream timeouts too."
[04:12] Priya: "Also need to clarify when we use private deployment vs dedicated. For this release, we're on Redwood Dedicated but plan to test Private later. The playbook should mention IP allowlists and VPC peer steps.
[04:28]"
[04:30] Samir: "Noted. We'll include a VPC peering checklist and the steps to rotate the endpoint if we need to fail over to private. That said, cross-region private failover is a heavier lift. We'll document the manual steps.
[04:48]"
[04:50] Lena Ortiz (PM): "I want to propose a clear runbook table: Trigger, Detection metric, Owner, Action, Escalation contact. We can map to the paging tiers Marcus mentioned. I can draft that and share."
[05:06] Marcus: "Please do. Also add a section on how to interpret KV-cache hit-rate charts, those were confusing during sim because the axes were different.
[05:18]"
[05:20] Ava: "Quickly, on observability: Redwood will share a perf dashboard for the canary with token-level cost and model variant breakdown. We'll also supply a synthetic traffic harness. Samir, you said you could share the harness?"
[05:34] Samir: "Yes, I'll publish it in the shared repo. Warning: the harness assumes a stable KV pattern; I'll include a mode that simulates cold starts.
[05:46]"
[05:48] Diego: "On legal/compliance: NimbusPay needs SOC2 artifact for audit showing role separation and key rotation. Can you include a short section in the playbook about who has KMS access and a link to the compliance pack?"
[06:05] Ava: "Yes, we'll attach the compliance pack. We'll redact PII but include procedural docs.
[06:12]"
[06:14] Priya: "One more ask: competitor fallback patterns — Anthropic had a case where fallback doubled token cost because they didn't batch properly for degraded mode. We want cost guardrails in the playbook.
[06:30]"
[06:32] Samir: "Good call. Add cost SLOs and a budget-based breaker: if estimated spend delta > X% for the hour, pause rollout and require manual approve.
[06:44]"
[06:46] Marcus: "On-call roster: we need a short list of 1st/2nd/3rd with contact methods — not full org. Who from Redwood will be on 1st?"
[06:56] Ava: "Samir 1st, Lena backup for product decisions, and a scheduled on-call engineer from Redwood infra for the actual flip. We'll make sure it's a small rotation.
[07:12]"
[07:14] Marcus: "Ok can we add an explicit 'panic button' procedure — page both Redwood AE and SRE, then execute immediate rollback script if payment-failure delta > threshold. I want the rollback script accessible only via SSO and logged.
[07:32]"
[07:34] Samir: "We have a rollback script but it's gated behind the orchestrator with an approval step — single-button rollback with OTP confirm. We'll document the logging hooks.
[07:46]"
[07:48] Ava: "Let's talk canary drill cadence — propose a full dress rehearsal two weeks before launch, then a smaller tabletop the day before. Is that acceptable?"
[07:58] Priya: "Two weeks is great. Our release window is 2026-11-18, so that puts the drill on 2026-11-04 — aligns with what we said.
[08:10]"
[08:12] Lena: "I'll own invites and scenario list. Samir, you'll bring the harness and failure injection patterns. Marcus, you bring your real traffic profile or a synthetic close to it.
[08:24]"
[08:26] Marcus: "Yep, I'll prepare a sampled-day profile and mark PII.
[08:30]"
[08:32] Ava: "On documentation: we'll publish the playbook as a single markdown with a TL;DR one-page flowchart for execs. Also include a short appendix listing what we consider 'acceptable semantic drift' during fallbacks.
[08:46]"
[08:48] Samir: "Semantic drift appendix — we should include example regression prompts and an automated eval harness that computes delta score. I'll include that.
[08:56]"
[08:58] Priya: "Question: for audit logs, does Redwood persist model variant metadata for 365 days by default? Our legal wants one year retention.
[09:10]"
[09:12] Samir: "Default is 90 days in hosted — but for Dedicated we can increase retention; we'll need an add-on retention policy or an export to NimbusPay's logging tenant. We'll note options in the playbook.
[09:24]"
[09:26] Ava: "Ok, let's capture that as an action item.
[09:28]"
[09:30] Marcus: "I want to flag the issue we saw: autoscaler oscillation around the 40% CPU threshold. In the sim it caused frequent VM churn and made the KV misses worse. Any mitigation?"
[09:44] Samir: "We can smooth autoscaler with a longer cool-down and apply request-based metrics not just CPU. Also add a pre-scale step during canary so that we pre-warm capacity.
[09:56]"
[09:58] Diego: "How does that affect cost? Our CFO will ask. Can you estimate the cost delta for pre-warm?"
[10:06] Samir: "We can model it. I'll include a conservative estimate in the cost appendix — pre-warm is usually cheaper than the cost of repeated instance churn and error remediation.
[10:18]"
[10:20] Lena: "We should also include escalation matrix if a third-party infra provider degrades — example: if a zone goes down, Redwood should automatically fail traffic to another region but only after NimbusPay ops approves for payment flows.
[10:36]"
[10:38] Ava: "We'll add a distinction: automatic cross-region fallback for non-critical traffic; for payment-critical routes require manual approve. We'll write the justification.
[10:50]"
[10:52] Marcus: "One last technical detail: we need a way to correlate traces to customer transactions — a mapping field in the trace. Can Redwood add a transaction_id field propagated to model logs?"
[11:04] Samir: "We can add an optional header, but it has PII implications. We'll add a pseudonymized transaction hash and document retention.
[11:16]"
[11:18] Diego: "Perfect. Let's wrap with who does what. Ava, you said you'll send the playbook v1 with a one-pager. Samir, harness + appendix. Lena, invite list and drills. Marcus, traffic profile.
[11:32] Ava: "Yep. Next steps: playbook v1 by 11/02, canary drill 11/04, final checklist by 11/15. Any objections?"
[11:44] Priya: "No objections. Appreciate the clarity.
[11:46]"
[11:47] Ava: "Great, thanks all. We'll follow up in the doc and add the action items in the shared ticket.
[11:54] Marcus: "Thanks.
[11:55] Diego: "Thanks everyone.
[11:56] Ava: "Meeting end.

References shared during call: 
- Link: playbook skeleton (internal) - will be shared after meeting
- Link: canary harness repo (REPO/infra/canary-harness)
- Link: perf spreadsheet with kv-cache simulations (will be attached)

End transcript.

[07:58] Priya: "Two weeks is great. Our release window is 2026-11-18, so that puts the drill on 2026-11-04 — aligns with what we said.

[09:10]"
[09:12] Samir: "Default is 90 days in hosted — but for Dedicated we can increase retention; we'll need an add-on retention policy or an export to NimbusPay's logging tenant. We'll note options in the playbook.
[09:24]"
[09:26] Ava: "Ok, let's capture that as an action item.
[09:28]"
[09:30] Marcus: "I want to flag the issue we saw: autoscaler oscillation around the 40% CPU threshold. In the sim it caused frequent VM churn and made the KV misses worse. Any mitigation?"
[09:44] Samir: "We can smooth autoscaler with a longer cool-down and apply request-based metrics not just CPU. Also add a pre-scale step during canary so that we pre-warm capacity.
[09:56]"
[09:58] Diego: "How does that affect cost? Our CFO will ask. Can you estimate the cost delta for pre-warm?"
[10:06] Samir: "We can model it. I'll include a conservative estimate in the cost appendix — pre-warm is usually cheaper than the cost of repeated instance churn and error remediation.
[10:18]"
[10:20] Lena: "We should also include escalation matrix if a third-party infra provider degrades — example: if a zone goes down, Redwood should automatically fail traffic to another region but only after NimbusPay ops approves for payment flows.
[10:36]"
[10:38] Ava: "We'll add a distinction: automatic cross-region fallback for non-critical traffic; for payment-critical routes require manual approve. We'll write the justification.
[10:50]"
[10:52] Marcus: "One last technical detail: we need a way to correlate traces to customer transactions — a mapping field in the trace. Can Redwood add a transaction_id field propagated to model logs?"
[11:04] Samir: "We can add an optional header, but it has PII implications. We'll add a pseudonymized transaction hash and document retention.
[11:16]"
[11:18] Diego: "Perfect. Let's wrap with who does what. Ava, you said you'll send the playbook v1 with a one-pager. Samir, harness + appendix. Lena, invite list and drills. Marcus, traffic profile.
[11:32] Ava: "Yep. Next steps: playbook v1 by 11/02, canary drill 11/04, final checklist by 11/15. Any objections?"
[11:44] Priya: "No objections. Appreciate the clarity.
[11:46]"
[11:47] Ava: "Great, thanks all. We'll follow up in the doc and add the action items in the shared ticket.
[11:54] Marcus: "Thanks.
[11:55] Diego: "Thanks everyone.
[11:56] Ava: "Meeting end.

References shared during call: 
- Link: playbook skeleton (internal) - will be shared after meeting
- Link: canary harness repo (REPO/infra/canary-harness)
- Link: perf spreadsheet with kv-cache simulations (will be attached)

End transcript.

title: Orion Systems - Connectivity audit & private access discovery
preview: Initial discovery focused on private connectivity options for Redwood Private: VPC peering vs private endpoints, egress minimization, multi-region constraints, and security requirements (KMS/SOC2/SAML). Customer asked for representative network diagrams, a staging pilot, and security artifacts. Agreed next steps and owners at the end.
text: Initial discovery focused on private connectivity options for Redwood Private: VPC peering vs private endpoints, egress minimization, multi-region constraints, and security requirements (KMS/SOC2/SAML). Customer asked for representative network diagrams, a staging pilot, and security artifacts. Agreed next steps and owners at the end.

title: Resource Corridor + Degrade Policy Workshop
preview: Working session to define the Resource Corridor construct (reserve, burst lanes, regional pools) and to pin down a pragmatic degrade policy for user-facing flows. We aligned on policy tiers, metric triggers, and a short pilot plan for canarying rules across hosted and dedicated deployments.
text: Working session to define the Resource Corridor construct (reserve, burst lanes, regional pools) and to pin down a pragmatic degrade policy for user-facing flows. We aligned on policy tiers, metric triggers, and a short pilot plan for canarying rules across hosted and dedicated deployments.

title: Perf triage: multi-warp kernel switch & CUDA-graph bisect
preview: Deep technical troubleshooting session focused on nondeterministic kernel switches under high-concurrency workloads. We replicated a 95th-percentile latency regression tied to CUDA-graph replay + persistent KV cache interactions on a multi-gpu on-prem cluster. Discussed root-cause hypotheses (warp scheduling, kernel fallback, quantization-induced math paths), proposed microbench experiments, and agreed on an instrumented bisect plan using NVTX, smallstep batch sweeps, and a TRT-LLM vs vLLM comparison under VPC-like network isolation.
