title: BYO Key Escrow & Transport Security Workshop - BlueHarbor Logistics
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2026-10-14 15:00 UTC
Duration: 62 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Daniel Kim (Redwood SE), Riley Patel (Redwood Security PM), Sofia Alvarez (CISO, BlueHarbor), Marcus Lee (SRE), Priya Ramesh (Security Lead), Ethan Park (Cloud Architect)

Summary:
High-level security review focused on BYOK (bring your own key) workflows, HSM integration and attestation, key escrow/rotation policy, transport security for key material during provisioning, and audit/logging requirements for compliance. Customer wants an implementation plan for a pilot that keeps keys in their custody where required and uses Redwood's runtime for inference.

Topics discussed: BYOK modes (customer-managed KMS, dedicated HSM connectors), envelope encryption, key wrapping vs direct-decryption, PKCS#11 connector options, FIPS/HSM attestation, multi-region key residency and failover, TLS and mTLS between Redwood and private deployment, audit log formats and retention, separation of duties and RBAC for key ops.

--- Transcript body (timestamps approximate) ---
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call, then we'll dive. From our side Daniel will cover the SE bits and Riley will cover security controls and attestations.
[00:08] Sofia Alvarez: Hi all, Sofia here. We're particularly focused on BYOK and the legal/regulatory constraints — we need to keep keys under our control for EU workloads.
[00:15] Marcus Lee: Marcus here. I'll be the person doing the hands-on with our AWS KMS/HSM and network setup.
[00:20] Priya Ramesh: Priya here, I'll own compliance and the audit requirements.
[00:24] Daniel Kim: Great, thanks. I'll share my screen in a moment — want to align on high-level constraints first.
[00:30] Ava Chen: Quick agenda: 1) BYOK modes and what Redwood supports, 2) HSM attestation + PKCS11 connector, 3) key transport/escrow and rotation, 4) audit and logging expectations, 5) next steps.
[00:40] Ethan Park: Also want to cover connectivity — whether we need a VPC peering or PrivateLink and what ports/protocols.
[00:46] Daniel Kim: Perfect. Okay, BYOK modes. Redwood supports three patterns: hosted BYOK where keys live in your cloud KMS and Redwood integrates via a connector; dedicated HSM where you attach an HSM (Cloud HSM or on-prem) using PKCS#11; and full-private deployment where the whole inference plane runs in your VPC and keys never leave. The distinction is important for key escrow and transport.
[01:10] Priya Ramesh: When you say "hosted BYOK" do you mean Redwood will call our KMS to unwrap keys each time? We have restrictions on synchronous key usage — prefer keeping unwrapped material out of third party memory.
[01:22] Daniel Kim: Right, so for hosted BYOK we typically do envelope encryption. The model artifacts and model checkpoints are encrypted at rest with a data key that is itself wrapped by your KMS key. Redwood's runtime can be configured to request a decrypted data key transiently into memory; we keep it in ephemeral memory only and we use strict mlock and zeroization on process exit. If you want zero-exposure to plaintext outside your enclave, we can recommend the dedicated HSM or private deployment usage.
[01:52] Sofia Alvarez: mlock? Is that something you do for Linux VMs to avoid swap? We need documentation on memory handling and ephemeral key lifecycle to satisfy auditors.
[02:02] Riley Patel: Yes, we can provide a memory handling doc. We also support option to keep keys in an HSM and perform cryptographic ops inside the HSM so the plaintext never hits host RAM. That's our PKCS#11 flow.
[02:15] Marcus Lee: For PKCS#11, do you support both AWS CloudHSM and on-prem Thales/HSM over PKCS#11? We currently have CloudHSM for EU-west.
[02:24] Daniel Kim: We do. The connector supports CloudHSM, Luna/Thales via standard PKCS#11 or HSM vendors exposing KMIP. A few customers use an HSM in their account and grant Redwood's connector a narrow set of capabilities.
[02:36] Priya Ramesh: We also need attestation that the HSM is FIPS 140-2 level 3 (or 2 depending on region) and a vendor-signed attestation. Can you share your vendor attestations?
[02:48] Riley Patel: Absolutely, we have HSM attestation bundles and our SOC 2 Type II report; we'll attach them after this call. We also have a FIPS compatibility matrix.
[02:58] Sofia Alvarez: Good. Now key transport: how does Redwood pick up the wrapped key material initially for model provisioning? If we provide key material in case of key escrow, how do you ensure transit protection?
[03:10] Daniel Kim: There are two common approaches. 1) You perform a one-time envelope wrap of the data key with your KMS and upload the wrapped key artifact to Redwood via TLS 1.3 with mutual TLS. 2) Or, Redwood makes API calls to your KMS over a private link (PrivateLink or VPC peering) and requests an unwrap using a short-lived role. Both flows are protected by mTLS and we support client cert rotation.
[03:36] Marcus Lee: So if we use PrivateLink, Redwood's control plane would be able to call our KMS endpoints inside AWS — that keeps traffic on the AWS network. We like that. What's the auth model? Assume Role with STS?
[03:47] Daniel Kim: Yes, we recommend using a cross-account IAM role with an assume-role restricted to unwrapping operations. If using CloudHSM, you can restrict the HSM user credential to only allow decrypt/wrap. For on-prem, we'd rely on client cert authentication and mutual TLS with an L4 firewall rule.
[04:00] Priya Ramesh: What about key escrow/backup? Our security policy requires a multi-party escrow policy — split knowledge for the root key. Can Redwood support staged key recovery?
[04:12] Riley Patel: We don't act as escrow by default for BYOK — keys remain under your control. If you want Redwood to hold an escrow envelope, we can do that under a contractual SLA and with KMS-wrapped envelopes; we'll provide audit logs and restricted access, and we can offer multi-signature release policies. But most customers prefer keeping the root key only in their HSM.
[04:36] Sofia Alvarez: For the pilot we want to keep the root KMS in our account, use CloudHSM for EU, and allow Redwood to run inference in a private deployment inside our VPC. We want the runtime to use a PKCS#11 plug-in pointing at our HSM cluster.
[04:50] Daniel Kim: That maps well to Redwood Private. In that model, the inference plane is deployed into your VPC, and we run under your tenancy. Our control plane still needs to orchestrate, but for key ops we can be configured to call only locally reachable endpoints.
[05:05] Ethan Park: How do you handle software updates to the runtime when it's in our VPC? We need to ensure signed artifacts and allowlist verification.
[05:13] Ava Chen: We use signed container images with digest verification and a reproducible build hash. For private deployments we also offer an approval flow — you can stage updates and approve them before they're pulled. We'll share the update signing policy.
[05:25] Marcus Lee: Audit logs: what shape do logs take? We need key usage logs, unwrap/unwrap attempts, who initiated operations, and full syscall-like provenance for SOC2 purposes. Retention must be 7 years for some of our EU contracts.
[05:40] Riley Patel: Our audit system records key operations with timestamp, operation type (unwrap/wrap/sign), key alias, short request ID, and the principal. In private deployment, logs can be shipped to your CloudWatch/Stackdriver/Syslog so they remain in your account. For hosted, we retain logs in our encrypted store and can export them on request. We can support long-term retention via S3 export or an SIEM connector (Splunk/Datadog). We'll provide sample log schema.
[06:03] Priya Ramesh: Will those logs include plaintext key material? No — only metadata, right?
[06:07] Daniel Kim: Correct, only metadata. We never write key plaintext to logs. We also redact request payloads by default.
[06:12] Sofia Alvarez: Let's talk key rotation. Our policy requires rotation every 90 days for data keys and annual rotation for root KMS. How do you manage rewrapping of model artifacts and cache invalidation?
[06:24] Daniel Kim: For envelope encryption, rotation process is: 1) generate a new data key under the new KMS key, 2) rewrap model artifacts (background job or on next deployment), 3) invalidate the runtime cache and KV cache — Redwood supports cache versioning and will seamlessly switch to new keys. We can coordinate a rolling rotation with minimal downtime.
[06:44] Marcus Lee: Does rotating the KMS root require re-encrypting everything? That could be expensive at scale.
[06:50] Daniel Kim: You can avoid mass re-encryption by using key-wrapping hierarchy: keep a stable data key that encrypts model shards and rotate only the wrapping KEK (key-encryption-key) more frequently; rewrap the KEK metadata rather than every artifact. We can provide a script and automation for rewrap.
[07:06] Priya Ramesh: Regulatory question: EU personal data must remain in EU. If Redwood's control plane is global, how do we ensure that unwrapping doesn't cross borders?
[07:16] Riley Patel: We offer regional control plane options and explicit routing policies. For private deployment we can ensure all unwrap calls happen inside your EU VPC region; for hosted BYOK we can negotiate a regional-only path or have you run our control plane stub in-region. We'll need to map the endpoints and audit the traffic path.
[07:33] Ethan Park: On the network side, what ports/protocols are used to talk to the HSM? PKCS#11 is usually local, but CloudHSM uses an ethernet-like interface and TLS to the HSM instance. We'll need more specifics for firewall rules.
[07:45] Daniel Kim: We'll share a one-pager: CloudHSM requires UDP/TCP ephemeral ranges for cluster communication and TLS 1.3 for management; PKCS#11 libraries are local so the runtime loads them like a shared object. For PKCS#11 over network gateways we use mTLS with certs and port 7443 commonly, but it's vendor-specific.
[07:59] Marcus Lee: Do you support external attestation — like a signed TPM quote or remote attestation so we can be sure the runtime isn't tampered with?
[08:08] Riley Patel: We have an optional attestation flow. For private deployments we can require node-level attestations via TPM and produce signed quotes that you can verify. For hosted we provide runtime integrity logs and container signing.
[08:18] Priya Ramesh: For SOC2 auditors we need separation of duties: who on Redwood can access key materials, and can you show role-based access controls?
[08:26] Ava Chen: We limit access by role. For BYOK where you control the KMS, Redwood support engineers never have access to the unwrapped key material unless explicitly requested and under break-glass procedures. We have RBAC for our engineers, and all privileged actions are logged with justification tags. We'll provide an RBAC matrix for review.
[08:42] Sofia Alvarez: About break-glass, what's the process if a support engineer needs to troubleshoot a customer's runtime which requires key unwrap?
[08:49] Daniel Kim: We have a documented process: a support request goes through change control and requires approval by two engineers and the customer's authorized contact. The session is recorded and audited, and temporary session keys are used. We can configure the process to require your explicit approval before any unwrap.
[09:04] Marcus Lee: Can we do ephemeral signing instead of unwrapping? For some flows we just need HMAC/signing so the HSM could sign tokens rather than us handing over data keys.
[09:12] Daniel Kim: Yes, we support in-HSM signing and HMAC operations. Inference-specific ops typically need decryption to access model shards, but for tokenization or request-level signing HSM ops are sufficient.
[09:24] Priya Ramesh: One of our auditors asked about KMS key aliases and lifecycle history. Do you record which key alias was used for each object?
[09:32] Riley Patel: Yes, logs include the key alias and a version ID. We also keep a catalog of key-to-artifact mapping that you can export.
[09:39] Sofia Alvarez: We need sample log extracts for the auditor showing a wrap/unwrap event and the associated fields.
[09:45] Riley Patel: We'll provide a sanitized sample that matches our production schema.
[09:50] Ava Chen: Quick pivot to inter-region failover: if a region loses access, do you have a plan for key failover without exposing keys to other jurisdictions?
[09:59] Daniel Kim: A few approaches: 1) Multi-region key replication under your KMS control so both regions have HSM replicas; 2) A cross-region rewrap via your command where the unwrapping happens in EU and rewrap under EU-regional key; or 3) Active-passive with manual failover to avoid automatic cross-border replication. We recommend approach 2 for strict residency controls.
[10:20] Marcus Lee: For the pilot, we prefer manual failover to maintain audit control.
[10:24] Priya Ramesh: Let's capture that as the pilot constraint.
[10:27] Ava Chen: Noted.
[10:29] Daniel Kim: A question from our side — do you want Redwood to maintain any escrow keys for disaster recovery, or should we always require a customer-initiated restore?
[10:37] Sofia Alvarez: For now, no Redwood-held root keys. We might revisit under a legal agreement.
[10:42] Riley Patel: That's fine. We can provide a playbook for customer-only recovery.
[10:46] Ethan Park: One more nit on TLS: do you enforce TLS 1.3 only? Cipher lists? We need to match our corporate policy which disallows certain ciphers.
[10:55] Daniel Kim: We enforce TLS 1.3 by default and have a cipher whitelist. We'll share the cipher suite list; if you need mutual TLS we can require client certs signed by your CA.
[11:06] Priya Ramesh: Ok, last section: compliance artifacts. We need SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 cert if available, FIPS/HSM attestations, and data-flow diagrams showing where keys transit.
[11:18] Riley Patel: We'll bundle SOC2, our ISO scope doc, FIPS attestations for our HSM vendors, and a dataflow diagram. We'll also include the PKCS#11 connector README and the Private deployment network hardening checklist.
[11:30] Ava Chen: Great. Let's summarize next steps and actions.
[11:35] Sofia Alvarez: Can we schedule the SE lab for week of Oct 26? Prefer a 2-hour window during EU mornings.
[11:41] Ava Chen: Yes, we'll propose Tues/Wed. Daniel will lead.
[11:45] Daniel Kim: I'll prepare a lab that spins up a small private deployment in a test VPC, demonstrates PKCS#11 connectivity to a CloudHSM cluster, and walks through key wrap/unwarp and rotation.
[11:55] Marcus Lee: For the lab we'll give a minimal permissions role and a dedicated CloudHSM cluster in an isolated account.
[12:02] Priya Ramesh: After the lab we want an evidence package for the auditors — logs, attestation, and a runbook signed by our security owner.
[12:10] Riley Patel: Understood. We'll assemble the package and include a step-by-step remediation checklist.
[12:14] Ava Chen: Awesome. Anything else?
[12:17] Sofia Alvarez: One ask — can you include cost ballpark: the extra cost of CloudHSM and the operational overhead for private deployment? We need to justify to finance.
[12:27] Ava Chen: We'll include a simple cost sheet with expected CloudHSM hourly and Redwood private node pricing.
[12:33] Marcus Lee: End-to-end, this looks feasible. Thanks everyone.
[12:37] Ava Chen: Thanks — we'll follow up with the artifacts by Friday.

End transcript.

References & links mentioned during call:
- PKCS#11 connector README (to be sent)
- SOC2 Type II report link (to be attached)
- Private deployment hardening checklist (to be sent)

[06:50] Daniel Kim: You can avoid mass re-encryption by using key-wrapping hierarchy: keep a stable data key that encrypts model shards and rotate only the wrapping KEK (key-encryption-key) more frequently; rewrap the KEK metadata rather than every artifact. We can provide a script and automation for rewrap.
[07:06] Priya Ramesh: Regulatory question: EU personal data must remain in EU. If Redwood's control plane is global, how do we ensure that unwrapping doesn't cross borders?
[07:16] Riley Patel: We offer regional control plane options and explicit routing policies. For private deployment we can ensure all unwrap calls happen inside your EU VPC region; for hosted BYOK we can negotiate a regional-only path or have you run our control plane stub in-region. We'll need to map the endpoints and audit the traffic path.
[07:33] Ethan Park: On the network side, what ports/protocols are used to talk to the HSM? PKCS#11 is usually local, but CloudHSM uses an ethernet-like interface and TLS to the HSM instance. We'll need more specifics for firewall rules.
[07:45] Daniel Kim: We'll share a one-pager: CloudHSM requires UDP/TCP ephemeral ranges for cluster communication and TLS 1.3 for management; PKCS#11 libraries are local so the runtime loads them like a shared object. For PKCS#11 over network gateways we use mTLS with certs and port 7443 commonly, but it's vendor-specific.
[07:59] Marcus Lee: Do you support external attestation — like a signed TPM quote or remote attestation so we can be sure the runtime isn't tampered with?
[08:08] Riley Patel: We have an optional attestation flow. For private deployments we can require node-level attestations via TPM and produce signed quotes that you can verify. For hosted we provide runtime integrity logs and container signing.
[08:18] Priya Ramesh: For SOC2 auditors we need separation of duties: who on Redwood can access key materials, and can you show role-based access controls?
[08:26] Ava Chen: We limit access by role. For BYOK where you control the KMS, Redwood support engineers never have access to the unwrapped key material unless explicitly requested and under break-glass procedures. We have RBAC for our engineers, and all privileged actions are logged with justification tags. We'll provide an RBAC matrix for review.
[08:42] Sofia Alvarez: About break-glass, what's the process if a support engineer needs to troubleshoot a customer's runtime which requires key unwrap?
[08:49] Daniel Kim: We have a documented process: a support request goes through change control and requires approval by two engineers and the customer's authorized contact. The session is recorded and audited, and temporary session keys are used. We can configure the process to require your explicit approval before any unwrap.
[09:04] Marcus Lee: Can we do ephemeral signing instead of unwrapping? For some flows we just need HMAC/signing so the HSM could sign tokens rather than us handing over data keys.
[09:12] Daniel Kim: Yes, we support in-HSM signing and HMAC operations. Inference-specific ops typically need decryption to access model shards, but for tokenization or request-level signing HSM ops are sufficient.
[09:24] Priya Ramesh: One of our auditors asked about KMS key aliases and lifecycle history. Do you record which key alias was used for each object?
[09:32] Riley Patel: Yes, logs include the key alias and a version ID. We also keep a catalog of key-to-artifact mapping that you can export.
[09:39] Sofia Alvarez: We need sample log extracts for the auditor showing a wrap/unwrap event and the associated fields.
[09:45] Riley Patel: We'll provide a sanitized sample that matches our production schema.
[09:50] Ava Chen: Quick pivot to inter-region failover: if a region loses access, do you have a plan for key failover without exposing keys to other jurisdictions?
[09:59] Daniel Kim: A few approaches: 1) Multi-region key replication under your KMS control so both regions have HSM replicas; 2) A cross-region rewrap via your command where the unwrapping happens in EU and rewrap under EU-regional key; or 3) Active-passive with manual failover to avoid automatic cross-border replication. We recommend approach 2 for strict residency controls.
[10:20] Marcus Lee: For the pilot, we prefer manual failover to maintain audit control.
[10:24] Priya Ramesh: Let's capture that as the pilot constraint.
[10:27] Ava Chen: Noted.
[10:29] Daniel Kim: A question from our side — do you want Redwood to maintain any escrow keys for disaster recovery, or should we always require a customer-initiated restore?
[10:37] Sofia Alvarez: For now, no Redwood-held root keys. We might revisit under a legal agreement.
[10:42] Riley Patel: That's fine. We can provide a playbook for customer-only recovery.
[10:46] Ethan Park: One more nit on TLS: do you enforce TLS 1.3 only? Cipher lists? We need to match our corporate policy which disallows certain ciphers.
[10:55] Daniel Kim: We enforce TLS 1.3 by default and have a cipher whitelist. We'll share the cipher suite list; if you need mutual TLS we can require client certs signed by your CA.
[11:06] Priya Ramesh: Ok, last section: compliance artifacts. We need SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001 cert if available, FIPS/HSM attestations, and data-flow diagrams showing where keys transit.
[11:18] Riley Patel: We'll bundle SOC2, our ISO scope doc, FIPS attestations for our HSM vendors, and a dataflow diagram. We'll also include the PKCS#11 connector README and the Private deployment network hardening checklist.
[11:30] Ava Chen: Great. Let's summarize next steps and actions.
[11:35] Sofia Alvarez: Can we schedule the SE lab for week of Oct 26? Prefer a 2-hour window during EU mornings.
[11:41] Ava Chen: Yes, we'll propose Tues/Wed. Daniel will lead.
[11:45] Daniel Kim: I'll prepare a lab that spins up a small private deployment in a test VPC, demonstrates PKCS#11 connectivity to a CloudHSM cluster, and walks through key wrap/unwarp and rotation.
[11:55] Marcus Lee: For the lab we'll give a minimal permissions role and a dedicated CloudHSM cluster in an isolated account.
[12:02] Priya Ramesh: After the lab we want an evidence package for the auditors — logs, attestation, and a runbook signed by our security owner.
[12:10] Riley Patel: Understood. We'll assemble the package and include a step-by-step remediation checklist.
[12:14] Ava Chen: Awesome. Anything else?
[12:17] Sofia Alvarez: One ask — can you include cost ballpark: the extra cost of CloudHSM and the operational overhead for private deployment? We need to justify to finance.
[12:27] Ava Chen: We'll include a simple cost sheet with expected CloudHSM hourly and Redwood private node pricing.
[12:33] Marcus Lee: End-to-end, this looks feasible. Thanks everyone.
[12:37] Ava Chen: Thanks — we'll follow up with the artifacts by Friday.

End transcript.

References & links mentioned during call:
- PKCS#11 connector README (to be sent)
- SOC2 Type II report link (to be attached)
- Private deployment hardening checklist (to be sent)

title: Quantization stability & SLO tradeoffs - KV warmup, batching interaction, on-prem considerations
preview:
Meeting header: 2026-10-07 15:30 UTC | Duration: ~72 min | Attendees: Samir (Redwood AE), Ava (Redwood SE), Priya (Nimbus ML Infra), Marco (Nimbus SRE), Helen (Nimbus Eng Lead)

Summary (auto): technical deep dive on quantization-induced stability and SLO impact. Discussed KV cache warmup strategies, batching interactions that amplify apparent accuracy regressions after INT8/AWQ conversion, on-prem networking considerations, and a plan for a warmup + regression benchmark. Agreed to exchange prompt sets and run a 24-hour warmup test.

00:00 Samir (Redwood AE): Alright everyone, thanks for joining — we'll run about an hourish deep dive on the quantization artifacts you saw, focusing on KV cache warmup, batching, and how that shows up in on-prem setups. Ava will lead the bench details.
00:22 Ava (Redwood SE): Hi hi, thanks. Quick ask before we start: Priya, can you confirm the model variant you're running? You mentioned a 7B variant but I want the exact— is it the community gpt-7b-xl or the custom-tuned fork?
00:36 Priya (Nimbus): It's the gpt-seven-b fork with a tiny finetune for medical parsing. Sorry my shorthand, it's the -med-tune variant.
00:44 Ava: Cool, that matters because kernel selection and quant mapping can differ a bit. Also are you on TF32/FP16 baseline or pure FP32 currently?
00:56 Priya: Baseline was FP16 on our on-prem A100s, we tried INT8 via gptq and then AWQ as an alternative. The first thing we noticed was sporadic hallucination spikes on longer prompts.
01:14 Samir: Got it. Could you describe the workload pattern — short chatty requests vs long document decoding?
01:22 Marco (Nimbus SRE): Mostly multi-turn, often warm-started from stored prefixes, so KV cache reuse is common. Peak QPS is moderate — 45-60 reqs/s — but tail latency is critical: 95th under 200ms.
01:38 Ava: That tail SLO is helpful. We often see quantization regressions amplified at tail due to batching and stale KV cache interactions. I'll sketch that out now.
02:05 Ava: (screen share ref) Simplified flow: request arrives -> router picks GPU -> runtime may batch -> KV cache hit/miss -> decode. If quant change shifts logits slightly, early tokens can cause cache divergence — leading to different cached states and compounded error.
02:35 Priya: Sorry, explain \"cache divergence\" again? We thought KV cache was just bytes saved, why would quantization change that?
02:44 Ava: Totally. So KV cache stores per-step keys/values. If the quantized model produces different hidden activations even by small epsilon, the keys/values differ. Downstream, when those cached tokens are reused as prefix, the newer values are not bit-identical, so their contribution changes, and errors can accumulate over a long decode.
03:05 Priya: Okay that makes sense, small numeric shift cascades.
03:12 Samir: Also worth noting kernel ordering and rounding differences across A100 drivers — sometimes the same quant file behaves slightly differently across driver versions.
03:26 Marco: We had one node that showed better accuracy than others; I assumed hardware flakiness. Could driver mismatch be the cause?
03:34 Ava: Absolutely. We've seen mismatched cuBLAS/cuDNN/kernel selection cause tiny differences. We add a checksum step in our deploy to detect cross-node differences.
03:50 Helen (Nimbus): On batching — we use dynamic batching to keep latency down. Is there any rule-of-thumb how batch size interacts with INT8/AWQ?
04:03 Ava: Two effects: first, effective sequence alignment inside batch can cause different per-step compute paths. Second, batching increases the chance a request will read a KV entry written under a different numeric regime if you're mixing model variants. For pure single-variant deploys, larger batches mainly affect latency, not accuracy, but they can amplify timing jitter which sometimes looks like accuracy noise.
04:30 Priya: When you say \"different numeric regime\" do you mean we might mix fp16 and int8 in routing?
04:37 Samir: Yes, in hybrid setups we route to fallbacks or canaries that might be quantized differently. That mixed traffic is a big vector for apparent regression.
04:48 Marco: We do have canary routing during rollout, but we didn't realize it could cause cache contamination.
04:55 Ava: Exactly — if a canary writes keys to a shared KV layer or if sticky routing isn't strict, subsequent requests hitting another variant will see the stale keys. In Redwood private we isolate KV per variant or use per-instance caches to avoid this.
05:20 Priya: Our on-prem config is shared NVMe-backed cache to save memory, so it's shared across pods. Hmm.
05:29 Ava: That explains a lot. Shared persistent caches are higher risk unless you version the cache key namespace with model id+quant tag.
05:40 Samir: Quick aside on warmup: we recommend a multi-phase warmup. Short 1-2 hour synthetic warmup is often insufficient. The patterns you care about — long multi-turn prefixes — take longer to populate and surface staleness.
06:00 Priya: We did a 2 hour warmup and thought we were fine. You're suggesting 24 hours?
06:07 Ava: At scale, yes. Especially if you have a low-frequency long-tail prompt. We propose: 1) synthetic warmup to exercise kernels; 2) replay of production prefixes for 24h to populate the KV. That surface regressions that only appear after repeated reuse.
06:26 Marco: Sound doable. Does that require holding GPU memory for the KV longer? We worry about memory pressure for multi-tenant nodes.
06:34 Ava: You can use sticky cache populations: replay to each node and then GC older entries; or use a compact KV with truncation policy tuned to your prefix length distribution. Redwood's runtime supports max-kv-entries per model instance and LRU/TTL policies.
06:52 Priya: About AWQ vs GPTQ — we tried AWQ after GPTQ looked noisy. AWQ reduced some hallucinations but introduced a different slowness pattern. Is that expected?
07:05 Ava: Yes. AWQ uses asymmetric quantization which can preserve ranges better but may change rounding behavior — that can alter softmax dynamics, which can improve certain probabilities but also shift token ordering in edge cases. GPTQ sometimes underestimates outlier weights and can bias toward safer tokens but lose nuance. There's no one-size-fits-all.
07:35 Samir: Also inference kernel choices differ — AWQ gets a different optimized kernel path that may have slightly higher latency per token. We can show kernel-level traces.
07:48 Marco: If AWQ adds latency, that could push your 95th over 200ms in our traffic. Any mitigations?
07:57 Ava: Several: 1) micro-batching (we use continuous batching with dynamic max batch size), 2) prefix caching to avoid recomputing earlier layers, 3) model pipelining or using faster kernel for early layers and AWQ for later layers — hybrid precision. But hybrid introduces complexity in correctness.
08:25 Priya: Hybrid precision — do you mean fp16 for first N layers, int8 for last layers?
08:33 Ava: Correct. Often earlier layers contain more signal, later layers can be quantized more aggressively. But you have to maintain KV key format compatibility. If your keys are created by mixed-precision runs, reuse must be consistent.
08:49 Priya: That's scary for us. We're constrained to deterministic outputs for regulatory reasons in some workflows.
08:56 Samir: For regulatory paths, we suggest canarying with deterministic checksums and automated rollback. Also document the acceptable delta thresholds for token distributions.
09:10 Helen: On deterministic checksums — do you calc over logits or final tokens?
09:15 Ava: Both. We do a per-token logit-delta histogram and a final-token-match rate. For your use case, final-token-match plus key-checksums for KV should be required.
09:27 Marco: You mentioned earlier that driver versions can shift kernels. How strict should our infra be?
09:33 Ava: Pin GPU driver, CUDA, and runtime libs across nodes for production. We recommend a canary pool with the exact same stack and periodic checksum runs across nodes to catch drift.
09:45 Priya: Question on batching: we observed that when we moved from batch size 1 to 4, some prompts suddenly regressed. Should we just keep batch size 1?
09:55 Ava: Not necessarily. Try to align sequence lengths inside batch (bucketing) to reduce padding-induced artifacts and use per-request sequence-aware caching. Also enable per-request latency caps — Redwood supports tail-targeted batching where requests close to SLO avoid joining big batches.
10:18 Samir: Another gotcha: random seed for sampling. If your eval uses nucleus sampling with temperature, small logit shifts change sample results dramatically. For deterministic evals, use greedy decode or fixed seeds where possible.
10:35 Priya: Our golden set uses stochastic decodes for nuance, so we can't just switch. We need an eval that mirrors production sampling.
10:44 Ava: Then compute distributional metrics (KL divergence, ngram overlap) and track changes rather than per-example token equality. That reduces noise sensitivity.
10:56 Marco: On on-prem networking — we expect 2ms ± jitter inside cluster, but occasionally nodes hit 10-20ms for NVMe reads. Does that matter for KV?
11:06 Ava: It does. If your KV is NVMe-backed and shared over network, latency spikes create asynchronous staleness windows. Those windows combined with batching can lead to a request reading slightly older keys than expected. For SLOs under 200ms, aim for sub-5ms stable KV access or local in-memory caches per instance with periodic persistence.
11:30 Priya: That's useful. Any suggestions for persistent cache namespace versioning?
11:36 Ava: Prefix cache keys with model-id:quant-tag:kernel-hash:deployment-id. That way, mismatched kernels or quant tags never reuse another variant's keys.
11:50 Priya: Good. We'll need that change.
11:54 Samir: Quick break — we'll pivot to the proposed benchmark plan. Ava?
12:00 Ava: Plan: 1) Baseline FP16 steady-state run with your production prompts to capture golden outputs. 2) Apply GPTQ and AWQ variants, run an isolated 24h replay of real prefixes across nodes to populate KV. 3) During replay, measure per-token logit deltas, final token divergence, p95/p99 latency, and kernel-level timings. 4) Run a staggered warmdown to see if regressions persist after GC.
12:34 Marco: For the 24h replay, can we limit to a subset of prefixes to reduce load?
12:39 Ava: Yes, pick the heavy-weight long-tail prefixes and high-risk prompt families. The goal is to replicate reuse patterns. Priya, that's where your example prompt sets come in.
12:51 Priya: We'll assemble three buckets: short chatty, long-form multi-turn medical case, and adversarial edge cases. We'll include golden outputs for each.
13:03 Samir: On measurement: we'll provide a small script to compute per-step KL and token-match, plus a dashboard with kernel traces. We'll also surface a simple signal: consecutive-run regression count (how many examples show worse metric vs baseline).
13:19 Helen: About staging: we prefer not to hit production GPUs for this. Should we spin a mirror cluster?
13:26 Ava: For best fidelity, mirror cluster with same driver stack is ideal. If cost is a constraint, we can run sequential replays on each node in smaller batches but that lengthens wall time.
13:38 Marco: We'll try to provision a 4-node mirror.
13:42 Priya: Any fast wins to try before the full run?
13:46 Ava: Yes: 1) Namespace the KV to prevent cross-variant contamination. 2) Pin drivers. 3) Run a 6h focused replay on the long-form bucket and inspect KL deltas — often that surfaces the major issues.
14:03 Samir: Let's capture the potential root cause matrix: numeric precision changes, kernel selection, KV cross-variant contamination, batching alignment, NVMe network jitter, and sampling stochasticity. We'll rank them by probability once we see the trace.
14:18 Priya: One more question: do you have an existing tool for per-token logit histograms? Or do we need to build?
14:25 Ava: Redwood has an eval agent that can instrument per-layer logits and produce histograms; we'll provide a minimal config to run it and a small collector script. We'll attach to the follow-up.
14:36 Marco: Security question — for on-prem pilot, how will Redwood access our cluster? We prefer SSH jump with restricted keys, no open inbound.
14:44 Samir: We support agent-based connectors or a temporary bastion. For sensitive clusters we can do air-gapped data exchange: you run the benchmark and upload the logs to a secure bucket for us to analyze. We'll share the security checklist after this call.
14:59 Helen: KMS integration — we use in-house HSM. Is that supported for model encryption or just key wrapping for artifacts?
15:06 Ava: We support KMS/HSM integration for key wrapping of model artifacts and for traffic to private control plane in Redwood Private. For on-prem, we can integrate via standard PKCS11/KMS adapters. We'll add docs.
15:20 Priya: Great.
15:25 Samir: Okay we are nearing the end. Quick recap: Redwood will run the proposed warmup + 24h replay on a mirror cluster (Ava), Nimbus will share prompt sets and provision mirror nodes (Priya/Marco), Samir will send the security and topology checklist and price ballpark. We'll reconvene with benchmark results mid-next week.
15:45 Helen: One tiny nit — when you run the bench, can you also include a run with pinned seed greedy decode so we can separate sampling noise from systematic drift?
15:53 Ava: Yep, we'll include greedy and stochastic modes.
15:57 Marco: And can you log kernel version/hash with each result?
16:01 Ava: Yes, kernel and driver checksum will be attached.
16:05 Priya: Thanks. That was useful.
16:09 Samir: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
16:12 (end)

Action items and artifacts referenced: 
- Redwood benchmark plan doc (link): https://redwood-internal/example-benchmarks/kv-warmup-plan (redwood shared link)
- Security checklist (will be sent): includes KMS adapters, PKCS11 notes, required ports and bastion setup
- Eval scripts: redwood-eval-agent v2.3 (attached in CRM record)

Notes on transcription: few speaker label swaps at 03:00-03:20 where automated system labeled Samir as Speaker 1; some technical tokens (AWQ/GPTQ/drivers) mis-transcribed in spots as AQW/GPP-TQ.

12:34 Marco: For the 24h replay, can we limit to a subset of prefixes to reduce load?
12:39 Ava: Yes, pick the heavy-weight long-tail prefixes and high-risk prompt families. The goal is to replicate reuse patterns. Priya, that's where your example prompt sets come in.
12:51 Priya: We'll assemble three buckets: short chatty, long-form multi-turn medical case, and adversarial edge cases. We'll include golden outputs for each.
13:03 Samir: On measurement: we'll provide a small script to compute per-step KL and token-match, plus a dashboard with kernel traces. We'll also surface a simple signal: consecutive-run regression count (how many examples show worse metric vs baseline).
13:19 Helen: About staging: we prefer not to hit production GPUs for this. Should we spin a mirror cluster?
13:26 Ava: For best fidelity, mirror cluster with same driver stack is ideal. If cost is a constraint, we can run sequential replays on each node in smaller batches but that lengthens wall time.
13:38 Marco: We'll try to provision a 4-node mirror.
13:42 Priya: Any fast wins to try before the full run?
13:46 Ava: Yes: 1) Namespace the KV to prevent cross-variant contamination. 2) Pin drivers. 3) Run a 6h focused replay on the long-form bucket and inspect KL deltas — often that surfaces the major issues.
14:03 Samir: Let's capture the potential root cause matrix: numeric precision changes, kernel selection, KV cross-variant contamination, batching alignment, NVMe network jitter, and sampling stochasticity. We'll rank them by probability once we see the trace.
14:18 Priya: One more question: do you have an existing tool for per-token logit histograms? Or do we need to build?
14:25 Ava: Redwood has an eval agent that can instrument per-layer logits and produce histograms; we'll provide a minimal config to run it and a small collector script. We'll attach to the follow-up.
14:36 Marco: Security question — for on-prem pilot, how will Redwood access our cluster? We prefer SSH jump with restricted keys, no open inbound.
14:44 Samir: We support agent-based connectors or a temporary bastion. For sensitive clusters we can do air-gapped data exchange: you run the benchmark and upload the logs to a secure bucket for us to analyze. We'll share the security checklist after this call.
14:59 Helen: KMS integration — we use in-house HSM. Is that supported for model encryption or just key wrapping for artifacts?
15:06 Ava: We support KMS/HSM integration for key wrapping of model artifacts and for traffic to private control plane in Redwood Private. For on-prem, we can integrate via standard PKCS11/KMS adapters. We'll add docs.
15:20 Priya: Great.
15:25 Samir: Okay we are nearing the end. Quick recap: Redwood will run the proposed warmup + 24h replay on a mirror cluster (Ava), Nimbus will share prompt sets and provision mirror nodes (Priya/Marco), Samir will send the security and topology checklist and price ballpark. We'll reconvene with benchmark results mid-next week.
15:45 Helen: One tiny nit — when you run the bench, can you also include a run with pinned seed greedy decode so we can separate sampling noise from systematic drift?
15:53 Ava: Yep, we'll include greedy and stochastic modes.
15:57 Marco: And can you log kernel version/hash with each result?
16:01 Ava: Yes, kernel and driver checksum will be attached.
16:05 Priya: Thanks. That was useful.
16:09 Samir: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the artifacts.
16:12 (end)

Action items and artifacts referenced: 
- Redwood benchmark plan doc (link): https://redwood-internal/example-benchmarks/kv-warmup-plan (redwood shared link)
- Security checklist (will be sent): includes KMS adapters, PKCS11 notes, required ports and bastion setup
- Eval scripts: redwood-eval-agent v2.3 (attached in CRM record)

Notes on transcription: few speaker label swaps at 03:00-03:20 where automated system labeled Samir as Speaker 1; some technical tokens (AWQ/GPTQ/drivers) mis-transcribed in spots as AQW/GPP-TQ.

title: Strandline Integrators GPU onramp framing
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-10-06
Time: 16:00 PT
Duration: 63 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen, Marco Rivera, Sofia Patel, Jamal Ng (Redwood); Priya Malhotra, Daniel Ko, Liam O'Sullivan, Ema Gomez (Strandline)

Summary:
Quick partner intro + technical discovery to frame a GPU onramp pilot: network/VPC requirements, model hosting options (hosted vs private), latency targets, pricing bands, integration ownership. Agree to run an end-to-end benchmark and produce a pilot SOW.

Topics:
- Strandline's current infra and why they're exploring partner onramps
- Redwood Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs
- Networking, KMS, and compliance concerns
- Pilot scope, timeline, and success metrics

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ava Chen (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for carving out time. Quick intros, then Marco will go deeper on the runtime stuff.
[00:12] Priya Malhotra (Strandline): Hi, Priya here, Head of Solutions at Strandline. We're exploring ways to accelerate customer deployments for our AI-driven document processing platform. Latency and cost are big levers for us.
[00:25] Daniel Ko (Strandline CTO): daniel here — key constraints: multi-region customers in eu/us, need for encryption at rest, and we'd prefer partner-managed capacity to avoid owning the full stack.
[00:37] Marco Rivera (Redwood SE): Great. Quick framing — Redwood offers hosted API, dedicated reserved capacity, and private VPC deployments. For partner onramp, common pattern is Redwood Dedicated with VPC peering or Private with a partner-managed control plane.
[00:55] Ema Gomez (Infra): A couple clarifying q's up front — do you support bringing your own GPU instances or do we have to use Redwood-provisioned infra?
[01:05] Jamal Ng (Redwood Partner Eng): Good q. We support both patterns typically. For a partner-managed onramp you can run our runtime inside partner infra (onsite or cloud) with a private control plane, or we can reserve capacity in our pools and give you predictable throughput. There's some tradeoffs in terms of patching and SLA.
[01:20] Liam O'Sullivan (Strandline): What's the latency delta between Dedicated vs Private — ballpark?
[01:26] Marco Rivera (Redwood SE): If you're colocated in the same region and peered, Private usually gives slightly better tail latency because fewer hops, but Dedicated with dedicated pools is often within single-digit ms difference depending on model and sequence length. For small seq lengths and chat workloads it's negligible. For batch/throughput you might prefer Dedicated.
[01:50] Priya: Our main workload is short sequence extraction and reranking — so tail latency matters. We also want to avoid long onboarding for customers.
[01:59] Sofia Patel (Redwood SE): That maps well to prefix/KV caching and continuous batching — we can show config that keeps 99th percentile low while still batching enough to save cost. We'll propose quantization profiles too, for cost/QoS balance.
[02:18] Daniel: On security — we need KMS integration and audit logs, SSO for devops, and data residency for some customers. Any red flags?
[02:28] Jamal: We integrate with customer KMS/HSM options via standard keywrap flows; audit logs are supported in the console and can be forwarded to your SIEM. For private deployments we can respect data residency as workloads run in your region. We'll send the SOC2 and security brief.
[02:45] Ava: I'll share the security one-pager after the call.
[02:48] Ema: Cool. Networking — what's the simplest path for us to do an initial pilot? We don't want to open up too many inbound rules.
[02:58] Marco: Two pragmatic approaches: 1) Redwood spins up a dedicated pool in the region and you call our API (hosted/dedicated) — minimal network changes, just outbound HTTPS from your services. 2) For private, we set up private connectivity (VPC peering or Transit Gateway) and run a control plane that talks to your infra. Option 1 is faster for pilot, option 2 gives stronger isolation.
[03:22] Priya: So for pilot let's do option 1 to validate latency/throughput, then we can move to private if needed. Is that safe from compliance perspective?
[03:30] Jamal: For most pilots it's fine; we can limit logs retention and ensure data in-transit is encrypted. But for regulated customers we'd push for private earlier.
[03:40] Liam: Pricing — any rough bands for Dedicated pilot?
[03:44] Ava: We'll send a pilot pricing band — it's typically monthly reserved pricing with a pilot discount. We'll include per-token benches so you can model cost across your expected QPS.
[03:55] [04:00] Marco: I'd like to understand your current model and runtime. Are you primarily using open LLMs or proprietary models? That changes the kernel/quantization choices.
[04:10] Daniel: We're leaning open. Using a mix of Llama2 derivatives and some small proprietary reranker models. We want to quantize to int8 ideally, but worried about accuracy delta.
[04:22] Sofia: Int8 quantization is usually viable for rerankers, but we'll run regression evals. Redwood Optimize can suggest quant profiles per model and give an estimated quality delta.
[04:35] Priya: We'd like to see an end-to-end bench — from request to token output — with our sample dataset. Can Redwood do that or do we run locally?
[04:44] Marco: We'll run a bench from Redwood side against our Dedicated pool and also provide the test harness so you can run in your environment. That lets us compare network delta. We'll need a small sample dataset and a representative prompt set.
[05:00] Ema: I'll provide a sanitized dataset — we can't share PII.
[05:05] Daniel: What about model version pinning and rollback — we need deterministic behavior in production.
[05:11] Jamal: Redwood supports model pinning and rollouts with canary/A-B. Rollback is immediate to previous pinned version. We'll demo the rollout console in the pilot.
[05:22] Liam: Any caveats around open-source license compliance when we package models across customers?
[05:28] Marco: We'll document recommended licensing for each model; for the pilot we'll help identify permissive options.
[05:35] Priya: Who owns the infra ops during the pilot?
[05:38] Ava: For hosted/dedicated pilot, Redwood owns the runtime infra. You'll own integration and orchestration on your side. For private onramp, we collaborate on ops — partner-run infra, Redwood provides the control plane and runtime image. We'll put responsibilities in SOW.
[05:55] [06:00] Daniel: OK timeline wise — we're aiming for an initial pilot in ~3 weeks. Is that realistic?
[06:06] Marco: Yes — if we get the dataset and VPC details quickly we can spin Dedicated pilot in 1-2 weeks and run benches the following week.
[06:14] Ema: We'll confirm network IPs and ephemeral keys.
[06:18] Priya: For success metrics, we'll track 99th pct latency under 350ms for inference, and a cost-per-1k-instructions target. Also model quality regressions must be <2% on our eval set.
[06:30] Sofia: Noted. We'll include those in pilot SOW.
[06:34] [06:40] Conversation digression — quick demo ask
[06:41] Liam: Can you show a quick sketch of how reranking flows through Redwood?
[06:45] Marco: Sure — (screen-share described) client -> Redwood API -> model (reranker) -> tokens -> optional KV cache -> return. We also support streaming and function-calls though rerankers typically are synchronous.
[07:10] Priya: About caching — if we have many similar prefixes, can Redwood cache at prefix-level?
[07:16] Marco: Yes, prefix and KV cache strategies are supported. Cache invalidation semantics vary by use-case; we'll propose a pattern for your workload.
[07:26] [07:30] Security deepening
[07:31] Daniel: For audit logs we need per-tenant segregation. Is that possible?
[07:36] Jamal: Yes, tenant-level logs and token-level cost breakdowns are available. We can forward logs to your SIEM as well.
[07:45] Ema: KMS integration — can we bring our HSM via AWS CloudHSM?
[07:49] Sofia: We support AWS KMS and CloudHSM integrations for keys used to encrypt data at rest. For private deployments, the keys remain in your boundary.
[07:58] [08:05] Priya: Any notable risks or blockers you've seen with other partners?
[08:10] Marco: Typical friction points: network egress rules (some partners forget outbound HTTPS rules), model licensing (some models have restrictions), and storage of logs for regulated customers. None are blockers, mostly process.
[08:24] Liam: Competitor question — How does this compare to CloudX GPUs where we've previously tested?
[08:29] Ava: Many customers find Redwood's ops abstractions and rollouts simpler; CloudX requires more infra glue. Performance is similar if tuned, but Redwood surfaces templates for batching/quantization which speeds time-to-healthy.
[08:45] [09:00] Pilot mechanics and deliverables
[09:01] Marco: Deliverables we'll provide: pilot SOW, integration diagram, benchmark script, sample monitoring dashboards, and a security pack (SOC2 + KMS notes). We'll also propose a 4-week pilot timeline in the SOW.
[09:18] Priya: Good. Onboarding owners — who will be main POC at Redwood?
[09:22] Ava: Primary POC will be me for commercial + Jamal for partner engineering and Marco/Sofia for SE work.
[09:29] Daniel: And who signs off on production readiness?
[09:32] Ava: We'll outline a checklist in SOW; typically Strandline signs off on metrics and Redwood signs off on infra stability.
[09:40] [09:50] Quick Q&A and small clarifications
[09:51] Ema: For the pilot, what access do you need to our telemetry? Logs?
[09:56] Marco: We ask for sample request traces and a subset of telemetry; everything can be sanitized. For full production, we recommend a forwarder to your SIEM rather than sharing raw logs.
[10:05] Priya: About SLA for Dedicated — what are the uptime numbers?
[10:09] Ava: We'll include SLA options in the contract; our Dedicated SLA is typically 99.9% with credits for major incidents. Private SLAs are customizable.
[10:20] Liam: Any gotchas for multi-region failover?
[10:24] Sofia: Multi-region routing is supported; common pattern is active-active with smart routing and fallbacks to lower-capacity variants if a region degrades. We'll show that in the rollout plan.
[10:40] [11:00] Closing and Recap
[11:01] Ava: Recap — Strandline wants a quick Dedicated pilot (option 1) to validate latency and cost using your sanitized dataset. Redwood to provide SOW, bench scripts, security pack. Deliverables and action items as captured.
[11:14] Priya: Sounds good. We'll provide the dataset and network details this week. Aim to start benching in two weeks.
[11:21] Ava: Great — thanks everyone. We'll follow up with the SOW and the security docs.
[11:26] Meeting ends.

Notes:
- Shared artifacts mentioned: pilot SOW template (link: https://redwood.ai/pilot-sow-template), security one-pager (attaching after call), benchmark harness (will be shared as repo link).
- Slight transcription noise: a few names misheard (e.g., Liam sometimes logged as Leon by auto-transcript).

End of transcript.

title: Supplier onboarding: MSA/DPA, SSO & KMS escalation intake
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-10-05
Start time (UTC): 15:00
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees (Redwood): Ava Chen (AE), Marcus Reed (SE), Priya Kapoor (CSM)
Attendees (Customer): Michael Turner (Procurement), Sofia Alvarez (Legal), Rajesh Iyer (Platform Security), Hannah Park (DevOps), Ethan Li (Eng Manager)
Shared links: onboarding checklist (redwood.com/onboard/chs), SOC2 report (link provided by Marcus at 00:06:12), draft pricing sheet (placeholder)

Summary (auto):
Initial procurement intake to escalate MSA/DPA and KMS/SSO requirements. Customer requests SOC2, KMS/HSM proof, SSO/SAML support, VPC/private deployment options, and pricing scenarios for hosted vs dedicated. Discussed timelines, data residency, and procurement gating. Agreed next steps and action items.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. I'm Ava from Redwood, account executive, and with me are Marcus, our SE, and Priya from CSM.
[00:07] Michael Turner: Hi, Michael here, head of procurement at Cobalt Health. Thanks, Ava. We're hoping to get an intake on the commercial and security path today.
[00:14] Sofia Alvarez: Sofia, legal couns— Counsel. I'll be focused on the DPA/MSA redlines and any data residency obligations.
[00:19] Rajesh Iyer: Rajesh from platform security. I'll focus on encryption at rest, KMS integration, and audit logging.
[00:24] Hannah Park: Hannah, DevOps. We'll talk about network, VPC peering, and pilot runbook.
[00:28] Ava Chen: Great. Quick agenda: align on MSA/DPA expectations, SSO/KMS needs, decide whether a private deployment is required for pilot, and outline pricing and procurement timeline.
[00:36] Marcus Reed: I'll jump in on the technical pieces — what our dedicated and private options look like and how KMS integration works.
[00:42] Michael Turner: We have, uh, an accelerated procurement window. Targeting procurement sign-off in mid-October if the legal and security boxes are checked. That's why this call is urgent.
[00:50] Sofia Alvarez: Quick note, we usually require a vendor to accept certain DPA clauses — subprocessors, international transfer mechanisms, and breach notification timelines — before commercial sign-off.
[00:59] Ava Chen: Totally understood. For context, Redwood's standard DPA covers subprocessors and notification, but we're open to narrow redlines. Marcus will attach the latest SOC2 doc and our DPA template after the call.
[01:12] Rajesh Iyer: On encryption, we need to know whether Redwood can integrate with our KMS (AWS KMS + custom HSM) for key custody, or if Redwood manages keys. We have strict key custody rules for PHI-adjacent workloads.
[01:27] Marcus Reed: Short answer: we support both models. Hosted customers typically use Redwood-managed keys with envelope encryption; for private/VPC deployments we can support customer-managed keys using your KMS — AWS KMS or an HSM via our Cloud HSM connectors. There are options for a bring-your-own-key (BYOK) flow.
[01:43] Hannah Park: Is there an HSM certification or attestation we can see? We need to show compliance to our auditors.
[01:49] Marcus Reed: We have a hardware-HSM integration guide and attestations — I'll send the whitepaper and a summary of our integration. We don't ship an HSM to customers, but our connectors support CloudHSM.
[01:58] Sofia Alvarez: And what about audit logs? We need immutable logs for request/response metadata plus admin actions — retention terms and exportability.
[02:07] Marcus Reed: We provide audit logs accessible via the console and can push logs into your SIEM (Splunk/Datadog/CloudWatch). Retention is configurable; enterprise tiers include longer retention and export APIs.
[02:17] Michael Turner: Okay, procurement wants an SLA for availability plus incident notification timelines. Typical ask is 99.9% uptime and 4-hour initial incident acknowledgement. Can you accommodate that or is that enterprise-only?
[02:29] Ava Chen: Dedicated and Private customers get SLA commitments; hosted API has published SLOs but enterprise SLA adds credits and faster response times. We can map the 99.9% and 4-hour ack into a contract for Dedicated or Private with the right commitment level.
[02:40] Rajesh Iyer: We also need to confirm data residency — certain datasets must remain within the EU region for regulatory reasons. Do you support region pinning?
[02:49] Marcus Reed: Yes, region pinning is supported on Dedicated and Private. For hosted, we can route to regional endpoints but not guaranteed physical isolation unless you choose a Dedicated region or Private deployment.
[03:00] Hannah Park: For the pilot, we'd like to do a small private deployment on GCP region EU-west1 or an on-prem VM if necessary. What's the minimum footprint?
[03:09] Marcus Reed: Minimum for a pilot private deployment is typically a single-node inference cluster with X GPUs — we can do a constrained pilot with a single 40GB GPU depending on model size. For strict on-prem, we'll need a runbook and a tech kickoff.
[03:22] Ethan Li: For latency, our product expects sub-200ms P50 for short completions. How does Redwood guarantee those numbers under a dedicated pool?
[03:30] Marcus Reed: We tune kernel selection, batching, and KV cache to meet P50 — dedicated pools with pinned capacity are the recommended path. We'll run a short benchmark with your workload to validate.
[03:38] Michael Turner: Can you run a benchmark with our 10k monthly users profile before legal signs? Procurement needs a performance doc to justify cost.
[03:45] Ava Chen: Yes, we can run a benchmark as part of the pilot. Typically that's gated by an NDA and a short PO, but we can coordinate an SE-led runbook to validate.
[03:55] Sofia Alvarez: On contract structure: our legal prefers limited exceptions on data processing terms. Is Redwood open to a mutual indemnity carveout for regulator-driven data access requests?
[04:06] Ava Chen: We can escalate that. Our enterprise legal has handled carveouts with other health customers; Marcus and I can take specific language back to legal.
[04:15] Rajesh Iyer: One more technical question: do you support SAML 2.0 for SSO and SCIM provisioning? We want RBAC integrated with our IDP.
[04:23] Marcus Reed: Yes, SAML 2.0 and SCIM are supported. Enterprise customers get SSO, SCIM provisioning, and granular RBAC. For Private deployments, we can integrate directly with your AD/Okta.
[04:35] Hannah Park: If we choose hosted initially and later move to dedicated, how much rework is involved for our config, secrets, and keys?
[04:43] Marcus Reed: We try to make transitions seamless — configuration and model pins move with minimal changes. Key custody transitions require a rotation plan. We document the migration steps in our runbook.
[04:52] Michael Turner: Procurement is looking at three pricing scenarios: pay-as-you-go hosted, committed dedicated capacity for 12 months, and a private deployment licensing model. Can you provide ballpark pricing?
[05:03] Ava Chen: We'll send a line-item pricing sheet. Ballpark: hosted per-token rates, dedicated discounted per-token with minimum commit, private has a platform fee plus support. We'll give examples based on your traffic.
[05:12] Hannah Park: Onboarding time — what's realistic for a pilot if legal signs within two weeks?
[05:18] Priya Kapoor: From CSM perspective, once we have a PO and legal greenlight, a hosted pilot can start in 3-5 business days; a private pilot/cluster typically takes 2-3 weeks depending on infra approvals.
[05:29] Sofia Alvarez: Also need phone/email escalation contacts and an RTO for critical issues.
[05:34] Ava Chen: We'll include a support escalation matrix in the SLA appendix.
[05:38] Rajesh Iyer: For breach notifications, we need a 72-hour disclosure requirement in the DPA. Is that acceptable?
[05:44] Ava Chen: Our DPA standard is 72 hours; we'll confirm the wording in the redline.
[05:48] Michael Turner: Great. Procurement also asked about vendor subprocessor lists and whether Redwood will notify us of changes.
[05:55] Marcus Reed: We maintain a subprocessor list and can include notification windows; it's in the DPA template.
[06:00] Hannah Park: Quick tangent — any known limitations on model sizes or token limits for private deployments?
[06:06] Marcus Reed: We support most open models and many popular opt variants. For very large models (>70B) you'll need larger GPU nodes; we'll advise on feasibility.
[06:16] Ethan Li: Regarding logs, can we get request-level metadata but redact user content for PII automatically before logs land in our SIEM?
[06:24] Marcus Reed: We can configure log redaction and supply metadata-only logs; detailed inspection would require agreement on what fields are redacted.
[06:32] Sofia Alvarez: On the MSA, we typically require a 60-day termination for convenience for certain vendors — is that negotiable?
[06:40] Ava Chen: That's a legal negotiation point; many enterprise customers have similar asks. We'll bring the proposal to Redwood legal.
[06:46] Michael Turner: Okay, let's cover procurement gating: if legal accepts DPA redlines and security signs SOC2 + KMS attestation, procurement can issue a PO within 5 business days.
[06:56] Priya Kapoor: To help, Marcus and I will prepare an onboarding checklist tailored to Cobalt Health with timelines and responsibilities.
[07:03] Ava Chen: We'll also include a pilot support SLA and a pricing appendix with the three scenarios.
[07:08] Rajesh Iyer: For the BYOK flow, what's the key rotation process? We need rotation without downtime.
[07:15] Marcus Reed: We support rotation with a double-write period and a key rollover procedure that doesn't drop in-flight requests; details in the BYOK runbook.
[07:24] Hannah Park: Is there a sample runbook? Can we review it before committing?
[07:28] Marcus Reed: Yes, I'll upload a sample and call out change windows.
[07:33] Michael Turner: One small procurement ask — can you provide references from other health customers with similar KMS needs?
[07:39] Ava Chen: We can provide references, subject to customer permission. We'll get that in the packet.
[07:44] Sofia Alvarez: Regarding indemnity, if regulators subpoena data, we need clarity on responsibilities and notification timelines.
[07:51] Ava Chen: Noted — we'll have legal address regulator-driven requests in the MSA redlines.
[07:56] Priya Kapoor: A practical next step: we'll schedule the tech deep dive next week — Marcus suggested the week of Oct 12. Does that work?
[08:04] Hannah Park: That works for DevOps.
[08:06] Ethan Li: Also, we need to lock down the expected throughput shape — bursts vs steady-state. I'll send a spreadsheet.
[08:12] Marcus Reed: Perfect — that will help determine whether dedicated or private is the better economic fit.
[08:16] Michael Turner: Procurement note — we'll likely prefer committed dedicated if cost can be justified, but legal gating and security attestations are the main blockers.
[08:24] Ava Chen: Understood. Marcus and I will prioritize the DPA redline and SOC2 artifacts. We'll aim to get those out within 48 hours.
[08:33] Sofia Alvarez: Please mark any items that require legal escalation so we can loop in counsel.
[08:38] Ava Chen: Will do.
[08:41] [09:00 marker omitted — meeting nearing close]
[08:43] Marcus Reed: Quick note on pricing: we'll show a committed example with a 12-month term, a 20% discount off hosted rates, and an onboarding support fee for private clusters.
[08:52] Hannah Park: For the pilot, let's target a 2-week smoke test with 24/7 on-call for the first 72 hours.
[08:58] Priya Kapoor: We'll staff the CSM rotation for that.
[09:02] Michael Turner: That should satisfy procurement as long as we have contractual language backing it.
[09:07] Ava Chen: Okay, final recap:
- Redwood to deliver DPA/MSA redlines (Ava) by 2026-10-08.
- Marcus to upload SOC2 report, KMS/HSM integration whitepaper, BYOK runbook, and sample runbook by 2026-10-06.
- Priya to prepare onboarding checklist and pilot support plan by 2026-10-09.
- Customer (Hannah/Ethan) to share traffic profile and throughput spreadsheet by 2026-10-09.

[09:44] Meeting ended.

title: 2026-09-17-precision-fallback-scheduling-and-kv-coherency-onprem-audit
preview:
Meeting header: 2026-09-17 13:30 UTC | Duration: 63 minutes | Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marcus Lee (Redwood SE), Jordan Patel (Redwood SRE), Priya Singh (HelioMed ML Infra), Tomás Rivera (HelioMed Platforms), Elena Novak (HelioMed Data Sci), Rahul Desai (HelioMed Security)

Summary: deep technical audit of recent post-quantization accuracy regressions observed on HelioMed on-prem deployment. Topics: INT8 vs FP8 vs GPTQ/AWQ tradeoffs, KV cache coherency/staleness, batching and sticky sessions, fallback scheduling and routing in private/VPC, KMS/encryption constraints for onprem.

00:00:00 - Ava Chen: okay everyone, thanks for hopping on, we have 63 minutes on this, the plan is to run through what you saw in the regression, how we debugged on hosted, and then propose a fallback scheduling plan for your onprem stack. quick check, audio/ok?
00:00:10 - Priya Singh: yeah all good, thanks Ava, and yes Tome1s is here, Elena and Rahul as well, we'll jump in.
00:00:18 - Tome1s Rivera: hi, just to set context quick — we upgraded to AWQ-8 hybrid profile last month to cut token cost, saw a 3.5-6% accuracy delta on our downstream extraction tasks on some prompts and then some tail latency spikes after KV cache was enabled.
00:00:35 - Marcus Lee: okay, good, that's the core. when you say AWQ-8 hybrid, do you mean the AWQ per-layer mix that we shipped in the last bundle, or a custom quant profile?
00:00:44 - Tome1s Rivera: it's the Redwood-supplied AWQ-8 mix, default layer selection. we didn't hand-tune.
00:00:50 - Elena Novak: to add — the failing prompts are pretty specific, a lot of context-heavy tables where the KV cache should help, but it looks like some tokens are being dropped or hallucinated.
00:00:59 - Marcus Lee: right, interesting. two buckets of failure commonly: quant-induced distribution shift (accuracy) and KV cache staleness/coherency causing wrong state. we'll step through both.

00:01:15 - Jordan Patel: quick infra note — this is on HelioMed onprem in a VPC with a customer-managed KMS, and the nodes run our private runtime container, not the hosted control plane. network to KMS is via a NAT gateway, so there is occasional jitter.
00:01:30 - Rahul Desai: also important: SOC2 audit incoming Q4 so we need to keep keys in our KMS, no shared keys with Redwood.

00:01:45 - Ava Chen: noted. okay we recorded an initial profiling run on hosted where we repro'd the accuracy gap — on hosted we see AWQ performing almost on par with GPTQ for many examples, but certain long-context prompts show drift. could be layerwise rounding sensitivity.
00:02:08 - Priya Singh: side question: can Redwood run a hybrid onprem profile where only the last N layers are higher precision? we tried int8 everywhere and that failed worse.
00:02:20 - Marcus Lee: yeah that's actually our recommended pattern for safety — keep bottom layers lower precision, top layers int16/FP8 depending on speed/ram, we call it "top-preserve" in internal notes. For AWQ/GPTQ there's often a sweet spot around preserving last 6-8 layers at FP8-like precision.
00:02:38 - Tome1s Rivera: FP8? we thought FP8 hadn't been validated on our runtime on this GPU sku. are there caveats?
00:02:45 - Marcus Lee: good call — F P eight support is hardware and kernel dependent. on some older Ampere nodes, the FP8 emulation path is slower than the int8 path and introduces jitter. for your onprem node model, we saw mixed perf in our profiling. we can run a specialized kernel selection pass.

00:03:05 - Elena Novak: the other weirdness — when we enabled KV cache sticky sessions (to reduce latency), throughput dropped sporadically and sometimes the cache looked stale for a particular session; sequences diverged after 40-80 tokens.
00:03:18 - Ava Chen: sticky KV cache interactions with quantized state are subtle — if the KV cache stored values were produced under one precision profile and read under another (e.g., a failover to fallback model), mismatch can amplify error. did you see any routed fallbacks?
00:03:33 - Priya Singh: yes during load spikes we have a fallback rule to route to a lower-cost pool that was configured with GPTQ-int8; there's a model variant mismatch. so session was sticky but model change happened mid-session.
00:03:46 - Marcus Lee: that explains a lot — sticky session + model-variant switch is a known pathology. we log mismatches but it can slip by if KV cache keys are reused without invalidation.

00:04:02 - Tome1s Rivera: so is the suggestion to invalidate KV on model switch, or to keep model family consistent across fallback tiers?
00:04:10 - Marcus Lee: both are options. best is to have compatible quant profiles across fallback families or a graceful dequant/transform on the fly. invalidating KV is simplest but adds latency as cold start tokens regenerate. we proposed a "precision-fallback scheduling" plan: 1) prefer capacity-based fallbacks within the same quant family; 2) if model variant changes, either invalidate KV or replay a short prefix to warm cache; 3) maintain a per-session quant-tag so reads can detect stale cache.

00:04:45 - Priya Singh: per-session quant-tagging sounds promising but is it heavy for throughput?
00:04:51 - Marcus Lee: minimal — it's just a small header and a quick hash compare. overhead <1% in our benchmarks. the heavy part is on-hit transform if you need to convert KV values between numeric formats.

00:05:10 - Elena Novak: about accuracy — can we get a layerwise sensitivity map? the failing prompts felt like attention-head sensitivity higher in middle layers.

00:05:35 - Ava Chen: action: Marcus will produce side-by-side charts (we'll attach link) — note: internal link: https://redwood.internal/quant-profiles/helio-2026-09 (redwood link placeholder).

00:05:59 - Marcus Lee: sure — G P T Q is a per-layer, per-channel quant algorithm that minimizes reconstruction error for weights using fine-tuning-like calibration, tends to preserve token-wise logits well but increases memory. AWQ is a faster approximate approach that can pack more but sometimes introduces small biases in later attention computations. those biases can accumulate in long context and with cached K V they produce different sampling distributions.

00:06:32 - Jordan Patel: any mitigation on sampling side? temperature or logit bias adjustments?
00:06:36 - Marcus Lee: tuning sampling can mask symptomatic errors but doesn't fix upstream drift. we recommend guardrails: 1) preserve top layers at higher precision, 2) per-session quant-tagging, 3) sticky session invalidation on model switch, 4) lightweight on-the-fly dequant for critical heads (experimental).

00:06:55 - Priya Singh: you mentioned dequant on-the-fly, can that work inside our VPC without key exchange? we can't export raw model weights.
00:07:03 - Marcus Lee: yes, dequant transforms happen inside the runtime container, no keys leave your VPC. but we need permission for a profiling-shared container image. Jordan can provide S3/registry access.

00:07:18 - Tome1s Rivera: latency SLOs — we saw a 200ms tail increase after sticky KV was enabled and fallback occurred. is that expected?
00:07:26 - Ava Chen: tail latency arises from cold-cache replays plus dequant transforms if we convert KVs. Also NAT/KMS jitter you mentioned contributes. For a plan: we can prioritize low-latency fallback pool in network-proximate racks and pre-warm KV by replaying the last 16 tokens when switching.
00:07:45 - Rahul Desai: replaying tokens sounds like it requires re-ingestion of PHI, is that safe? our logs must not persist protected health info outside your audit scope.
00:07:54 - Ava Chen: good call — we'll implement replay as transient in-memory operations and never persist to Redwood control plane. we'll provide a whitepaper and SOC2 scope statement.

00:08:10 - Elena Novak: about batching — we use continuous batching in the runtime, sometimes batch coalescing delays short requests. do quant profiles change batching efficiency?
00:08:20 - Marcus Lee: yes, quant size affects kernel throughput and therefore ideal batch size. INT8 paths usually have higher tokens/sec but smaller effective batch sizes for low-latency. FP8 or mixed precision sometimes improves throughput for long sequences. there's a trade: throughput vs per-request latency. we'll share a batching recommendation matrix per profile.

00:08:42 - Priya Singh: on the evaluation harness, do you require us to anonymize production prompts? we can't share PHI.
00:08:49 - Ava Chen: yes please anonymize or use synthetic replacements. even a small set of canonicalized prompts that preserve structure is fine.

00:09:00 - Tome1s Rivera: show of hands: are we comfortable with invalidating KV on model switch for now? it will reduce some tail cases.
00:09:06 - Priya Singh: we prefer that as a first step. we'd accept some cold replays if it reduces hallucinations.
00:09:11 - Rahul Desai: security approves as long as metadata is not exfiltrated and KMS keys stay in our account.

00:09:20 - Marcus Lee: perfect. next let me dig into two concrete experiments: A) run GPTQ-int8 and AWQ-8 per-layer sensitivity on your failing set (to see which layers cause top divergences), B) run onprem benchmark with sticky KV disabled vs enabled with per-session quant-tag to measure divergence windows.
00:09:42 - Elena Novak: timeline?
00:09:45 - Marcus Lee: we can deliver initial charts in 7 days, and onprem benchmark needs access so likely 10-12 days.

00:09:55 - Ava Chen: let's pivot to deeper nitty gritty — the transcript log output you sent showed occasional token-normalization errors around punctuation tokens and bracketed tables. those often map to attention head sensitivity.
00:10:09 - Marcus Lee: yep, in the ablation we saw head 7 and 10 in layers 11-14 produce outsized KL when quantized. our mitigation is head-preserving dequant for those heads. it's experimental but low-cost if we only dequant those slices.

00:10:30 - Tome1s Rivera: is head-level dequant feasible in your runtime? I thought execution kernels were monolithic.
00:10:36 - Marcus Lee: the runtime supports per-slice kernels; we've used it for cache-hotspots and early exit. dequant for individual heads is okay but increases code-path variety and testing burden. Jordan: would you be ok with a canary?
00:10:50 - Jordan Patel: yes in canary cluster with telemetry we can do it. we just need image and runbook.

00:11:00 - Priya Singh: if we keep last 8 layers at FP8 and dequant two heads in layer 12, what is the expected token cost impact?
00:11:08 - Marcus Lee: ballpark: +8-12% per-token cost but often regains accuracy, so end-user error rate drops and overall effective cost can be neutral or positive. we'll provide a precise cost table with token math.

00:11:22 - Elena Novak: any known regression cases where GPTQ did worse than AWQ?
00:11:27 - Marcus Lee: sometimes GPTQ overfits the calibration set causing rare tokens to be mispredicted. AWQ smoother distribution helps out-of-distribution tokens. it's workload dependent.

00:11:40 - Ava Chen: switching topics: deployment hygiene — we recommended tagging model versions and storing quant profile metadata in your model registry. do you currently do that?
00:11:49 - Tome1s Rivera: we store model versions but not quant profile metadata, we rely on image naming convention which is flaky.
00:11:56 - Ava Chen: we'll share a lightweight schema for model artifact metadata that includes quant algorithm, per-layer preserved ranges, K V cache format version, and sampling params. that's critical for debugging in prod.

00:12:10 - Rahul Desai: from compliance, we need audit logs for any fallback that modifies precision or invalidates cache. can Redwood populate those logs into our SIEM?
00:12:18 - Ava Chen: yes Redwood Console has hooks for audit exports to SIEM/Syslog; for Private we can push logs to your S3 or log collector. note that for onprem we need to configure log sinks and retention — we'll provide default parsers.

00:12:34 - Marcus Lee: just to call out again — KV schema versioning is important. if you change KV numeric layout between runs, old caches may be incompatible. we include a KV header with schema version but if you disabled it in your build that's a gotcha.

00:12:49 - Tome1s Rivera: interesting, I think our runtime strips headers for perf, hmm.
00:12:53 - Priya Singh: we stripped it for size, but maybe that was a mistake.

00:13:21 - Marcus Lee: under memory fragmentation, prefer smaller micro-batches and more frequent evacuation. our runtime can do dynamic packing; we'll supply tuning knobs.

Topics: INT8 vs FP8 vs GPTQ/AWQ tradeoffs, KV cache coherency/staleness, batching and sticky sessions, fallback scheduling and routing in private/VPC, KMS/encryption constraints for onprem.

title: Weekly pilot sync - retry budget, rate-windowing & SLA handoff
preview: Redwood and Nimbus reviewed pilot reliability telemetry from the last 72 hours: spikes of 502/504s correlated with token-burst traffic from a scheduled batch job. Team agreed next steps: tune client-side retry budget, add a short token-window rate limiter on the Redwood side, run a controlled load test against the canary model variant, and share runbook + cost impact. Security/procurement will get the pricing snapshot for scaling to Dedicated if needed.
