title: Lyra Biotech segmentation SOC2 playbook review
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2026-08-30
- Start: 15:00 UTC
- Duration: 58 minutes
- Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marcus Li (Redwood SE), Dr. Priya Nair (Lyra Head of IT), Ethan Morales (Lyra Security Engineer), Sofia Alvarez (Lyra Compliance)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
Lyra Biotech is evaluating Redwood Private for SOC2 and ISO-aligned private inference. Primary concerns: network segmentation, strict egress controls, KMS/HSM integration, SSO/RBAC mapping, and audit log retention. Action items: diagrams, playbook draft, sample logs, and lab setup.

Topics: network isolation, ingress/egress controls, firewall rule patterns, bastion/jump hosts, RBAC/SSO, KMS integration, audit logs and retention, SOC2 evidence collection.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ava Chen: All right thanks everyone for joining. I'm Ava from Redwood, AE on the account, and with me is Marcus our solutions engineer. How's everyone doing?
[00:06] Priya Nair: Hi, thanks. Busy week but good. Thanks for setting this up.
[00:09] Marcus Li: Hey all. Quick note, I'll be driving the arch part, we have a couple of diagrams to walk through.
[00:14] Ethan Morales: Great. We're primarily focused on scoping the network controls for a private deploy — we need to map to our SOC2 controls and our internal zero trust policy.
[00:21] Ava Chen: Perfect. Goal for today is to review a proposed segmentation playbook and validate our KMS/no egress model. We'll capture open items and schedule the lab.

[00:30] Marcus Li: Ok pulling up the first slide — apologies if some product names come through weird in the transcript, but when I say \"Redwood Private\" I mean the VPC-hosted control plane option we talked about. So architecture high level: control plane endpoints in your management VPC, inference nodes in a dedicated private subnet, no public IPs on the worker nodes.
[00:48] Sofia Alvarez: So control plane sits in our account or in Redwood's? We need exact tenancy for SOC2 scoping.
[00:54] Marcus Li: For Private we typically offer a customer-controlled control plane that we can run as a managed appliance in your VPC; so tenancy is yours. For the hosted control plane option we can do bring-your-own-control plane with RBAC that integrates to your SAML IdP.
[01:07] Priya Nair: (mumble) okay, that helps. For audit evidence we need to show separation of duties — who can push model versions and who can change network rules. Does Redwood map to IAM roles?
[01:18] Ava Chen: Yes. We map operators into RBAC roles and can sync from Okta/AzureAD via SAML. Marcus, do you want to add how role-sync works?
[01:27] Marcus Li: Sure. We support SAML assertions to create role mappings — groups translate into Redwood roles like admin, deployer, viewer. The tenant admin can lock down the key rotation admin role separately. One caveat: early releases had a manual step for some service-to-service roles; we've automated most of that but we'll flag the remaining small steps in the playbook.

[01:46] Ethan Morales: The HSM question is important. Lyra's policy says customer-managed keys need to be on an HSM with annual attestation. Can Redwood integrate with Cloud HSM or a third-party HSM?
[01:56] Marcus Li: We integrate with AWS KMS and can point to a customer-managed CMK backed by an HSM. For on-prem HSMs we support PKCS#11 integration in the private package, but there are a few network requirements for the attestation endpoints. We'll detail that.
[02:12] Priya Nair: Does that mean keys never leave our account?
[02:15] Marcus Li: Correct for the CMK case: keys remain in your KMS, we use the KMS API for crypto operations. There is a small enclave on the host that performs tokenization and never exfiltrates the raw key material.
[02:26] Sofia Alvarez: How do we prove key rotation to auditors? We need an auditable rotation log.
[02:31] Ava Chen: We can surface key rotation events in the audit log — they show KMS rotation events and Redwood role actions. We'll include a sample audit export schema.

[02:40] Marcus Li: Moving on to network segmentation: recommended pattern is three tiers — management VPC, inference VPC (customer owned), and an egress gateway VPC. Workers in the inference VPC live in private subnets with route table entries that point to a controlled egress NAT or proxy. No direct internet routes on worker subnets.
[02:59] Ethan Morales: We already have a global egress proxy that does TLS inspection and DLP. Can we force all outbound to that proxy?
[03:05] Marcus Li: Yes, you point the inference subnet's default route to your egress gateway. Redwood supports DNS whitelisting and we can lock outbound to specific endpoints for our control plane. Note that model downloads during deploy may need allowlists for our artifact storage.
[03:18] Priya Nair: Model artifacts — do you store any of our data or models off-cluster? We need to know for data residency.
[03:24] Ava Chen: For Private deployments, artifacts can live in your object store; we can optionally cache in a customer-owned bucket. We don't persist user prompts in hosted logs unless configured. For compliance we'll show the retention slides.

[03:38] Marcus Li: On ingress, we recommend a single bastion/jump host pattern for operator access — the bastion is logged and uses SSM or SSH jump with MFA. No direct SSH to worker nodes. For API traffic, your application should call the VPC-internal inference endpoint over a private network or VPC peering — we also support PrivateLink style endpoints for minimal blast radius.
[04:00] Ethan Morales: For peering vs PrivateLink — peering has route table complexity; PrivateLink avoids transitive routing. Which do you see customers prefer?
[04:07] Marcus Li: Large regulated customers prefer PrivateLink for stricter isolation; peering is simpler for internal infra teams. We'll show both in the playbook with templates.

[04:18] Sofia Alvarez: Audit logs, retention and export — our SOC2 scope requires 12 months on certain events and 7 years for specific compliance records. What are Redwood's capabilities?
[04:27] Marcus Li: We emit structured audit events for user actions, model deployments, KMS usage, and system events. For Private you can ship logs to your SIEM (e.g., Splunk) via secure streaming. Retention is controlled by you. We provide log signing and attestations for evidence.
[04:40] Priya Nair: We use Splunk with specific ingestion schemas; can you provide sample syslog/JSON formats?
[04:45] Marcus Li: Yes, included in the sample schema we will send — includes field mappings, example events, and a mapping to typical SOC2 control IDs.

[04:53] Ava Chen: Question from my side — if you require air gapped model imports, do you have an offline process to seed models?
[04:59] Priya Nair: Yes, exactly. We might need to import models via signed artifact bundles.
[05:04] Marcus Li: We support signed model bundles and an offline import workflow. That will be in the on-prem playbook. There is a small manual verification step on the first import for checksum verification — will document that.

[05:18] Ethan Morales: What about egress blocklists — can we configure deny-by-default and only allow DNS+IP to our proxy?
[05:24] Marcus Li: Absolutely. Deny-by-default is our recommendation. We also have a small set of control-plane endpoints that need egress to function — those can be routed via the proxy and allowlisted. We'll provide a minimal allowlist with FQDNs and IP ranges.

[05:36] Sofia Alvarez: For identity — mapping Okta groups to Redwood roles is good, but how do you handle service-to-service credentials? We need short-lived tokens and auditable service keys.
[05:47] Marcus Li: Service tokens are minted with short TTLs and are scoped by role and model. We recommend issuing through your secrets manager; Redwood supports using STS-like federation so credentials are ephemeral. Those token operations are logged.

[05:58] Priya Nair: Any suggestions for pen-testing or scanning? We need to include vendor assessment steps for SOC2.
[06:03] Ava Chen: We can provide our pen-test report and third-party assessment summaries; for Lyra-specific pen testing we suggest a staged approach — control plane test, then worker-level test in a staging VPC with agreed blast radius. Marcus will include a template for scope and rules of engagement.

[06:18] Marcus Li: A couple of nuance items: 1) KV cache and model runtime telemetry — we can mask prompt content in traces if required; 2) rollback and canary policies — we recommend network-level circuit breakers so traffic can fail over to a read-only fallback when nodes scale down.
[06:36] Ethan Morales: Masking prompts is critical. We can't have PHI or PII show up in logs for production.
[06:41] Marcus Li: Understood — masking and redaction hooks are configurable. We will show sample filter configs.

[06:50] Sofia Alvarez: On ISO controls, we need evidence of least privilege and periodic access review. How easy is it to get a list of active role assignments?
[06:58] Marcus Li: There's an API and Console export for role assignments and last-used timestamps. We can schedule automated reports weekly or on-demand for auditors.

[07:08] Priya Nair: Ok can we go through an example timeline for pilot to production?
[07:13] Ava Chen: Sure — typical: Week 1: design review and network diagrams; Week 2: private deploy in a staging VPC + connectivity test; Week 3: ingest model artifacts and run benchmark; Week 4-5: compliance evidence collection and pen test; Week 6: production cutover with audit checklist. We're flexible on exact durations.

[07:32] Ethan Morales: What about rollback paths if a model misbehaves and we need to revoke keys or block egress quickly?
[07:39] Marcus Li: You can revoke service tokens centrally and we support immediate policy updates pushed to agents. For egress, changing the route to a blackhole or updating network ACLs is an option — we'll document the runbook.

[07:50] Ava Chen: I'll be honest — a common customer ask is how to demonstrate to auditors that software updates can't change network rules without approval. We suggest a GitOps flow for infra-as-code with signed commits and a required approver. Marcus, we have a sample that ties Redwood role approvals into the GitOps PR flow, right?
[08:06] Marcus Li: Yes — we support webhook-based gates and an approver workflow; we also persist approval actions in the audit log. That gives you the chain of custody evidentiary trail.

[08:17] Sofia Alvarez: Any limitations we should note for on-prem air-gapped deployments? Latency or model size constraints?
[08:24] Marcus Li: On-prem can have hardware variation; large models may need GPU pools with sufficient memory. For deterministic latency under load, we recommend quantized model variants and reserving capacity. We'll include guidance for model profiling and performance SLOs.

[08:37] Priya Nair: Good. Can you share the sample audit events now or after the call?
[08:41] Ava Chen: We'll share after the call. Marcus will attach the sample schema and the playbook draft.

[08:47] Marcus Li: Quick note — a few terms might be garbled in the transcript: \"Redwood Console\" sometimes shows up as \"Redwood Consol\" in the auto notes. Ignore that.

[08:55] Ethan Morales: Slight question on data residency: Lyra processes EU patient data, we need data at rest in EU. Can object storage and logs be region-locked?
[09:02] Marcus Li: Yes; for Private you control the storage location. Logs can be shipped to your EU Splunk instance and storage buckets can be region-specific. We'll show configuration snippets.

[09:13] Priya Nair: For SOC2 we need a signed subprocessor list, are you able to produce that?
[09:19] Ava Chen: Yes, we'll send the current subprocessor list. For Private, the number of subprocessors is smaller since many services live in your account.

[09:27] Sofia Alvarez: Any custom telemetry we should be aware of? We need to ensure telemetry doesn't capture PII.
[09:33] Marcus Li: Telemetry is opt-in per event and we provide field-level redaction. We'll show the telemetry catalog.

[09:40] Ava Chen: Let's recap and call out owners. Priya, you'll provide VPC ranges and egress proxy details. Marcus will draft the segmentation playbook and firewall rule examples. I'll send SOC2/ISO scoping doc and subprocessors. We'll also schedule the lab for mid-September.

[09:56] Priya Nair: Sounds good. Can you include a short runbook for emergency key revocation in the materials? That's a hard requirement for our compliance team.
[10:03] Marcus Li: Yes, we'll include an emergency revocation runbook with CLI commands and example audit entries.

[10:11] Ethan Morales: Also include an example of a deny-by-default egress NACL and a minimal allowlist for control-plane endpoints.
[10:18] Marcus Li: Noted.

[10:21] Ava Chen: Any other questions?
[10:23] Sofia Alvarez: One last — do you have customer references in healthcare who've done Private and SOC2? We'd like to speak to them.
[10:29] Ava Chen: Yes, a couple — I'll share intro emails (pending customer permission).

[10:34] Priya Nair: Great thanks everyone.
[10:36] Ava Chen: Thanks all, we'll follow up with the items and calendar invites for the lab.

End of transcript.

References mentioned (will send links in follow-up):
- sample audit log schema (JSON examples)
- segmentation playbook draft (network diagrams + Terraform snippets)
- KMS/HSM integration checklist and PKCS#11 notes
- SOC2/ISO scoping checklist

Recorded notes about transcription artifacts: some names and product terms may be mis-transcribed (e.g., \"Redwood Console\" as \"Consol\"), timestamps approximate.

title: Conversational API sprint demo — streaming fallbacks & cost controls
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-08-15
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Ruiz (Redwood SE), Priya Nair (VantageCare), Samir Patel (VantageCare), Elena Gomez (VantageCare)

Summary:
Quick demo focused on hosted conversational API — streaming behavior, tuning parameters for latency vs cost, and recommended retry/backoff patterns. Customer is evaluating for a pilot (chat triage and summarization workflows) and is concerned about latency tail, cost controls, and compliance (SOC2, VPC options).

Topics covered:
- Streaming vs synchronous generation
- Tokenization and batching tradeoffs
- KV cache/prefix caching and how it affects cost
- Retry strategies, idempotency, and client-side backoff
- Console + Optimize recommendations for cost and quantization suggestions
- Security/enterprise questions (SSO, KMS, data retention)

Action items (recap at end included in metadata): see action_items field

Transcript:
[00:00:00] Ava (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for taking time, we'll run about 40ish minutes. Uh, quick intros then Marco will do a short live demo of the streaming behavior and then we'll talk about retries and cost controls.
[00:00:18] Priya (VantageCare): Sounds good, thanks. We're particularly stretched on p99 latency for our triage flow, and trying to understand how Redwood's hosted API will behave under bursty traffic.
[00:00:30] Marco (Redwood SE): Yep, that's the right focus. I'll demo two scenarios — small chat exchanges where we prioritize latency and a summarization batch where throughput matters. We'll show how batching and the KV cache policies change token cost.
[00:00:45] Samir (VantageCare): Quick Q up front, do you support streaming partial tokens for our UI so users see tokens as they arrive? That's important for perceived latency.
[00:00:52] Ava: Yes, streaming is first-class — you can stream deltas. We also provide retry-safe semantics if the stream drops; the SDK exposes reconnection helpers.
[00:01:05] Elena (VantageCare): And for enterprise, do you have VPC/private control plane options? We'll need to talk about that after.
[00:01:12] Ava: Absolutely, Redwood Private and Dedicated are options. For pilot we usually recommend hosted, then Dedicated if they need guaranteed capacity. We'll share the Private deploy checklist.

[00:01:35] Marco: Ok, sharing my screen — starting with a simple conversation. I'm calling the chat.gen endpoint in streaming mode with temperature 0.2, max_tokens 256, and stream true. Notice how the SDK returns chunks that you can render as partial messages.
[00:01:55] Marco: (typing) The first chunk arrives in about 120 ms average on my test rig, p95 is 220 ms. When I bump concurrency the p95 climbs, as you'd expect.
[00:02:10] Priya: Do you do auto-batching on the server side? We have lots of short messages so batching seems like it might actually increase latency.
[00:02:18] Marco: Yes, it's workload-aware. There's continuous batching but the runtime observes per-route latency SLOs and will avoid large batch windows if you set low-latency policy. You can pin routes to a low-latency profile in the Console.
[00:02:35] Samir: How does that interact with cost? If we force low latency does cost spike a lot?
[00:02:40] Ava: There's a tradeoff. Lower batching means fewer tokens amortized across GPU overhead, so unit cost per token goes up. Optimize will show the cost delta and recommend quantization/models to compensate.

[00:03:10] Marco: Now a quick demo of caching — I send a repeated prefix and show KV cache hits. When the prefix hits, subsequent generations are much cheaper and faster. Note: the auto-generated transcript sometimes calls KV cache "K V cash" so apologies for any typos in the logs, hehe.
[00:03:32] Priya: For our triage, a lot of questions share identical opening context. That cache sounds useful. Is the caching per-account or per-route?
[00:03:40] Marco: It's per-deployment with configurable scope — you can do per-customer or shared cache. Cache retention is configurable and we have eviction policies.

[00:04:05] Elena: Let's talk retries — in production we get transient 5xxs from other providers and retry storms can make things worse. What's your recommended pattern?
[00:04:15] Marco: We recommend idempotency keys for synchronous calls and resumable streams for long responses. Client-side backoff should be jittered exponential; server signals include Retry-After. For streaming, if you reconnect within a short window we can resume from last confirmed token if you used the resume token.
[00:04:36] Priya: Resume token? Where does that live? Is that something we store?
[00:04:40] Marco: The SDK manages it for you by default, but you can persist it. It's basically a cursor in the KV cache. Caveat: resuming across model variant fallbacks may not be exact so we recommend resume for short interruptions, and falling back to sync for critical operations.

[00:05:05] Samir: You mentioned model fallbacks earlier — can you give an example when that happens?
[00:05:10] Ava: If capacity in a GPU pool is constrained, smart routing will send to a compatible variant — e.g., a quantized variant or slightly smaller model. There's an automatic fidelity policy and you can set strict or permissive modes.
[00:05:25] Elena: Hmm, we worry about hallucination differences between variants. How do you surface regression risk?
[00:05:31] Marco: We tie rollouts to eval sets — Console shows quality deltas for your prompts. If a variant has worse rouge/precision on your eval prompts, auto-fallback won't pick it unless you allow it.

[00:05:55] Priya: On security, SOC2 and SSO are must-haves. Can you share docs? Also KMS for keys?
[00:06:02] Ava: Yes — we'll send the SOC2 and ISO artifacts; we integrate with KMS/HSM for key management and support SAML SSO and RBAC. I'll attach the whitepaper after the call.

[00:06:30] Marco: Back to ops: here's how you'd tune for cost using Optimize — you feed a representative workload, it recommends quantization (like int8 or int4 for some models), batching targets, and suggests which routes to pin low-latency vs low-cost. We'll show a sample recommendation panel.
[00:06:55] Samir: Is the Optimize module separate cost? Or part of the Console?
[00:07:00] Ava: Optimize is an add-on but we include a basic set of recommendations in Console by default. Full Optimize includes deeper simulation and model-level recommendations.

[00:07:25] Marco: Quick demo of failure mode: I intentionally drop packets to show how the SDK retries with backoff. You can see the stream reconnect and then we either resume or re-generate depending on resume token validity. The client sees events: onRetry, onResume, onFailure.
[00:07:48] Priya: If a stream partially completes and we re-generate, do we get duplicate content?
[00:07:52] Marco: If you re-generate without idempotency the service may create a different continuation. Best practice: persist the resume token and use resume flow; otherwise use an idempotency key for synchronous re-generation.

[00:08:15] Elena: For our legal team — data retention and logs, are payloads stored? We need deletion/retention controls.
[00:08:22] Ava: By default, hosted requests are logged for 30 days for monitoring, but Enterprise plans can opt out or configure retention. We also support audit logs via the Console with exports.

[00:08:45] Marco: Now a real example: I trigger a 30-second summarization job of a long thread. For that workflow, batching and larger max_tokens helps throughput. If you stream summarization we can send partial analysis updates so UI can progressively show the summary.
[00:09:06] Priya: Could you run a quick back-of-the-envelope on cost difference if we go from 8-token batching to 1-token low latency?
[00:09:12] Marco: Roughly, your token unit cost could be 2-3x higher in low-latency mode depending on sequence length because GPU kernels aren't amortized as well. We'll provide numbers after we see your sample distribution.

[00:09:35] Samir: Any gotchas around idempotency keys and our existing request tracing? We already have a correlation id in headers.
[00:09:42] Marco: You can reuse your correlation ids; idempotency keys are separate but implemented as a header so you can map them. The Console shows token-level billing aligned with request ids for debugging.

[00:10:05] Priya: Can we get an on-prem option for initial pilot? We can't move all data to hosted yet.
[00:10:12] Ava: For pilot we usually do hosted with strict data retention and private networking peering if needed, but we can discuss a Dedicated reservation or Private deployment if the customer requires on-prem. That route comes with longer lead time and a separate contract.

[00:10:40] Elena: Walk me through monitoring — how do we detect regressions or cost spikes?
[00:10:47] Marco: Console has per-route dashboards, and Optimize can send alerts for quality regressions tied to prompt sets. We also integrate with Datadog and have webhooks for anomalies.

[00:11:12] Marco: Ok, I'll run a quick live bench now — firing up 200 concurrent short chat requests. You might notice some variance in p95.
[00:11:22] (sound of typing, brief pause)
[00:11:45] Marco: Results posted in chat: p50 85ms, p95 280ms, errors 0.9% when concurrency peaks. If we pin route to low-latency the p95 drops to about 210ms but cost per token rises about 1.8x.
[00:12:05] Priya: Those numbers look promising. Can you show the same test with a quantized variant?
[00:12:10] Marco: Sure — switching to the quantized variant; now p50 95ms, p95 260ms, cost down ~30%. There's a small delta in quality we measured on a small eval set.

[00:12:35] Samir: On the SDK, what's the recommended client-side behavior on websocket disconnects?
[00:12:40] Marco: Reconnect with jittered backoff, attempt resume with the resume cursor, and throttle your retry attempts with a max attempts cap; metrics-wise, emit onRetry events so you can alert if retries exceed thresholds.

[00:13:05] Priya: We have compliance questions around encryption in transit and at rest. KMS covers the at-rest, right?
[00:13:12] Ava: Correct. TLS in transit and KMS for at rest; for Dedicated/Private we can integrate with your KMS provider. We'll include a KMS integration note in the security pack.

[00:13:30] Elena: About billing — can we get a breakdown per-route? Our finance team wants to map cost to product features.
[00:13:38] Ava: Yes, Console provides token-level breakdown by route and model. We also support billing sub-accounts and tags so you can allocate cost back to teams.

[00:13:55] Samir: Any competitors we should try to benchmark against?
[00:14:00] Ava: Some customers benchmark against OtherCloudAI and ModelServePro. We also suggest you run throughput/latency checks with your real workloads.

[00:14:20] Priya: Ok head nod — what's the recommended next step to get pilot started? We want a 2-week pilot with a dataset and a smoke test.
[00:14:28] Ava: We'll send a pilot SOW and a runbook. Marco will run an initial benchmark with your traffic profile. Let's schedule kickoff the week of Aug 24th.

[00:14:45] Marco: Quick note on failure modes: if you hit a model fallback, the Console will annotate the request with the variant used and link the eval delta so you can inspect. That way there are no surprise quality regressions.

[00:15:05] Elena: Great. Any remaining questions on the customer side?
[00:15:10] Samir: One last one — can we get a short sample of the SDK integration for our mobile client? Latency matters on mobile networks.
[00:15:18] Marco: Yes, we'll send a mobile-focused SDK snippet that demonstrates streaming partials and reconnection logic. Also include guidance on reducing upstream RTT like regional endpoints and use of keepalives.

[00:15:38] Ava: Ok, recap — we'll send pricing + pilot SOW, security docs (SOC2/ISO + KMS integration guide), mobile SDK snippet, and Marco will run the bench. Priya to share traffic profile and latency SLOs.
[00:15:52] Priya: I'll send the profile by Wednesday.
[00:15:55] Ava: Fantastic. Thanks everyone — we'll follow up in email and schedule the kickoff.
[00:16:05] (call wraps, small chatter)

Notes on transcription quality: medium — a few product names and technical words may be slightly garbled (eg: KV cache -> "K V cash", resume token sometimes transcribed as "resume tokenn").

References shared during call:
- Pilot SOW template (link noted in follow-up)
- Security whitepaper (SOC2/ISO)
- Benchmark results (will be uploaded to shared drive)
- Mobile SDK snippet (will be attached)

End of transcript.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-08-15
Start: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Marco Ruiz (Redwood SE), Priya Nair (VantageCare), Samir Patel (VantageCare), Elena Gomez (VantageCare)

Summary:
Quick demo focused on hosted conversational API — streaming behavior, tuning parameters for latency vs cost, and recommended retry/backoff patterns. Customer is evaluating for a pilot (chat triage and summarization workflows) and is concerned about latency tail, cost controls, and compliance (SOC2, VPC options).

Topics covered:
- Streaming vs synchronous generation
- Tokenization and batching tradeoffs
- KV cache/prefix caching and how it affects cost
- Retry strategies, idempotency, and client-side backoff
- Console + Optimize recommendations for cost and quantization suggestions
- Security/enterprise questions (SSO, KMS, data retention)

Action items (recap at end included in metadata): see action_items field

Transcript:
[00:00:00] Ava (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for taking time, we'll run about 40ish minutes. Uh, quick intros then Marco will do a short live demo of the streaming behavior and then we'll talk about retries and cost controls.
[00:00:18] Priya (VantageCare): Sounds good, thanks. We're particularly stretched on p99 latency for our triage flow, and trying to understand how Redwood's hosted API will behave under bursty traffic.
[00:00:30] Marco (Redwood SE): Yep, that's the right focus. I'll demo two scenarios — small chat exchanges where we prioritize latency and a summarization batch where throughput matters. We'll show how batching and the KV cache policies change token cost.
[00:00:45] Samir (VantageCare): Quick Q up front, do you support streaming partial tokens for our UI so users see tokens as they arrive? That's important for perceived latency.
[00:00:52] Ava: Yes, streaming is first-class — you can stream deltas. We also provide retry-safe semantics if the stream drops; the SDK exposes reconnection helpers.
[00:01:05] Elena (VantageCare): And for enterprise, do you have VPC/private control plane options? We'll need to talk about that after.
[00:01:12] Ava: Absolutely, Redwood Private and Dedicated are options. For pilot we usually recommend hosted, then Dedicated if they need guaranteed capacity. We'll share the Private deploy checklist.

[00:01:35] Marco: Ok, sharing my screen — starting with a simple conversation. I'm calling the chat.gen endpoint in streaming mode with temperature 0.2, max_tokens 256, and stream true. Notice how the SDK returns chunks that you can render as partial messages.
[00:01:55] Marco: (typing) The first chunk arrives in about 120 ms average on my test rig, p95 is 220 ms. When I bump concurrency the p95 climbs, as you'd expect.
[00:02:10] Priya: Do you do auto-batching on the server side? We have lots of short messages so batching seems like it might actually increase latency.
[00:02:18] Marco: Yes, it's workload-aware. There's continuous batching but the runtime observes per-route latency SLOs and will avoid large batch windows if you set low-latency policy. You can pin routes to a low-latency profile in the Console.
[00:02:35] Samir: How does that interact with cost? If we force low latency does cost spike a lot?
[00:02:40] Ava: There's a tradeoff. Lower batching means fewer tokens amortized across GPU overhead, so unit cost per token goes up. Optimize will show the cost delta and recommend quantization/models to compensate.

[00:03:10] Marco: Now a quick demo of caching — I send a repeated prefix and show KV cache hits. When the prefix hits, subsequent generations are much cheaper and faster. Note: the auto-generated transcript sometimes calls KV cache "K V cash" so apologies for any typos in the logs, hehe.
[00:03:32] Priya: For our triage, a lot of questions share identical opening context. That cache sounds useful. Is the caching per-account or per-route?
[00:03:40] Marco: It's per-deployment with configurable scope — you can do per-customer or shared cache. Cache retention is configurable and we have eviction policies.

[00:04:05] Elena: Let's talk retries — in production we get transient 5xxs from other providers and retry storms can make things worse. What's your recommended pattern?
[00:04:15] Marco: We recommend idempotency keys for synchronous calls and resumable streams for long responses. Client-side backoff should be jittered exponential; server signals include Retry-After. For streaming, if you reconnect within a short window we can resume from last confirmed token if you used the resume token.
[00:04:36] Priya: Resume token? Where does that live? Is that something we store?
[00:04:40] Marco: The SDK manages it for you by default, but you can persist it. It's basically a cursor in the KV cache. Caveat: resuming across model variant fallbacks may not be exact so we recommend resume for short interruptions, and falling back to sync for critical operations.

[00:05:05] Samir: You mentioned model fallbacks earlier — can you give an example when that happens?
[00:05:10] Ava: If capacity in a GPU pool is constrained, smart routing will send to a compatible variant — e.g., a quantized variant or slightly smaller model. There's an automatic fidelity policy and you can set strict or permissive modes.
[00:05:25] Elena: Hmm, we worry about hallucination differences between variants. How do you surface regression risk?
[00:05:31] Marco: We tie rollouts to eval sets — Console shows quality deltas for your prompts. If a variant has worse rouge/precision on your eval prompts, auto-fallback won't pick it unless you allow it.

[00:05:55] Priya: On security, SOC2 and SSO are must-haves. Can you share docs? Also KMS for keys?
[00:06:02] Ava: Yes — we'll send the SOC2 and ISO artifacts; we integrate with KMS/HSM for key management and support SAML SSO and RBAC. I'll attach the whitepaper after the call.

[00:06:30] Marco: Back to ops: here's how you'd tune for cost using Optimize — you feed a representative workload, it recommends quantization (like int8 or int4 for some models), batching targets, and suggests which routes to pin low-latency vs low-cost. We'll show a sample recommendation panel.
[00:06:55] Samir: Is the Optimize module separate cost? Or part of the Console?
[00:07:00] Ava: Optimize is an add-on but we include a basic set of recommendations in Console by default. Full Optimize includes deeper simulation and model-level recommendations.

[00:07:25] Marco: Quick demo of failure mode: I intentionally drop packets to show how the SDK retries with backoff. You can see the stream reconnect and then we either resume or re-generate depending on resume token validity. The client sees events: onRetry, onResume, onFailure.
[00:07:48] Priya: If a stream partially completes and we re-generate, do we get duplicate content?
[00:07:52] Marco: If you re-generate without idempotency the service may create a different continuation. Best practice: persist the resume token and use resume flow; otherwise use an idempotency key for synchronous re-generation.

[00:08:15] Elena: For our legal team — data retention and logs, are payloads stored? We need deletion/retention controls.
[00:08:22] Ava: By default, hosted requests are logged for 30 days for monitoring, but Enterprise plans can opt out or configure retention. We also support audit logs via the Console with exports.

[00:08:45] Marco: Now a real example: I trigger a 30-second summarization job of a long thread. For that workflow, batching and larger max_tokens helps throughput. If you stream summarization we can send partial analysis updates so UI can progressively show the summary.
[00:09:06] Priya: Could you run a quick back-of-the-envelope on cost difference if we go from 8-token batching to 1-token low latency?
[00:09:12] Marco: Roughly, your token unit cost could be 2-3x higher in low-latency mode depending on sequence length because GPU kernels aren't amortized as well. We'll provide numbers after we see your sample distribution.

[00:09:35] Samir: Any gotchas around idempotency keys and our existing request tracing? We already have a correlation id in headers.
[00:09:42] Marco: You can reuse your correlation ids; idempotency keys are separate but implemented as a header so you can map them. The Console shows token-level billing aligned with request ids for debugging.

[00:10:05] Priya: Can we get an on-prem option for initial pilot? We can't move all data to hosted yet.
[00:10:12] Ava: For pilot we usually do hosted with strict data retention and private networking peering if needed, but we can discuss a Dedicated reservation or Private deployment if the customer requires on-prem. That route comes with longer lead time and a separate contract.

[00:10:40] Elena: Walk me through monitoring — how do we detect regressions or cost spikes?
[00:10:47] Marco: Console has per-route dashboards, and Optimize can send alerts for quality regressions tied to prompt sets. We also integrate with Datadog and have webhooks for anomalies.

[00:11:12] Marco: Ok, I'll run a quick live bench now — firing up 200 concurrent short chat requests. You might notice some variance in p95.
[00:11:22] (sound of typing, brief pause)
[00:11:45] Marco: Results posted in chat: p50 85ms, p95 280ms, errors 0.9% when concurrency peaks. If we pin route to low-latency the p95 drops to about 210ms but cost per token rises about 1.8x.
[00:12:05] Priya: Those numbers look promising. Can you show the same test with a quantized variant?
[00:12:10] Marco: Sure — switching to the quantized variant; now p50 95ms, p95 260ms, cost down ~30%. There's a small delta in quality we measured on a small eval set.

[00:12:35] Samir: On the SDK, what's the recommended client-side behavior on websocket disconnects?
[00:12:40] Marco: Reconnect with jittered backoff, attempt resume with the resume cursor, and throttle your retry attempts with a max attempts cap; metrics-wise, emit onRetry events so you can alert if retries exceed thresholds.

[00:13:05] Priya: We have compliance questions around encryption in transit and at rest. KMS covers the at-rest, right?
[00:13:12] Ava: Correct. TLS in transit and KMS for at rest; for Dedicated/Private we can integrate with your KMS provider. We'll include a KMS integration note in the security pack.

[00:13:30] Elena: About billing — can we get a breakdown per-route? Our finance team wants to map cost to product features.
[00:13:38] Ava: Yes, Console provides token-level breakdown by route and model. We also support billing sub-accounts and tags so you can allocate cost back to teams.

[00:13:55] Samir: Any competitors we should try to benchmark against?
[00:14:00] Ava: Some customers benchmark against OtherCloudAI and ModelServePro. We also suggest you run throughput/latency checks with your real workloads.

[00:14:20] Priya: Ok head nod — what's the recommended next step to get pilot started? We want a 2-week pilot with a dataset and a smoke test.
[00:14:28] Ava: We'll send a pilot SOW and a runbook. Marco will run an initial benchmark with your traffic profile. Let's schedule kickoff the week of Aug 24th.

[00:14:45] Marco: Quick note on failure modes: if you hit a model fallback, the Console will annotate the request with the variant used and link the eval delta so you can inspect. That way there are no surprise quality regressions.

[00:15:05] Elena: Great. Any remaining questions on the customer side?
[00:15:10] Samir: One last one — can we get a short sample of the SDK integration for our mobile client? Latency matters on mobile networks.
[00:15:18] Marco: Yes, we'll send a mobile-focused SDK snippet that demonstrates streaming partials and reconnection logic. Also include guidance on reducing upstream RTT like regional endpoints and use of keepalives.

[00:15:38] Ava: Ok, recap — we'll send pricing + pilot SOW, security docs (SOC2/ISO + KMS integration guide), mobile SDK snippet, and Marco will run the bench. Priya to share traffic profile and latency SLOs.
[00:15:52] Priya: I'll send the profile by Wednesday.
[00:15:55] Ava: Fantastic. Thanks everyone — we'll follow up in email and schedule the kickoff.
[00:16:05] (call wraps, small chatter)

Notes on transcription quality: medium — a few product names and technical words may be slightly garbled (eg: KV cache -> "K V cash", resume token sometimes transcribed as "resume tokenn").

title: POC weekly — latency & throughput dissect vs incumbent
preview: Weekly POC check-in: compared Redwood inference results vs incumbent baseline; discussed latency regressions under small-batch steady state and throughput under long-context bursts; identified KV cache warmup and routing policy as main levers; agreed on next benchmarks and deliverables.

SE to share bench spreadsheet and config diff; AE to update pilot timeline and pricing assumptions.
